entries.text:
**Relative potency** (vs hydrocortisone): glucocorticoid **5×**, mineralocorticoid **0** (table 50-2 G&G14). **Intermediate duration of action** (biological t½ 12-36 h).

**Triamcinolone acetonide** is one of the most widely used forms:
• **Intra-articular/intra-lesional**: local depot for osteoarthritis/bursitis, duration 4-6 weeks.
• **ICS** (Azmacort): equipotent to beclomethasone, an alternative in persistent asthma.
• **Oral topical** (Kenalog in Orabase) for aphthous stomatitis.
• **Intravitreal** in macular edema.

**Typical local ADRs**: cutaneous/subcutaneous atrophy with superficial infiltration, depigmentation of the site, rarely septic arthritis from contamination. Systemic effects only at high doses or with absorption from large surface areas.
• **Oral PK** (G&G14): **bioavailability ~90%**, **plasma t½ 3-5 h** but biological effect prolonged to **~36 h** by intracellular receptor binding → accounts for the intermediate duration. Orally it can give temporary relief in **Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis** flare-ups.

**Definition** (G&G14): "the consequence of excessive generalized stimulation of 5-HT receptors, especially when SSRIs, SNRIs, TCAs, MAOIs, and triptans are combined".

**Clinical triad** (**Hunter** criteria, sensitivity 84%, specificity 97%):
1. **Altered mental status**: agitation, hypomania, confusion.
2. **Autonomic hyperactivity**: hyperthermia (even >40 °C), tachycardia, profuse sweating, mydriasis, shivering, diarrhea.
3. **Neuromuscular hyperactivity**: tremor, **spontaneous or inducible clonus** (especially in the lower limbs), hyperreflexia, rigidity.

**Most frequently implicated drugs**:
• **MAOI + SSRI/SNRI/TCA** (most dangerous combination — mandatory washout of 2-5 weeks after fluoxetine)
• Triptans + SSRI/SNRI (overestimated but real risk)
• Tramadol, meperidine, fentanyl, ondansetron, linezolid, methylene blue, MDMA

**Differential diagnosis**: vs **neuroleptic malignant syndrome** (NMS) — slower rigidity, no clonus, onset over days; vs **malignant hyperthermia** — exposure to halogenated anesthetics/succinylcholine.

**Treatment**: discontinuation of all serotonergic agents, supportive care (cooling, hydration, BZD for agitation), and in severe cases **cyproheptadine** 12 mg + 2 mg every 2 h (5-HT2A/H1 antagonist) or IV **chlorpromazine**. Intubation + paralysis in severe cases.

**Detailed mechanism** (G&G14): blockade of **voltage-gated Na⁺ channels** in the inactivated state (use-dependent) → ↓ glutamate release. Also modulation of some HVA-type Ca²⁺ channels.

**Pharmacokinetics**:
• **Oral bioavailability**: ~98%.
• **Metabolism**: hepatic glucuronidation (UGT1A4) — **not CYP** → few classic-type interactions, but metabolism modulated by UGT inducers.
• **t½ ~25-33 h** in monotherapy; **becomes ~15 h** with inducers (CBZ, phenytoin, phenobarbital, rifampin); **becomes ~60 h** with valproate (UGT inhibitor) → high **rash risk** → **halved titration**.
• **Children < 2 years**: greater clearance → proportionally higher doses.

**FDA/EMA indications**:
• Epilepsy: focal seizures (mono- or add-on), generalized tonic-clonic, **Lennox-Gastaut syndrome**.
• Bipolar I disorder: **prevention of depressive relapses** (less effective on acute manic episodes).

**ADRs**:
• **Benign skin rash 10%** (usually within the first 8 weeks) → precautionary discontinuation.
• **Severe rash (SJS/TEN, DRESS, HLH) 0.1-1%** → slow titration is preventive. Children > adults.
• Dizziness, diplopia, ataxia, headache (dose-dependent).
• Generally "clean": no marked sedation, no weight ↑, no significant cognitive effects, mood-neutral. Reproductive profile **better** than most AEDs (but not nil).

**Standard titration scheme** (adults, without valproate): 25 mg/day × 2 weeks → 50 mg/day × 2 weeks → +50 mg every 1-2 weeks up to 100-200 mg/day. **With valproate**: 25 mg every other day × 2 weeks → 25 mg/day × 2 weeks → very slow titration. Never bypass → SJS.

**G&G14 profile**:
• **Spectrum**: similar to erythromycin but **more active against Haemophilus, Moraxella, Legionella, Mycoplasma, Chlamydia, atypical mycobacteria (MAC)**.
• **PK**: oral bioavailability **~55%** (meals slow but do not reduce it), t½ **3-7 h** → BID. Good tissue penetration (lung, tonsils, soft tissues).
• **Metabolism**: **CYP3A4** → **14-OH-clarithromycin** (active metabolite) + other metabolites.

**Clinically relevant interactions** (clarithromycin = **strong inhibitor of CYP3A4 and P-gp**):
• ↑ **digoxin** (renal P-gp inhibition, table G&G14 ch. 6) → toxicity.
• ↑ statins (atorva-, simva-, lova-) → rhabdomyolysis.
• ↑ warfarin (bleeding).
• ↑ cyclosporine, tacrolimus, sirolimus.
• ↑ theophylline, carbamazepine, ergotamines (ergotism).
• ⚠️ With **quinidine, disopyramide, antipsychotics** → ↑ risk of **torsades de pointes** (additive effect on QT).
• To be avoided with other QT↑ agents (FQ, class IA/III antiarrhythmics).

**Key indications**:
• Community-acquired pneumonia (atypical or susceptible S. pneumoniae).
• Pharyngotonsillitis/otitis media (alternative in β-lactam-allergic patients).
• **Triple anti-H. pylori regimen**: clarithromycin 500 mg + amoxicillin 1 g + PPI BID, 14 days (in areas with clarithromycin resistance <15%; otherwise quadruple therapy with bismuth).
• Prophylaxis and treatment of **MAC** in HIV/AIDS patients.
• Eradication of early **B. burgdorferi** in Lyme borreliosis.

**ADRs**: GI (less marked than erythromycin), metallic aftertaste, rare ↑ transaminases, allergies. **Pregnancy**: category C → avoid if possible (alternative: azithromycin). Dose adjustment in **severe CKD**.

**Human IgG2 anti-RANKL mAb** (G&G14): RANKL is the signal that activates osteoclasts → blocking it shuts down bone resorption.
• **Dose**: 60 mg SC every 6 months (osteoporosis); 120 mg every 4 weeks in bone metastases.
• Unlike bisphosphonates it does NOT deposit in bone → **reversible** effect: with abrupt discontinuation → osteoclastic **rebound** with **multiple vertebral fractures** → always sequence with a bisphosphonate.
• Usable in **renal failure** (no renal elimination).
• ADRs: **hypocalcemia** (correct Ca²⁺/vit D first), **ONJ** (osteonecrosis of the jaw), atypical femoral fractures, skin infections.
• **Mechanism — it mimics osteoprotegerin (OPG)**, the physiological decoy that sequesters RANKL away from the RANK receptor → blockade of osteoclast formation and activation. Besides osteoporosis it is indicated to **increase bone mass in adults at high fracture risk on cancer therapy** (e.g. hormone-deprivation).

**Enolic (oxicam)** NSAIDs with a **long half-life** → once-daily dosing (piroxicam t½ ~50 h).
• **Meloxicam** = **COX-2 preferential** at low doses (less gastric injury); **piroxicam** = non-selective and among the most **gastrotoxic** NSAIDs.
• Hepatic metabolism via **CYP2C9** (interactions with warfarin; slow metabolizers).
• ADRs: typical of NSAIDs (GI, renal, cardiovascular); the prolonged persistence accentuates piroxicam's gastric risk.
• **Piroxicam** undergoes **enterohepatic recycling** (<5% excreted unchanged) and pyridyl-ring hydroxylation via the **CYP2C subfamily** → t½ ~50 h, steady state in 7-12 days; the **EMA (2007)** downgraded it: **no longer first-line** and not for acute pain. **Meloxicam** 7.5 mg/day is less gastrotoxic than piroxicam, but the **advantage is lost at 15 mg/day**.

**Potent synthetic mineralocorticoid** (9α-fluoro-hydrocortisone): mineralocorticoid activity ~125× hydrocortisone (glucocorticoid potency ~10).
• **Indications** (G&G14): replacement therapy for **Addison disease/adrenal insufficiency** (with a glucocorticoid), **congenital adrenal hyperplasia** with salt wasting, **orthostatic hypotension/dysautonomia**.
• **Dose**: 0.05-0.2 mg/day orally.
• Mechanism: ↑ Na⁺ reabsorption + ↑ K⁺/H⁺ excretion in the distal tubule.
• ADRs: hypertension, edema, **hypokalemia**, alkalosis → monitor blood pressure and electrolytes.
• **Relative potencies** (table 50-2): glucocorticoid **10×**, mineralocorticoid **125×** hydrocortisone; **long t½ (≥ 24 h)** → **divided doses are not necessary**. In salt-wasting congenital adrenal hyperplasia it is combined with hydrocortisone (0.05-2 mg/day).

**Glycopeptide** related to vancomycin (G&G14): same mechanism (binds the **D-ala-D-ala** of peptidoglycan), Gram+ spectrum (MRSA, enterococci, C. difficile orally).
• Advantages: **long half-life** → 1 dose/day (IM or IV), **no 'red man syndrome'**, **less nephrotoxicity** than vancomycin.
• Available in **Europe** (not in the USA).
• **VanA cross-resistance** with vancomycin; monitoring of levels is useful in prolonged treatments.
• **Precise PK** (G&G14): protein binding **90-95%**, **very long t½ up to ~100 h** (→ once-daily dosing), renal elimination by filtration. **Resistance**: the **VanA** phenotype gives inducible cross-resistance to teicoplanin **and** vancomycin; **VanB** is induced by vancomycin only → many strains **remain teicoplanin-susceptible**.

**Sulfonanilide COX-2 preferential** NSAID (selectivity similar to celecoxib; G&G14) → good gastric tolerability.
• ⚠️ Documented **idiosyncratic hepatotoxicity** → AIFA: **maximum duration 15 days**, 100 mg BID, contraindicated in liver disease; **not available in the USA** precisely because of the hepatic risk.
• Indications: acute pain, dysmenorrhea, short-term osteoarticular inflammation.
• Like other NSAIDs it undergoes **oxidative hepatic metabolism** (CYPs, mainly **CYP2C9/CYP1A2/CYP3A4**) before renal excretion; its **COX-2 selectivity, comparable to diclofenac/etodolac/meloxicam**, explains lower gastric injury but **does not remove the cardiovascular risk** of COX-2-preferential agents nor the idiosyncratic hepatic risk.

**Chimeric anti-TNF-α mAb** (human Fc + murine Fab): neutralizes **soluble and membrane-bound** TNF-α → RA, Crohn disease/ulcerative colitis, psoriasis, spondyloarthritis.
• **IV** every 6-8 weeks; the murine component makes it **immunogenic** → **anti-drug antibodies (ADAs)** and infusion reactions → combined with **methotrexate** to reduce immunogenicity.
• ⚠️ Before starting: **screening for latent TB** and hepatitis B (risk of reactivation); risk of serious infections and (rarely) lymphoma.
• **Quantitative PK**: chimeric IgG1 mAb, ~**25% murine / 75% human**; **IV 5 mg/kg** at weeks **0-2-6**, then maintenance every ~**8 weeks**; terminal half-life ~**8-10 days** (clearance ↑ if anti-infliximab antibodies develop).
• **Contraindicated in severe congestive heart failure**; it can induce **antinuclear antibodies (ANA) and, rarely, a lupus-like syndrome**. **Infusion reaction in ~1 in 6 patients** (fever, urticaria, **hypotension**, dyspnoea within 1-2 h). For Crohn's induction: clinical response by ~14 days, **remission in ~30%**.

**First oral direct renin inhibitor** for hypertension (G&G14): blocks upstream the conversion of angiotensinogen → Ang I.
• **Very low bioavailability (~2.5%)**.
• ADRs: **hyperkalemia**, diarrhea (dose-dependent), rare angioedema.
• ⚠️ **Contraindicated with ACE inhibitors/ARBs in diabetics** (**ALTITUDE** trial: ↑ renal/cardiovascular events) and in **pregnancy** (like all RAAS blockers). Use is marginal today.
• **P-glycoprotein substrate**: intestinal P-gp caps absorption (bioavailability ~2.5%) and drives interactions (cyclosporine/itraconazole raise aliskiren).
• **Minimal hepatic metabolism**: largely eliminated **unchanged in feces** (renal and CYP3A clearance minor) → few metabolic interactions.
• High-fat meals markedly cut absorption → dose consistently with respect to meals.

Inhibits bacterial **dihydrofolate reductase (DHFR)** → blocks regeneration of **tetrahydrofolate**; together with the sulfonamide it achieves a **sequential blockade** of the folate pathway (co-trimoxazole).
• **Selectivity**: affinity for bacterial DHFR ≫ human.
• Alone: uncomplicated cystitis. In combination: UTIs, **Pneumocystis jirovecii**, nocardiosis.
• ADRs: **hyperkalemia** (blocks the **ENaC** channels of the distal tubule, amiloride-like effect — especially at high doses, CKD, elderly), megaloblastic anemia (reversible with **folinic acid**), rash.
• **Quantified selectivity**: **~100,000×** more drug is needed to inhibit human vs bacterial DHFR; in cotrimoxazole the **SMX:TMP ratio is 20:1** and the combination can become **bactericidal** (each agent alone is bacteriostatic). ⚠️ **↑ serum creatinine without a fall in GFR** (it blocks tubular creatinine secretion); **avoid with high-dose methotrexate** (↑ MTX toxicity).

**First-generation cephalosporins** (cefazolin IV, cephalexin oral; G&G14): spectrum mostly **Gram+** (MSSA staphylococci, streptococci) + some Gram- (E. coli, Klebsiella, Proteus).
• **Key indication: surgical prophylaxis** — cefazolin IV within **60 min** of incision (covers cutaneous Gram+ organisms of the surgical site).
• They do **not** cross the blood-brain barrier → **not for meningitis**.
• Good tolerability; cross-allergy with penicillins is low (~1-2%; avoid in case of penicillin anaphylaxis).
• **Cefazolin**: ~85% plasma protein-bound, excreted by **glomerular filtration**; the **only parenteral first-generation cephalosporin** marketed in the U.S. and drug of choice for **methicillin-susceptible S. aureus endocarditis** (2 g IV every 8 h).
• **Cephalexin**: **not metabolized**, 70–100% excreted unchanged in urine → useful orally for pharyngitis and mild-to-moderate urinary infections.

**Zolpidem, zaleplon, zopiclone/eszopiclone** — 'Z' hypnotics, non-BZD but GABA-A agonists **selective for the α1 subunit** (G&G14) → **hypnotic** effect without appreciable anxiolytic/muscle-relaxant/anticonvulsant action.
• Rapid onset, short half-life (zaleplon very short → useful for nocturnal awakenings) → little 'hangover'.
• Signature ADR: **complex parasomnias** (sleepwalking, sleep-driving with amnesia).
• Tolerance/dependence lower than BZDs but not absent; antagonized by **flumazenil**.
• **Hepatic metabolism**: **zaleplon** is metabolized mainly by **aldehyde oxidase** (and minorly CYP3A4), t½ ~1 h, with **inactive** metabolites; **eszopiclone** via **CYP3A4 + CYP2E1**.
• Consequence: **CYP3A4 inducers/inhibitors** alter the hypnotic effect.

**Trazodone, nefazodone** (SARI): **5-HT2A** antagonism + weak SERT inhibition + **H1/α1** blockade (G&G14).
• **Low-dose trazodone = hypnotic** (H1/α1 sedation dominates), at high doses an antidepressant; no dependence → widely used for **insomnia**.
• ADRs: sedation, orthostatic hypotension, **priapism** (rare, urological emergency).
• **Nefazodone**: withdrawn in many countries for severe **hepatotoxicity**.
• **Trazodone** (t½ ~6 h) is converted to the active metabolite **m-chlorophenylpiperazine (mCPP)**, a tryptaminergic (serotonergic) agonist.
• **Nefazodone** has a very short t½ (2–4 h) and an active metabolite (hydroxynefazodone) → requires multiple daily doses.

**Venlafaxine, desvenlafaxine** — **SNRIs** (G&G14): reuptake of serotonin and, at higher doses, norepinephrine.
• **Dose-dependent effect**: low doses ≈ SSRI; with increasing doses the **noradrenergic** component is added (useful in neuropathic pain, like duloxetine).
• ADRs: nausea, insomnia, sexual dysfunction, **dose-dependent ↑ blood pressure** (monitor); marked **discontinuation syndrome** (short half-life) → taper slowly.
• **Venlafaxine** is O-demethylated by **CYP2D6 (polymorphic)** to the equipotent active metabolite **desvenlafaxine** (O-desmethylvenlafaxine), then cleared by hepatic and **renal** routes.
• Venlafaxine and desvenlafaxine are **weak CYP2D6 inhibitors** → low interaction risk vs the strong SSRIs.

**Carbenicillin, ticarcillin** — extended-spectrum anti-**Pseudomonas** penicillins (carboxyl group; G&G14).
• Active against many Gram- organisms including Pseudomonas, but susceptible to β-lactamases → often combined with an inhibitor (**ticarcillin + clavulanate**).
• Today largely **supplanted by the ureidopenicillins** (piperacillin/tazobactam), which are more potent.
• ADRs: high **Na⁺ load** (heart failure), platelet dysfunction/bleeding, hypokalemia, hypersensitivity.
• **Anti-Pseudomonas hierarchy**: ticarcillin > carbenicillin but < **piperacillin** → ureidopenicillins have replaced them.
• **Ticarcillin + clavulanate** extends cover to aerobic/anaerobic Gram-negatives and **Stenotrophomonas maltophilia** (intra-abdominal and urinary infections).
• **Carbenicillin indanyl sodium** = acid-stable oral ester excreted unchanged in urine → only **urinary tract infections** by Proteus (non-mirabilis) and P. aeruginosa. Carbenicillin and ticarcillin are **discontinued** in the U.S.

**Etifoxine** — non-BZD anxiolytic: acts on the mitochondrial **TSPO (18 kDa)** receptor → ↑ **neurosteroids** that positively modulate GABA-A (a site different from BZDs), plus a direct action on GABA-A.
• Advantages over BZDs: less sedation, **less dependence/withdrawal**, does not impair memory.
• ⚠️ Reports of **hepatotoxicity** and severe cutaneous reactions (DRESS / Stevens-Johnson) → contraindicated in liver disease (EMA/AIFA warnings).
_(Not present in Goodman & Gilman: a drug used predominantly in Europe.)_
• **Pleiotropic** profile: beyond GABA/neurosteroid modulation, it has **anti-neuroinflammatory** action plus immune-neuroendocrine effects.
• **Adjuvant in neuropathic pain** (marked suppression).
• **Anxiolytic activity not inferior to lorazepam**, without dependence/withdrawal or rebound anxiety on discontinuation.

Transcriptional action (GRE/NF-κB) explains the hours-long latency, so GCs are useless in acute emergencies; rapid non-genomic membrane effects are secondary. Annexin-1, induced by glucocorticoids, inhibits cytosolic PLA2 and lowers iNOS and COX-2, confirming the upstream block of the arachidonic-acid cascade. Transactivation underlies metabolic effects and ADRs (gluconeogenesis, osteoporosis) whereas transrepression underlies the anti-inflammatory effect; dissociated GCs try to separate the two. On stopping, the main risk is HPA-axis suppression with secondary adrenal insufficiency and a withdrawal syndrome; risk increases with dose, potency and duration over ~3 weeks, so taper gradually and do not fully stop until adrenal function recovers.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 15060718, 34253540.

Because it retains both glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid activity, hydrocortisone reproduces physiological cortisol and is the agent of choice in adrenal crisis (100 mg IV then 50-100 mg every 6-8 h). For chronic Addison replacement it is combined with fludrocortisone, since hydrocortisone's mineralocorticoid activity alone is not enough; physiological oral replacement is about 15-25 mg/day in divided doses to mimic the circadian rhythm. Cortisone is inactive until hepatic 11β-HSD1 converts it to cortisol, so it is ineffective when applied topically. Its short biological half-life causes less HPA-axis suppression than the fluorinated agents.

Prednisone/prednisolone are the workhorse of chronic corticosteroid therapy (vasculitis, SLE, IBD, transplantation); the anti-inflammatory equivalent dose is about 5 mg (≈20 mg hydrocortisone). Axis-sparing regimens, a single morning dose and/or alternate-day dosing, exploit the intermediate half-life to limit HPA-axis suppression. Use beyond 3 weeks at 5-7.5 mg/day or more suppresses the axis, so taper slowly on stopping. Per the 2022 ACR guideline, even 2.5 mg/day for more than 3 months warrants fracture-risk assessment plus calcium/vitamin D and, in medium-to-high-risk patients, bisphosphonates, denosumab or teriparatide. In pregnancy prednisone/prednisolone are the preferred glucocorticoids because placental 11β-HSD2 largely inactivates them.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 37845798, 34253540.

Pulse therapy uses high-dose IV methylprednisolone (e.g. 0.5-1 g/day for 3 days) for acute transplant rejection, multiple-sclerosis relapses, rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis and severe vasculitis. It binds albumin rather than transcortin, giving more linear kinetics than cortisol, with ~5× glucocorticoid potency and negligible mineralocorticoid activity. Importantly, the steroids validated for fetal lung maturation in preterm birth are betamethasone and dexamethasone, which cross the placenta, not methylprednisolone, which is inactivated by placental 11β-HSD2 — correcting the base answer. The IM acetate depot prolongs exposure but suppresses the axis, so it should be avoided for repeated dosing.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 33368142, 34253540.

Dexamethasone is the brain steroid: it reduces peritumoral vasogenic oedema by restoring the blood-brain barrier, lowering water and serum-protein content in glioblastoma in a dose-dependent fashion. It is also used in the dexamethasone suppression test for Cushing syndrome and as a chemotherapy antiemetic. In obstetrics an antenatal course (e.g. betamethasone 12 mg IM in two doses 24 h apart) before preterm birth reduces respiratory distress syndrome, neonatal death and intraventricular haemorrhage (Cochrane evidence). With essentially no mineralocorticoid activity but very strong and long-lasting HPA-axis suppression, these fluorinated agents are unsuitable for chronic or replacement therapy and carry a high risk of iatrogenic Cushing syndrome and hyperglycaemia.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 3598684, 33368142.

The shared strategy is high hepatic first-pass clearance (ciclesonide, budesonide, fluticasone) or local activation, giving a maximal topical and minimal systemic effect. The hallmark local adverse effects of inhalers are oral candidiasis and dysphonia, both device- and dose-dependent, so a spacer plus rinsing the mouth after each dose reduces them. At high doses or with prolonged use, systemic effects re-emerge: HPA-axis suppression and, in children, growth slowing because of the high surface-to-weight ratio. For cutaneous products, occlusive dressings and damaged skin (eczema, burns) increase absorption, risking skin atrophy, telangiectasia and striae; use the lowest effective potency for the shortest time and avoid the face and skin folds with potent agents.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 8415878, 11576072.

**The two COX enzymes, clinically**:
• **COX-1** = constitutive, housekeeping → its inhibition explains gastric injury, antiplatelet effect and reduced renal perfusion.
• **COX-2** = induced by inflammation → target of the anti-inflammatory effect.
**GI risk hierarchy** (IPD meta-analysis of 754 trials): all NSAIDs raise upper-GI complications, but the magnitude differs widely — ibuprofen ~4× and naproxen ~4× vs baseline, coxibs only ~1.8×.
**Class cardiovascular risk**: high-dose coxibs and diclofenac raise major vascular events by about one third; naproxen carries the lowest CV risk (likely a more sustained antiplatelet effect). **All NSAIDs roughly double the risk of heart failure.**
**Practical rule**: no NSAID is "safe" on both the GI and CV fronts → choose by the patient's risk profile and use the lowest effective dose for the shortest time.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 23726390.

**Why irreversibility matters**: the platelet is anucleate → it cannot resynthesize the acetylated COX-1, so a low daily dose abolishes thromboxane for the platelet's lifespan (~7-10 days). Endothelium, by contrast, **regenerates** COX-2 → vasoprotective prostacyclin is spared.
**ASA + ibuprofen interaction** (clinical data): ibuprofen taken before ASA competitively occupies the COX-1 channel and **prevents ASA from acetylating the catalytic serine (Ser592 of COX-1 / Ser516 of COX-2)** → the cumulative antiplatelet effect is lost; **celecoxib does not interfere**. Practice: give ASA ≥30 min before, or ≥8 h after, ibuprofen.
**Reye syndrome**: encephalopathy + microvesicular hepatic steatosis, linked to ASA during **viral infections** (influenza, varicella) in children → avoid ASA as an antipyretic in minors.
**Salicylism**: first respiratory alkalosis (direct stimulation of the respiratory centre), then high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis; tinnitus and hearing loss are early signs.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 16952493.

**Ibuprofen = the best-tolerated NSAID**: at OTC doses (≤1200 mg/day) the GI risk is low and the safety profile is comparable to paracetamol, without paracetamol's hepatotoxic risk.
**Within-class differences**:
• **Naproxen** = long half-life (~14 h) → once/twice daily; it is the propionic acid with the **lowest cardiovascular risk**, useful in high-CV-risk patients who need an NSAID.
• **Dexketoprofen/ketoprofen** = potent short-half-life analgesics.
**CV risk for ibuprofen too**: at high doses (~2400 mg/day) it can raise major coronary events; it also interferes with ASA's antiplatelet effect (see ASA card).
**Stereochemistry**: these are racemic mixtures, but only the **S** enantiomer inhibits COX; for ibuprofen a one-way R→S bioconversion occurs in vivo.
• **Naproxen — long but variable half-life**: Goodman gives it as **9-25 h** (the card's ~14 h falls within the range) → consistent with its place among once-/twice-daily NSAIDs.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 19949916.

⚠️ **Exam caveat**: diclofenac's "COX-2 preference" does NOT make it cardiovascularly safe. In individual-participant meta-analyses, **high-dose diclofenac raises major vascular events as much as a coxib** (~+1/3, mainly coronary events). For this reason many agencies have restricted its use in patients with established CV disease.
**Intermediate GI toxicity**: lower than naproxen/ibuprofen but still present (RR ~1.9 for upper-GI complications).
**Hepatotoxicity**: diclofenac is among the NSAIDs most associated with transaminase elevation → monitor liver function during prolonged treatment.
**Useful PK**: metabolized via **CYP2C9** (polymorphisms) and partly CYP3A4; short half-life despite synovial-fluid accumulation that prolongs the joint effect.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 23726390.

**Very potent but toxic NSAID** → reserved for selected indications rather than routine chronic therapy.
**Typical niche indications**:
• **Pharmacological closure of a patent ductus arteriosus** in the preterm neonate (inhibits the PGs that keep it open).
• **Acute gout attack** and acute arthritis, where a rapid, intense anti-inflammatory effect is needed.
• Ankylosing spondylitis, pericarditis.
**Toxicity limiting its use**:
• **CNS**: severe frontal headache, dizziness, confusion (more marked than other NSAIDs, due to high lipophilicity).
• Significant **renal and GI** effects; enterohepatic recirculation prolongs exposure.
• Rare bone-marrow suppression.
• **Potent** (≈**20× aspirin**) but **35-50% ADR rate**. Exam interactions: it **antagonises the natriuretic/antihypertensive effect of furosemide and thiazides** and blunts that of **β-blockers, ACE inhibitors and ARBs** (renal PG inhibition); **probenecid** raises its plasma levels.

**Opioid-like analgesia, NSAID mechanism**: indicated for moderate-to-severe acute pain (post-operative, colic), often as an alternative to or opioid-sparing agent → no respiratory depression or dependence.
**Binding duration limit**: use ≤5 days (combining parenteral and oral routes) because of high gastric toxicity and renal risk; not for chronic pain.
**Key contraindications**: active bleeding or bleeding risk (inhibits platelet aggregation), renal impairment, perioperative use in high-bleeding-risk surgery, term pregnancy.
**PK**: half-life ~5-6 h, mainly renal elimination → reduce dose in the elderly and in renal impairment.
**Also available ophthalmically** (post-cataract inflammation) and intranasally.

**Rationale of CV risk (prostacyclin/thromboxane hypothesis)**: coxibs suppress endothelial antiplatelet/vasodilatory **prostacyclin (PGI2)** but **not platelet thromboxane A2** (COX-1) → a prothrombotic imbalance.
**Historical evidence**:
• **Rofecoxib (Vioxx)**: the VIGOR trial showed more myocardial infarctions vs naproxen (RR ~2.4); a cumulative meta-analysis confirmed RR ~2.2 for MI and showed the excess was NOT explained by "naproxen cardioprotection" → **withdrawn in 2004**; valdecoxib also withdrawn for severe skin reactions (SJS/TEN).
• **Celecoxib** survives (lower selectivity); the PRECISION trial showed CV safety non-inferior to naproxen/ibuprofen at moderate doses.
**Real residual advantage**: less gastric toxicity and no antiplatelet effect → useful in patients at high GI but low CV risk. They remain nephrotoxic like other NSAIDs.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 11509060.

**Toxicology (core of the card)**: at therapeutic doses paracetamol is conjugated to glucuronide/sulfate; only ~5-10% goes through **CYP2E1** generating **NAPQI**, neutralized by **glutathione**. In overdose the conjugation pathways saturate, glutathione is depleted → NAPQI covalently binds hepatocyte proteins → **centrilobular necrosis**. Paracetamol is **the commonest cause of acute liver failure** in Western countries.
**Antidote N-acetylcysteine (NAC)**: replenishes glutathione (donates thiol groups); maximal efficacy within ~8 h of ingestion, but indicated later too. The decision is guided by the **Rumack-Matthew nomogram** (paracetamol level vs time). Current IV regimens use 2 bags.
**Factors increasing risk**: CYP2E1 induction by **chronic ethanol** and fasting/malnutrition (reduced glutathione stores).
**Indicative threshold**: acute toxic dose ~150 mg/kg (or >7.5-10 g in adults).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 37552484.

**Why "inverse agonists":** the **H1 receptor is constitutively active**, producing basal signalling (e.g. tonic **NF-κB** activation in allergic inflammation) even without histamine. Anti-H1 drugs not only block histamine but **stabilise the inactive receptor state**, lowering basal activity = **inverse agonism** → they work best taken **regularly/prophylactically**.
• **Functional antagonism by adrenaline** (anaphylaxis): acts on **different** receptors (α1, β1, β2), opposing histamine via an independent and **faster** mechanism → first-line lifesaver; antihistamines are only adjuncts.
• **H3/H4** (H3: CNS autoreceptor; H4: eosinophil/mast-cell chemotaxis) remain research targets without current drugs.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 15564772, 27885525.

**Side effects = map of blocked receptors** (poorly selective):
• **Central H1 block** → sedation, plus **antiemetic/anti-motion-sickness** effect and ↑ appetite.
• **Muscarinic block** → anticholinergic syndrome (dry mouth, urinary retention, constipation, blurred vision, tachycardia) → caution in **closed-angle glaucoma, prostatic hypertrophy, the elderly** (delirium/falls).
• **α1 block** → orthostatic hypotension.
**Clinical trick**: sedation and anticholinergic action are exploited as **indications** — diphenhydramine/doxylamine as OTC hypnotics and in **pregnancy** (doxylamine+pyridoxine for nausea, among the few deemed safe), promethazine as antiemetic/sedative. **Diphenhydramine = prototype**; chlorpheniramine (alkylamine) is the **least sedating**.

**The terfenadine/astemizole story explains the whole class:** both were **prodrugs** activated by **CYP3A4**. With CYP3A4 inhibition (**ketoconazole, erythromycin, grapefruit juice**) or overdose, the **parent compound** accumulated and blocked **hERG (IKr)** K⁺ channels → **QT prolongation → torsades**. This is direct channel block, **not** an anti-H1 class effect.
• **Fix**: give the **preformed active metabolite** (fexofenadine from terfenadine) → no CYP dependence, no QT risk.
• **Cetirizine** = **carboxylated metabolite of hydroxyzine**; carboxylation makes it **poorly lipophilic** → little BBB crossing → low sedation.
• **Loratadine/cetirizine at therapeutic doses do NOT prolong QT** — the original card's caution is historical, not a real risk for these two.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 10597863, 10376921, 9951950, 11772135.

**Key idea: a "refinement" of 2nd generation**, not a new mechanism.
• **Pure active enantiomers/metabolites**: **levocetirizine** = active **(R)** enantiomer of cetirizine; **desloratadine** = active metabolite of loratadine; **fexofenadine** = active metabolite of **terfenadine**. Using the already-active fraction → lower dose, less CYP variability, clean cardiac profile (**fexofenadine does not prolong QTc**).
• **Fexofenadine**: substrate of **P-glycoprotein/OATP** transporters → **grapefruit/apple/orange juice reduce its absorption** (↓ efficacy); Al/Mg antacids also lower absorption.
• **Ketotifen**: besides anti-H1 it is a **mast-cell stabiliser** (cromone-like) → prophylaxis (ophthalmic/mild childhood asthma), but it **is sedating** (crosses the BBB), unlike the others here.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 9506246, 9951950.

**Clinical hierarchy**: H2-blockers are now largely **superseded by PPIs** (more potent, block the pump downstream) but remain useful for **nocturnal** acid control and on-demand dyspepsia.
• ⚠️ **Tachyphylaxis**: antisecretory effect **wanes within days** of continuous use (tolerance) — a limitation vs PPIs.
• **Cimetidine — two classic exam pitfalls**: (1) **antiandrogen** — binds the androgen/DHT receptor → **gynecomastia, ↓ libido, impotence** (reversible); (2) **potent CYP450 inhibitor** (1A2, 2C9, 2D6) → ↑ warfarin, phenytoin, theophylline, benzodiazepines. Famotidine/nizatidine lack these → preferred.
• **Ranitidine withdrawn (2020)**: **NDMA** (N-nitrosodimethylamine, probable carcinogen) contamination from intrinsic molecular instability → global FDA/EMA recall. A **pharmaceutical-quality** issue, not a receptor effect.
• **Antisecretory duration — not identical across the class**: ~**4-5 h** for cimetidine vs ~**6-8 h** for ranitidine, famotidine and nizatidine (the card's ~6-8 h is the class value).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 428705, 23165798, 35696227, 31771343.

**Severity-based strategy** (G&G14): mild-moderate attacks → NSAIDs/paracetamol ± caffeine; moderate-severe or unresponsive → triptans.
• **Rationale for caffeine combination**: analgesic adjuvant (adenosine A1/A2A receptor block → modest cerebral vasoconstriction) and ↑ absorption of co-formulated drugs; daily use → itself a cause of rebound headache.
• **Naproxen** is the long-t½ NSAID (~14 h) preferred to cover the whole attack; often combined with a triptan to ↓ recurrence.
• **Anti-overuse rule**: limit to ≤2 days/week; exceeding this → **medication-overuse headache (MOH)**, to suspect in any chronic daily headache.
• **Antiemetic** (metoclopramide) is useful not only for nausea but to restore gastric emptying and analgesic absorption.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 33974014.

**'Dirty' drugs** (G&G14): non-selective partial agonism at 5-HT1, 5-HT2, α-adrenergic and dopaminergic receptors → efficacy but also widespread toxicity; now superseded by triptans except in selected cases (long/recurrent attacks, status migrainosus).
• **Dihydroergotamine (DHE)**: less arterial vasoconstriction and less emetogenic than ergotamine → preferred; parenteral/nasal-spray routes.
• **Ergotism** (chronic intoxication/overdose): intense vasospasm → limb ischemia up to gangrene, and rarely **retroperitoneal/pleuropulmonary/valvular fibrosis** (5-HT2B class effect of ergot derivatives).
• **Dangerous interactions**: **CYP3A4** inhibitors (macrolides, azoles, protease inhibitors) → ↑ levels → acute ergotism (contraindicated combination).
• **Key rule**: NEVER combine with triptans or use them **within 24 h** of each other → additive coronary vasoconstriction.

**3-level mechanism** (G&G14): 5-HT1B (constriction of dilated meningeal vessels), 5-HT1D (↓ release of vasoactive peptides/CGRP from trigeminal endings), 5-HT1F (central inhibition of the trigeminal nucleus).
• **Expected yield**: pain-free at 2 h in ~20-30% of patients; 'triptan-type' symptoms (chest tightness/paresthesias) in ~25%, **generally non-ischemic**.
• **CV contraindications** from coronary 5-HT1B agonism: ischemic heart disease, vasospasm (Prinzmetal), uncontrolled hypertension, stroke/TIA, peripheral arterial disease; also contraindicated in **hemiplegic and basilar migraine**.
• **NEVER within 24 h** of ergot derivatives or another triptan (additive vasoconstriction).
• **Interaction**: with MAOIs ↑ exposure (sumatriptan/rizatriptan are MAO-A substrates) → avoid; serotonin-syndrome risk with SSRIs/SNRIs is real but overstated.
• **Long t½**: **frovatriptan** (~26 h) is useful in menstrual migraine for mini-prophylaxis.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 33974014.

**Three classes, one shared target (the CGRP/trigeminal pathway), favorable CV profile** (G&G14).
• **Lasmiditan (ditan, 5-HT1F)**: effective without vasoconstriction → option in cardiac patients; in exchange it has **central effects** (dizziness, somnolence, paresthesias) and mandates a **driving restriction for 8 h** after the dose; potential mild dependence/abuse → controlled schedule in the USA.
• **Gepants (rimegepant, ubrogepant; -gepant = small-molecule CGRP-receptor antagonist)**: oral, for the acute attack; **rimegepant** is approved **also for prevention** (every other day). No vasoconstriction → usable where triptans are contraindicated; they do not cause overuse headache (indeed an option in MOH).
• **Anti-CGRP antibodies (prevention)**: erenumab (anti-receptor), fremanezumab/galcanezumab/eptinezumab (anti-peptide); SC/IV dosing monthly or quarterly, **no CV or hepatic toxicity signal**; indicated after failure of ≥2 standard preventives, useful with overuse or comorbidity (anxiety/depression). **Not first-line because of cost.**

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 33974014.

**Comorbidity-guided choice** (G&G14): the preventive drug is selected by exploiting a beneficial action on an associated condition.
• **Propranolol / metoprolol**: first choice with hypertension/anxiety; propranolol is **safe in pregnancy** (mind peripartum timing); avoid in asthma and with depression.
• **Topiramate**: effective and, unlike the others, **causes weight loss** (useful in the obese); signature ADRs = paresthesias, cognitive/verbal deficits, kidney stones, **teratogenic risk (cleft lip/palate)**.
• **Valproate**: effective but **strongly teratogenic** (neural tube defects, cognitive deficits) → contraindicated in women of childbearing potential without secure contraception.
• **Amitriptyline**: useful with coexisting insomnia/tension headache/depression.
• **Anti-CGRP antibodies / gepants**: modern options after failure of classics.
• **Expected**: ~50% reduction in frequency among responders; a ≥2-3 month trial is needed before judging efficacy.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 15266476.

**Receptor→drug logic** (G&G14): the antiemetic is chosen according to the receptor predominating in the vomiting mechanism.
• **CTZ in the area postrema = lacks a blood-brain barrier** → exposed to blood-borne toxins; rich in **D2** and **5-HT3** receptors (targets of metoclopramide/domperidone and setrons).
• **Motion sickness / vestibular**: antimuscarinics (scopolamine) and anti-H1 → the vestibulo-cerebellar pathway is cholinergic/histaminergic, NOT serotonergic → setrons are ineffective here.
• **Chemotherapy-induced vomiting**: strong 5-HT3 release (acute) and **substance P/NK1** (delayed) → regimen **setron + dexamethasone + NK1 antagonist (aprepitant)**; BZDs cover anticipatory (conditioned, cortical) vomiting.
• **Pregnancy vomiting (1st trimester)**: first line **doxylamine + pyridoxine (vit. B6)**, with a good fetal safety profile.
• **Setrons**: ADRs = headache, constipation, **QT prolongation** (especially high-dose IV ondansetron).

**Same target (D2), one key difference = the blood-brain barrier** (G&G14).
• **Metoclopramide crosses the BBB** → central D2 block → **extrapyramidal disorders**: acute dystonias (young people), drug-induced parkinsonism and **tardive dyskinesia** (elderly, prolonged use), often **irreversible** → in the USA use is limited to **≤12 weeks** (warning) and avoided in children (also risk of methemoglobinemia in neonates). It also raises prolactin (galactorrhea/amenorrhea).
• **Domperidone does NOT cross the BBB** → almost no extrapyramidal effects, but **prolongs the QT** → ↑ risk of **sudden cardiac death**, dose-dependent (higher risk with doses >30 mg/day and in the elderly): use the lowest dose for the shortest time, avoid with other QT-prolonging drugs or CYP3A4 inhibitors.
• **Prokinesis**: both ↑ gastric peristalsis; metoclopramide adds 5-HT4 agonism and 5-HT3 antagonism → more potent as a prokinetic and antiemetic in chemotherapy-induced vomiting.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 8512437.

**Key therapeutic distinction**: in **asthma** the obstruction is reversible and inflammation-driven (eosinophils, mast cells) → **inhaled corticosteroids (ICS)** are the cornerstone; in **COPD** fixed, neutrophilic, poorly steroid-responsive obstruction prevails → **bronchodilators** (LAMA/LABA) are the foundation.
• **Never use a LABA or SABA as maintenance monotherapy in asthma**: a LABA "masks" inflammation and must **always be combined with an ICS** (see β2-agonist card).
• **Stepwise approach (GINA)**: even mild asthma now uses ICS (as-needed or regular), not SABA alone.
• Consistent with the card: chronic SABA-only use, beyond tachyphylaxis, is linked to **worse control and higher risk** without anti-inflammatory cover.

**Core exam warning**: a **LABA as monotherapy in asthma increases asthma-related mortality**. In the **SMART** trial, salmeterol added to usual care raised asthma deaths (RR ~4.4) → hence the FDA box warning and the rule: **LABA always + ICS**, never alone. In **COPD**, by contrast, LABA monotherapy is acceptable.
• **Dose-dependent β2 selectivity**: at high doses selectivity is lost → β1 stimulation → **tachycardia/arrhythmias**.
• **Hypokalaemia and hyperglycaemia** are proven, dose-dependent systemic β2 effects (β2 drives K⁺ into muscle); caution with diuretics and in heart failure.
• **Tremor** (skeletal-muscle β2) is the most frequent ADR and a common cause of poor adherence.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 16424409, 2575521.

**Why monitoring is mandatory**: theophylline has a **narrow therapeutic window** and **near zero-order kinetics** at higher doses → small dose increases cause large rises in plasma levels.
• **Hepatic metabolism via CYP1A2** (and partly CYP3A4): **cigarette smoke induces CYP1A2** → clearance rises and higher doses are needed; **stopping smoking** (or acute illness) can cause **toxicity** at an unchanged dose.
• CYP1A2 inhibitors (**ciprofloxacin, fluvoxamine, cimetidine, erythro/clarithromycin**) → ↑ theophylline levels → risk of **seizures and arrhythmias**.
• **Fever/heart failure/liver disease** reduce clearance. Today it is a **second/third-line** drug precisely because of its narrow safety margin.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 10427467.

**Clinical role by phenotype**: antimuscarinics are **more important in COPD** than in asthma. In the 4-year **UPLIFT** trial, **tiotropium** reduced **exacerbations and hospitalisations** and improved quality of life, although it **did not slow FEV1 decline**.
• **LAMA = first-choice maintenance therapy in COPD**; in asthma they are add-ons (e.g. tiotropium) when ICS+LABA are insufficient, not first-line agents.
• **Slow dissociation from M3** (prolonged bronchodilation) and **fast dissociation from M2** explain once-daily tiotropium efficacy.
• **Caution**: closed-angle glaucoma and **urinary retention** (prostatic hypertrophy); dry mouth is common.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 18836213.

**Local ICS effects and prevention (exam)**: oropharyngeal candidiasis and **dysphonia** are the typical local ADRs and are **dose-dependent**; a meta-analysis shows the risk falls with **mouth rinsing after inhalation** and with a **spacer** (reduces oropharyngeal deposition and improves the lung fraction).
• The **spacer** is especially useful with pressurised inhalers (MDI) in children and the elderly who cannot coordinate actuation and inspiration.
• ICS **do not work in the acute attack** (transcriptional effect, hours–days): acute crises need **systemic glucocorticoids**.
• Consistent with the card: systemic ICS effects are minimal at usual doses but can appear with **prolonged high doses** (adrenal suppression, osteoporosis, cataract).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 17378253.

**Clinical placement**: cromones are purely **prophylactic**, **ineffective in the acute attack** (no bronchodilator effect), and are now **largely superseded by low-dose ICS**, which control asthmatic inflammation more effectively.
• Residual use: **prophylaxis of exercise-induced bronchospasm** or known allergen/irritant exposure, taken **before** the trigger.
• **Excellent safety** profile (poor absorption, no systemic effects) → a historical option in **paediatrics and pregnancy**, but their modest efficacy limits real-world use.
• They need **frequent dosing** (several times daily) and several weeks for full effect → poor adherence.

**Montelukast neuropsychiatric warning (exam)**: the **FDA imposed a boxed warning** for neuropsychiatric events (mood changes, sleep disturbance, **suicidal ideation**), restricting the **allergic rhinitis** indication to patients unresponsive to other therapies; the asthma indication is retained but carries the warning.
• **Hepatotoxicity** monitored with transaminases mainly concerns **zileuton** (5-LOX inhibitor); the -lukasts show less.
• **Lower efficacy than ICS** as maintenance monotherapy: leukotriene modifiers are **add-ons** or an alternative in mild/paediatric disease and in **exercise-induced** or **aspirin-exacerbated** respiratory disease.
• Practical advantage: **oral, once-daily** (montelukast) → useful when inhaler technique is poor.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 33744471.

**Phenotype-based rationale (exam)**: biologics are **add-ons in severe asthma uncontrolled** by high-dose ICS + LABA, chosen by phenotype.
• **Omalizumab (anti-IgE)**: binds **free IgE** and is **non-anaphylactogenic** because it does not recognise IgE already bound to FcεRI → reduces both early and late responses; indicated in **severe allergic asthma** (elevated IgE). Dose and interval depend on weight and IgE level.
• **Anti-IL5 / anti-IL5R** (mepolizumab, reslizumab; benralizumab): for the **eosinophilic phenotype**. In the **MENSA** trial subcutaneous mepolizumab cut exacerbations by ~50%; **benralizumab** (anti-receptor) **depletes eosinophils via ADCC** (**SIROCCO** trial).
• Benefits: fewer exacerbations and **oral-corticosteroid sparing**; limits: **high cost** and the need to select the phenotype (IgE vs eosinophils).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 16464445, 25199059, 27609408.

**Exam point: immunogenicity is the class signature.** Even a protein "identical" to the endogenous one can trigger neutralizing antibodies: the historical case is **epoetin alfa (Eprex)**, where removing albumin from the EU formulation plus the **subcutaneous** route favored aggregates → anti-EPO antibodies → **pure red cell aplasia** (PRCA), cross-reacting with all EPOs (PMID 14608000).
• **"The process makes the product"**: cell host, glycosylation and formulation (buffer, preservatives) affect efficacy, PK and immunogenic risk → standardization and pharmacovigilance are mandatory.
• **PEGylation**: besides ↑ half-life, it lowers immunogenicity by masking epitopes (e.g. pegfilgrastim, certolizumab pegol).
• All parenteral because peptides/proteins are degraded by gastric juices and too large for intestinal absorption.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 14608000.

**Suffix mnemonic (decreasing immunogenicity):** -**o**mab = murine → -**xi**mab = chimeric → -**zu**mab = humanized → -**u**mab = human. The more murine the sequence, the higher the risk of **anti-drug antibodies (ADA)** that neutralize and shorten half-life.
• **Fragments** lacking Fc: **Fab** (e.g. certolizumab, abciximab) and **scFv** → no Fc functions (no complement lysis/ADCC), short half-life → often PEGylated to extend it.
• The **Fc portion** sets subclass and effector function: IgG1 = strong complement activation and cell-mediated cytotoxicity (useful in oncology); IgG2/IgG4 = weak effectors (preferred when only target neutralization is wanted).
• Fc also binds **FcRn** → endosomal recycling that explains the long (weeks) half-life of whole antibodies.
_(Established naming stems: Goodman & Gilman, 14th ed.; no specific PMID citable for the suffix rule alone.)_

**Risk hierarchy (exam point).** Reactivation of **latent TB** is more frequent with **monoclonal antibodies** (infliximab, adalimumab) than with the soluble receptor **etanercept**, which carries low risk: mAbs bind membrane TNF and break down the granuloma (PMID 25617816). → **Mandatory screening before therapy** (IGRA/Mantoux + chest X-ray) and exclusion of hepatitis B.
• **Heart failure**: the **ATTACH** trial showed infliximab does not improve NYHA III/IV and at high dose (10 mg/kg) **increases death/hospitalization** (HR 2.84) → contraindicated in advanced cardiac disease (PMID 12796126).
• **Demyelination/MS** and **heart failure** remain class contraindications; comorbidity management well summarized in PMID 33861409.
• All raise the risk of serious infections (including TB, fungal, viral reactivation) and, rarely, lymphoma.
• **Two TNF-α receptors**: **p55 (TNFR1)** and **p75 (TNFR2)**; etanercept is the **p75-receptor** binding portion fused to an IgG1 Fc → this distinguishes the soluble receptor from the monoclonal antibodies.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 25617816, 12796126, 33861409.

**Why it is less immunogenic (but not immune).** Being **fully human** it generates fewer anti-drug antibodies than the chimeric infliximab, but **ADA** remain a frequent cause of secondary **loss of response** with falling serum levels.
• **Key strategy**: combination with an **immunomodulator** (methotrexate, azathioprine) reduces ADA formation and can **recover** lost response, restoring therapeutic levels and remission better than dose intensification alone (PMID 36840779).
• **Practical PK**: **sc** dosing every 2 weeks (self-injection → excellent compliance), half-life ~2 weeks.
• The cytochrome interaction is not direct: by lowering inflammation (hence the TNF-α that represses CYP1A2/2D6/3A4) it may **normalize** the metabolism of narrow-therapeutic-index drugs → reassess doses.
• **Quantitative PK**: loading dose **160 mg sc**, then **80 mg** at week 2 and maintenance **40 mg every 2 weeks** from week 4; sc bioavailability ~**64%**; half-life ~**2 weeks**.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 36840779.

**Clinical proof of the mechanism (exam point).** Etanercept is the **soluble p75-Fc receptor**: it binds circulating TNF but **not** membrane-bound TNF, so it does not lyse the expressing cell. Direct consequence: in **Crohn's disease** it proved **ineffective** (39% response vs 45% placebo, P=0.763), unlike the antibodies infliximab/adalimumab (PMID 11677200).
• Consistently, it carries a **lower TB reactivation risk** than the anti-TNF mAbs (PMID 25617816).
• It also binds **lymphotoxin (TNF-β)**, unlike the antibodies.
• Approved in **pediatrics** too (e.g. juvenile idiopathic arthritis, pediatric plaque psoriasis); **sc** dosing weekly or twice weekly, generally well tolerated (injection-site reactions common but mild).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 11677200, 25617816.

**Biosimilar ≠ generic (core exam point).** A generic (small molecule) is **identical** and only demonstrates **bioequivalence**; a biosimilar is a protein made by living cells → never identical, requiring an EMA **"comparability exercise"** on quality, non-clinical and clinical data, with mandatory **immunogenicity** assessment.
• Why immunogenicity matters: the **Eprex** (epoetin) case shows a formulation change can trigger neutralizing antibodies and **pure red cell aplasia** → post-marketing pharmacovigilance is built into approval (PMID 14608000).
• **Traceability**: for biologics the brand name and **batch/lot** are recorded, because immune-mediated reactions can be product-specific.
• **Switching/interchangeability**: allowed but the decision is clinical (EMA: specialist center, not automatic pharmacy substitution); meta-analyses show no relevant efficacy/safety concerns (PMID 22045529).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 14608000, 22045529.

**Exam point: pick the insulin from its action profile.** The basal-bolus regimen mimics physiology: a **rapid-acting analogue** at meals covers the glucose peak, a **long-acting analogue** provides the flat basal supply.
• **Rapid (lispro, aspart, glulisine)**: the mutation prevents hexamer formation → monomers absorbed at once → injected **right at the meal** (0-15 min), short duration (~3-5 h) → less late hypoglycemia than regular.
• **Regular (human)**: must be injected **30 min before** the meal because it has to dissociate from hexamers; it is the only one usable **IV** (ketoacidosis, intensive care).
• **Peakless long-acting (glargine, detemir, degludec)**: steady release → fewer nocturnal hypoglycemias vs NPH; **degludec** has the longest duration (>24 h at steady state) → the most stable profile.
• **Signature safety**: hypoglycemia is the dose-limiting ADR; lipohypertrophy from repeated injections at the same spot makes absorption erratic → **always rotate the site**.

**Exam point: secretagogues → hypoglycemia + weight gain.** They stimulate insulin secretion regardless of glycemia, so they can lower glucose even when not needed → **hypoglycemia**, sometimes severe and **prolonged** (especially elderly and CKD).
• In the **ADOPT** trial **glyburide (glibenclamide)** caused more hypoglycemia than metformin and rosiglitazone and lost efficacy sooner (5-year monotherapy failure 34% vs 21% metformin) → prefer **gliclazide/glimepiride**, lower risk (PMID 17145742).
• **Weight gain**: hyperinsulinemia is anabolic → +1-3 kg, a drawback in the obese.
• **Glyburide**: active renally-excreted metabolites + long duration → the most dangerous in the elderly/nephropathic.
• **Chlorpropamide** (1st gen): very long t½, **SIADH-type hyponatremia** and a **disulfiram-like** reaction with alcohol.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 17145742.

**Exam point: the "short" sulfonylurea for the postprandial spike.** Repaglinide and nateglinide close the same K-ATP channel as sulfonylureas but with **fast, reversible** binding and kinetics → brief insulin secretion synchronized to the meal.
• **"One meal, one dose; no meal, no dose"**: skipped if the meal is skipped → useful for irregular schedules and predominant postprandial spikes.
• Versus sulfonylureas: less interprandial/nocturnal hypoglycemia, but still present (they are secretagogues → **not** in T1DM).
• **Repaglinide**: hepatic metabolism via **CYP3A4 + CYP2C8** → ↑ hypoglycemia risk with gemfibrozil (2C8 inhibitor, combination to avoid); usable in CKD. **Nateglinide**: less potent, a D-phenylalanine derivative.
• Consistent with the class rule: **never** combine with a sulfonylurea (same target, no benefit).

**Exam point: first-line agent with no hypoglycemia and no weight gain, but it fears hypoxia.** It lowers glucose without stimulating insulin → **no hypoglycemia as monotherapy** and **weight-neutral/lowering**.
• ⚠️ **Lactic acidosis (MALA)**: by inhibiting mitochondrial complex I it raises lactate production and lowers its clearance; **rare (<10 cases/100,000 patient-years)** but with **~50% mortality**, triggered by drug accumulation (CKD) plus a second insult (sepsis, hypoperfusion, contrast media) (PMID 26773926, 38983827).
• **Renal threshold**: contraindicated if **eGFR <30**; reduce dose at 30-45; withhold before major surgery and iodinated contrast.
• **Vitamin B12 deficiency**: dose/duration-dependent ileal malabsorption (risk already **>4 months** of therapy) → macrocytic anemia/neuropathy → check B12 in chronic use (PMID 38034222, 28925645).
• Dose-dependent **GI** effects improve with meals and extended-release formulations.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 26773926, 38983827, 38034222, 28925645.

**Exam point: insulin sensitizers burdened by fluid retention, fractures and (pioglitazone) bladder cancer.** PPAR-γ activation reabsorbs sodium in the collecting duct → **edema and ↑ preload** → **heart failure** (absolute contraindication in NYHA III-IV) and **weight gain** (also from new adipocytes); in ADOPT rosiglitazone caused more weight and edema than the others (PMID 17145742).
• **Fractures**: TZDs shift mesenchymal cells toward the adipocyte at the expense of the osteoblast → ↑ fractures in men and women (HR ~1.4), even in young women (PMID 20590749).
• **Pioglitazone and bladder cancer**: slight but significant, **dose- and time-dependent** risk (OR ~1.13) → avoid it with unexplained hematuria or a history of bladder cancer (PMID 29476615).
• **Rosiglitazone** was withdrawn/restricted for the cardiovascular signal; today TZDs are **not first-line** and require ALT <2.5× ULN.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 20590749, 29476615, 17145742.

**Exam point: it acts only in the intestinal lumen → local effect, no hypoglycemia.** By inhibiting brush-border α-glucosidase it slows polysaccharide cleavage, flattening the **postprandial glucose peak** without stimulating insulin → **alone it causes no hypoglycemia**.
• **Exam pitfall**: if the patient is also on a sulfonylurea/insulin and becomes hypoglycemic, correct with **pure glucose (dextrose)**, NOT table sugar/sucrose, because acarbose blocks its cleavage and thus its absorption.
• **GI effects** (flatulence, bloating, diarrhea) arise from unabsorbed carbohydrates fermented in the colon → eased by starting low ("start low, go slow").
• Modest effect on HbA1c; contraindicated in chronic bowel disease (IBD, malabsorption, obstruction).

**Exam point: weight loss + cardiovascular protection, paid for with GI effects.** Insulin secretion is **glucose-dependent** → low hypoglycemia risk as monotherapy; slowed gastric emptying and central appetite action give the **weight loss** that defines the class.
• **Proven cardiovascular benefit**: in the **LEADER** trial liraglutide reduced cardiovascular and all-cause mortality in high-risk T2DM (PMID 27295427) → preferred in patients with atherosclerotic disease.
• **Gastrointestinal ADRs** (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea) are the most frequent and the leading cause of discontinuation → slow dose titration (PMID 27295427).
• ⚠️ **Acute pancreatitis**: flagged in regulatory warnings → stop if persistent abdominal pain (trials did not show a significant increase in incidence).
• Contraindicated with personal/family history of **medullary thyroid carcinoma / MEN2**; avoid lixisenatide in gastroparesis.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 27295427.

**Exam point: oral, weight-neutral, no hypoglycemia, but watch heart failure with saxagliptin.** By prolonging endogenous GLP-1 half-life they act in a **glucose-dependent** way → **low hypoglycemia risk** and **weight-neutral**; convenience of the **oral** route (advantage over injectable GLP-1 RAs).
• **Modest effect** on HbA1c (~0.5-0.8%); less effective than GLP-1 RAs, which instead lower weight.
• ⚠️ **Heart failure**: in the **SAVOR-TIMI 53** trial **saxagliptin** was CV-neutral for ischemic events but **increased hospitalizations for heart failure** (HR 1.27) → caution in cardiac patients; effect not clearly a class one (PMID 23992601).
• ⚠️ **Acute pancreatitis** and **arthralgias** in the class warnings (in SAVOR, pancreatitis rates matched placebo).
• **Linagliptin**: mainly **biliary** elimination → the only one needing no dose adjustment in CKD.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 23992601.

**Exam point: the cardio-renal benefit matters more than the glucose-lowering effect.** By blocking renal glucose reabsorption they induce glycosuria → weight loss and osmotic diuresis, with glycemic efficacy **dependent on GFR** (poor in advanced CKD).
• **Proven cardio-renal protection**: in the **EMPA-REG OUTCOME** trial empagliflozin reduced cardiovascular death, heart-failure hospitalizations and all-cause mortality (PMID 26378978) → indicated in heart failure and diabetic nephropathy, beyond glucose control.
• ⚠️ **Euglycemic ketoacidosis (EDKA)**: ketoacidosis with normal/mildly raised glucose → **insidious diagnosis**; withhold before surgery, fasting, acute illness (PMID 37797963).
• ⚠️ **Genitourinary infections**: glycosuria promotes **candidiasis/balanitis** and UTIs; rarely **Fournier's gangrene** (PMID 26378978).
• Other effects: hypotension/volume depletion (synergy with diuretics), a slight ↑ amputation risk with some molecules.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 26378978, 37797963.

**Exam point: what each hyperglycemic agent is for.** These drugs **raise** blood glucose, used in emergencies and specific conditions.
• **Glucagon**: antidote for **severe hypoglycemia** when venous access is unavailable (IM/SC/intranasal) → mobilizes hepatic glycogen; **ineffective if hepatic stores are depleted** (fasting, liver disease, alcoholism). Also useful in **beta-blocker** overdose (cAMP-mediated inotropic effect bypassing the β-receptor).
• **Octreotide**: somatostatin analogue → inhibits GH/glucagon/insulin; used in **acromegaly, neuroendocrine tumors (carcinoid, VIPoma)** and esophageal varices; in **sulfonylurea overdose** it suppresses insulin secretion, reducing rebound hypoglycemia.
• **Diazoxide**: opens K-ATP channels (SUR1 site) → blocks insulin release → first-line treatment of **congenital hyperinsulinism** and inoperable **insulinomas** (PMID 37454648); ADRs: salt/water retention, hirsutism.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 37454648.

**Exam point: the key interaction is chelation/altered absorption, not a systemic effect.** Al³⁺, Mg²⁺ and Ca²⁺ are **polyvalent cations** that form **insoluble chelates** with many oral drugs, lowering their bioavailability.
• **Quinolones**: an RCT showed Al³⁺ (sucralfate) cuts moxifloxacin bioavailability to **~40%** through chelation with polyvalent cations (PMID 11352442); the same holds for levofloxacin (PMID 9758306) → **stagger by 2-4 h**.
• A similar effect occurs with **tetracyclines, iron, bisphosphonates, levothyroxine, some antiretrovirals**.
• By **raising urinary pH** they slow excretion of basic drugs and speed it for weak acids (e.g. salicylates).
• Class ADRs (already noted): **Al → constipation**, **Mg → diarrhea** (the combination balances bowel habit); Al chelating phosphate can cause hypophosphatemia, Mg accumulates in renal failure. Antacids are safe in pregnancy.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 11352442, 9758306.

**Exam point: tachyphylaxis is the class signature.** **Tolerance** to H2 antagonists is a well-documented class effect: loss of antisecretory efficacy appears **within a few doses** and explains why PPIs, acting downstream, are more reliable long-term (PMID 11403541).
• Mechanism: H2 blockade raises **gastrin**, which up-regulates the other stimuli (ACh, gastrin on ECL cells) and drives **acid rebound** on withdrawal — also a class phenomenon (PMID 11403541).
• **Famotidine** is now the prototype: more potent, free of cimetidine's interactions (cimetidine inhibits CYP1A2/2C19/2D6/3A4 and is anti-androgenic → gynecomastia).
• **Ranitidine withdrawn** (2020) for **NDMA** contamination, a nitrosamine classed as a probable carcinogen (PMID 32741598).
• Advantage over PPIs: they **do not impair vitamin B12 absorption**; adjust the dose in renal failure (tubular secretion).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 11403541, 32741598.

**Exam point: a historical class, now marginal due to a poor efficacy/anticholinergic-effect ratio.** **Pirenzepine** and telenzepine are relatively selective for **M1** receptors of enteric ganglia and ECL/parietal cells, so they inhibit the **cephalic/cholinergic** phase of acid secretion with fewer systemic anticholinergic effects than non-selective atropine-like drugs.
• As **protonatable tertiary amines** they do not cross the blood-brain barrier → peripheral effects (dry mouth, blurred vision, tachycardia, urinary retention), not central ones.
• Antisecretory efficacy is **lower** than H2 blockers and PPIs → never first-line; today largely superseded.
• Classic anticholinergic contraindications: **closed-angle glaucoma, prostatic hypertrophy, GI atony/obstruction**.
_(Niche drugs, sparsely covered in recent sources: mechanistic data from Goodman & Gilman, 14th ed.; no specific pertinent PMID.)_

**Exam point: long-term effects are the core of the question.** Deep, prolonged acid blockade explains most of the chronic ADRs.
• **Hypomagnesemia**: meta-analysis → adjusted OR **1.71** with a dose-response relationship (PMID 31689852); it is typically **refractory to Mg supplementation until the PPI is stopped** and recurs on rechallenge (PMID 23808631) → check Mg in long courses.
• **Fractures, community-acquired pneumonia, kidney disease and C. difficile infection** are recognized associations of prolonged use (PMID 29132520); reduced absorption of **B12/iron/calcium**.
• **Acid rebound on withdrawal**: an RCT in healthy volunteers → after 8 weeks of esomeprazole **44%** develop acid symptoms on stopping vs 15% placebo (PMID 19362552; hypergastrinemic mechanism, PMID 11403541) → **taper**, do not stop abruptly.
• **Omeprazole + clopidogrel**: omeprazole (a CYP2C19 inhibitor) lowers surrogate markers of clopidogrel efficacy; the FDA discourages the combination, although the COGENT RCT did not confirm an excess of events → prefer **pantoprazole** or temporal separation (PMID 19904241).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 31689852, 23808631, 29132520, 19362552, 11403541, 19904241.

**Exam point: misoprostol is the only cytoprotectant with RCT proof of NSAID-ulcer prevention.** In Graham's trial (PGE₁ analogue, 200 µg) NSAID gastric ulcers fell to **1.4% vs 21.7%** with placebo — the first demonstration they are **preventable** (PMID 2904006).
• Mechanism (already noted): **EP3** agonist → ↓ HCl, ↑ mucus/bicarbonate. Dose-limiting ADR = **diarrhea/cramps**; **contraindicated in pregnancy** (ecbolic) → not for B12 deficiency.
• **Sucralfate**: a sucrose-octasulfate + Al(OH)₃ gel adhering to the ulcer crater. Its **Al³⁺ chelates quinolones** → RCT: moxifloxacin bioavailability cut to ~40% (PMID 11352442) → stagger; Al accumulates in renal failure.
• **Bismuth**: barrier plus antibacterial action on *H. pylori* (quadruple therapy). ADRs: **black stools and tongue** (bismuth sulfide), and in renal failure **bismuth encephalopathy**.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 2904006, 11352442.

**Exam point: metoclopramide's limit is tardive dyskinesia, domperidone's is QT.** **Metoclopramide**, a **D2** blocker that crosses the BBB, can cause **persistent tardive dyskinesia** like antipsychotics (PMID 8100643; PMID 12102590) → EMA/FDA limit dose and duration (≤ 5 days, avoid in the elderly).
• **Domperidone**: a D2 antagonist that does **not** cross the BBB (no EPS) but **prolongs QT** → arrhythmia/sudden death, especially at high doses/elderly → lowest dose, short course.
• **Erythromycin**: a **motilin** receptor agonist at sub-antibiotic doses → useful in acute diabetic gastroparesis, but with rapid tachyphylaxis plus CYP3A4 inhibition and ↑ QT.
• **Cisapride withdrawn** for torsades de pointes (hERG block); **neostigmine** (an anticholinesterase) is reserved for colonic pseudo-obstruction (Ogilvie's syndrome), not a routine prokinetic.
_(Established class effects: Goodman & Gilman, 14th ed. for domperidone-QT and erythromycin-motilin.)_

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 8100643, 12102590.

**Exam point: it is safe at therapeutic doses, but abuse is cardiotoxic.** At correct doses loperamide acts only on **peripheral µ** receptors (a **P-gp** substrate that keeps it out of the CNS → no euphoria/abuse).
• **Overdose/abuse** (for euphoria or to manage opioid withdrawal): block of the **hERG** channel (IK r) and **Nav1.5** → **QRS and QT** prolongation, torsades de pointes and death (PMID 27530870).
• FDA pharmacovigilance collected **48 serious cardiac events**, median abuse dose **250 mg/day**, with TdP and **10 deaths** (PMID 28073687).
• **Contraindicated in invasive infectious diarrhea** (fever/blood): slowing transit retains the pathogen and toxin → risk of megacolon (also in active IBD) and **age < 2 years**.
• Antidote for central/respiratory toxicity: **naloxone**.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 27530870, 28073687.

**Exam point: two pitfalls — stimulant abuse and the logic of PAMORAs in opioid-induced constipation.** **Stimulants** (senna, bisacodyl) help short-term, but **chronic abuse** (often for weight loss) → K⁺ loss in stool and secondary hyperaldosteronism → **hypokalemia**, colonic atony, dehydration.
• **Osmotics** (lactulose, PEG, Mg salts): retain water iso-osmotically; **lactulose** has the added indication in **hepatic encephalopathy** (it acidifies the colon and traps NH₄⁺).
• **Opioid-induced constipation (OIC)**: ordinary laxatives are often insufficient because they do not remove the cause (µ activation in the GI tract) → **PAMORAs** are needed, **peripheral** µ antagonists that do not cross the BBB.
• **Methylnaltrexone** (sc) reverses constipation **without** removing analgesia or triggering withdrawal (PMID 23881667); oral analogues: **naloxegol/naldemedine**; **lubiprostone** (a ClC-2 chloride-channel agonist) as a secretory alternative.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 23881667.

**Exam point: sulfasalazine toxicity comes from sulfapyridine and is pharmacogenetic.** Sulfasalazine is a prodrug split by **colonic azoreductases** into **5-ASA** (active) + **sulfapyridine** (the carrier, responsible for most adverse effects).
• Sulfapyridine is inactivated by **N-acetylation (NAT2)**: **slow acetylators** accumulate sulfapyridine and have **more dose-dependent ADRs** (36% vs 8% in fast acetylators; PMID 17377643) → nausea, headache, hemolytic anemia, reversible oligospermia; idiosyncratic: rash, Stevens-Johnson, agranulocytosis.
• Sulfasalazine impairs **folate** absorption → supplement folic acid.
• **Pure 5-ASA** (mesalazine, olsalazine, balsalazide) removes sulfapyridine → much better tolerated; rare but worth knowing: **interstitial nephritis** → monitor creatinine.
• Mechanism (already noted): **topical** mucosal action (↓ NF-κB, PPAR-γ), **not** COX → first-line in mild-to-moderate ulcerative colitis.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 17377643.

**Exam point: each class has its sentinel toxicity.** • **Thiopurines** (azathioprine/6-mercaptopurine): **myelosuppression** depends on **TPMT** (and NUDT15) polymorphisms; CPIC guidelines recommend **reducing the starting dose in deficient patients** to avoid bone-marrow aplasia (PMID 30447069) → genotype-guided dosing + serial blood counts.
• **Anti-TNF** (infliximab, adalimumab): before starting, **screen for latent TB and hepatitis B** (reactivation risk); immunogenicity → anti-drug antibodies and loss of response.
• **Natalizumab** (anti-integrin **α4**, not gut-selective): also blocks cerebral lymphocyte trafficking → **JC virus reactivation → PML**, of which it was the main driver of the iatrogenic surge (PMID 33219338) → risk-stratify with anti-JCV serology.
• **Vedolizumab** (anti-**α4β7**, **gut-selective**): blocks only intestinal homing (MAdCAM-1 ligand) → effective for induction and maintenance of ulcerative colitis (GEMINI 1, PMID 23964932) **without** CNS effects, unlike natalizumab (PMID 29767705).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 30447069, 33219338, 23964932, 29767705.

**Exam key: the RANK/RANKL/OPG axis governs remodeling.** Osteoblasts display **RANKL**, which binds RANK on osteoclast precursors → osteoclast differentiation and activation; **osteoprotegerin (OPG)** is the decoy receptor that sequesters RANKL and curbs resorption. It is the direct target of denosumab.
• **Estrogens and PTH act on this axis**: estrogens ↑ OPG and ↓ RANKL/IL-6 → less resorption; **intermittent PTH** is anabolic (↑ osteoblasts via Wnt), whereas chronically high PTH (hyperparathyroidism) is catabolic.
• **Calcitriol**: also needed to keep calcemia normal for mineralization; in severe deficiency → osteomalacia/rickets, with secondary hyperparathyroidism.
• **Peak bone mass** reached by ~25-30 years: the higher it is, the larger the reserve against senile/postmenopausal osteoporosis.

**Exam point: it is the foundation, not the cure.** Calcium + vitamin D are the prerequisite of every anti-osteoporosis therapy because all antiresorptive/anabolic drugs were tested on this background and they correct the negative calcium balance; any new drug must prove superiority over calcium + vit D alone.
• **Mandatory before potent antiresorptives** (bisphosphonates, especially denosumab): correct hypocalcemia and vitamin D deficiency before starting to avoid symptomatic hypocalcemia.
• **Typical textbook doses**: ~1000-1200 mg/day of elemental calcium (diet + supplement) and 600 IU/day of vitamin D (RDA for adults, including those over 70).
• **ADR**: calcium carbonate causes constipation/dyspepsia; excess → hypercalcemia, hypercalciuria, **kidney stones**; caution with unnecessary supplementation.

**Exam point: effective on bone but no longer first-line.** Hormone replacement therapy (HRT) prevents postmenopausal bone loss and reduces fractures, but the **WHI** study downsized its use for the risk/benefit balance (↑ VTE, stroke, and with estrogen-progestin ↑ breast cancer and coronary events).
• **Now indicated** mainly for menopausal vasomotor symptoms, at the lowest dose and for the shortest time; bone is an added benefit, not the primary indication.
• **Intact uterus → always add a progestin**: unopposed estrogens cause endometrial hyperplasia and ↑ risk of endometrial carcinoma.
• **Contraindications**: prior VTE/thromboembolism, estrogen-dependent breast or endometrial cancer, active liver disease, undiagnosed vaginal bleeding.

**Exam point: raloxifene is the "bone" SERM.** Estrogen agonist on bone and liver, antagonist on breast and endometrium: ↑ bone density and **reduces vertebral fractures**, lowers LDL/total cholesterol and **reduces the risk of ER+ breast cancer** (PMID 11006795), without stimulating the endometrium (unlike tamoxifen).
• **Signature ADR to remember**: ⚠️ **↑ venous thromboembolic risk** (like estrogens → contraindicated in immobilization/prior VTE), hot flashes, lower-limb cramps.
• **Limitation**: effective mainly on **vertebral** fractures, weak data on nonvertebral/hip → not ideal in severe osteoporosis with hip-fracture risk.
• **Tamoxifen** = breast antagonist (breast cancer therapy), but **partial agonist on endometrium** → ↑ endometrial carcinoma risk → not used for osteoporosis.
• **PK**: absolute bioavailability ~2% (extensive first-pass glucuronidation), t½ ~28 h, 60 mg/day orally (PMID 11006795).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 11006795.

**Exam point: today it is a niche drug.** Weak, short-acting antiresorptive; salmon/eel calcitonin is more potent than human but more immunogenic (sensitization with antibodies → loss of efficacy → use in intermittent cycles).
• **Most typical exam indication: bone pain**, particularly **acute vertebral fracture** and **Paget's** disease, exploiting the analgesic effect; in **hypercalcemia** it acts fast but transiently (useful as a bridge while a bisphosphonate takes effect).
• **Osteoporosis**: modest efficacy (mainly reduces vertebral fractures) → used only when estrogens/other drugs are contraindicated.
• ⚠️ **Safety signal** (long-term nasal spray): a possible small ↑ in **cancer** risk → EMA restricted chronic use in osteoporosis → prefer short courses.

**Three very high-yield exam points.** 1) **Dosing instructions** (due to poor bioavailability and esophagitis): in the morning fasting, with a full glass of plain water, **staying upright/seated ≥30 min** without lying down or eating → prevents **esophagitis/esophageal ulcer**. 2) **Osteonecrosis of the jaw (ONJ)**: rare but serious, more frequent with high-dose IV formulations in oncology → **dental exam and oral clearance before** starting and avoid extractions during therapy. 3) **Atypical femoral fractures** (subtrochanteric/diaphyseal): risk that **increases with treatment duration** (prodromal thigh pain → investigate).
• **Proven fracture efficacy**: alendronate (FIT trial) reduces hip (RR ~0.47), vertebral (RR ~0.52) and clinical fractures, already within 12 months (PMID 11095442).
• **Duration and drug holiday**: after ~5 years (oral) or 3 years (IV) reassess risk; in low-risk patients a **2-3 year drug holiday** exploiting the residual bone reservoir; continue to 10/6 years only if high risk. Atypical-fracture risk rises with duration, ONJ risk does not (ASBMR, PMID 26350171).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 11095442, 26350171.

**Exam point: it is the only (with its analogs) ANABOLIC, bone-building drug.** **Intermittent** dosing (1 SC injection/day) stimulates osteoblasts more than osteoclasts → new bone, whereas chronically high PTH does the opposite. It markedly reduces vertebral and nonvertebral fractures (Neer FPT trial: vertebral fracture RR ~0.35; PMID 11346808).
• **Limited duration**: course usually **≤24 months once in a lifetime**, due to the **osteosarcoma signal seen in rats** at high doses/long exposure.
• **Always sequence with an antiresorptive** (bisphosphonate/denosumab) on stopping: the bone gain is lost if not consolidated; teriparatide's antifracture protection nonetheless persists for months after stopping (PMID 15477438).
• **Reserved for severe/high-risk osteoporosis** or failure of other drugs (also because of high cost).
• ⚠️ **Contraindicated** in Paget's disease, prior skeletal radiotherapy, bone metastases/bone tumors, hypercalcemia → all conditions at osteosarcoma risk or risk of worsening.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 11346808, 15477438.

**The four exam traps** (beyond the mechanism/PK already covered):
• **Tolerance and dependence**: physical dependence is the rule with chronic use; abrupt withdrawal produces a true **withdrawal syndrome** (rebound anxiety/insomnia, tremor, autonomic hyperactivity) that in severe cases reaches **seizures and delirium** — historically described precisely for BDZs and barbiturates (PMID 39710834). **Always taper** slowly.
• **Frail elderly**: sedation, ataxia, cognitive/psychomotor decline → **falls, fractures (especially hip) and confusion/delirium**; prefer **short t½ agents without active metabolites** (lorazepam, oxazepam) and deprescribe when feasible (PMID 27223416; PMID 40342014).
• **Anterograde amnesia**: dose-dependent, exploited in premedication but the basis of criminal "date-rape drug" use (triazolam, flunitrazepam).
• **Respiratory depression**: modest alone, but **synergistically potentiated by alcohol and opioids** → apnea/death (FDA boxed warning for the opioid-BDZ combination).
**Short vs long t½**: short → less hangover but more abrupt/intense withdrawal and rebound; long → accumulation and prolonged sedation in the elderly/hepatic patient.
