**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 39710834, 27223416, 40342014.

**Competitive antagonist** at the BDZ site of GABA-A: it displaces BDZs but does **not** antagonize barbiturates, alcohol, opioids or other depressants (different site).
• **Resedation**: t½ (~1 h) is **shorter** than almost all BDZs → the effect fades in 1-2 h and the patient may **resedate** → repeated doses or a continuous infusion plus prolonged observation are often needed (PMID 8306565).
• ⚠️ **Seizure risk**: it precipitates **acute withdrawal** (and seizures) in chronically BDZ-dependent patients and lowers the seizure threshold in **tricyclic antidepressant co-ingestions** → must **not** be used when TCA intoxication is suspected (PMID 8239101; PMID 8306565).
• **Cautious use**: the antidote is NOT routine in every BDZ coma; reserve it for selected cases (e.g. iatrogenic sedation), titrating 0.1-0.2 mg.
• Other ADRs: nausea/vomiting, agitation, panic attacks, arrhythmias.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 8306565, 8239101.

**Why they are now second-line** (vs BDZs):
• **Narrow therapeutic index**: unlike BDZs they directly depress the CNS even **without GABA** (opening the Cl⁻ channel at high doses) → the dose-response curve continues to **coma, respiratory depression and death**, lacking the BDZ "ceiling" → **lethal in overdose** and the historical leading cause of drug suicides.
• **Potent enzyme induction**: phenobarbital is a **strong CYP450 inducer — notably CYP2B6, CYP3A4 and CYP2C9** (and is itself metabolized mainly by CYP2C9), as well as of **glucuronidation (UGT)** and P-glycoprotein → it speeds metabolism and **reduces the efficacy** of warfarin/oral anticoagulants, Ca-channel blockers, glucocorticoids, oral contraceptives, antimicrobials and antineoplastics (PMID 25515681). It also induces its **own** metabolism (pharmacokinetic tolerance).
• **Severe withdrawal**: physical dependence with a potentially lethal discontinuation syndrome (seizures, delirium), as with BDZs (PMID 39710834) → always taper.
• **Not analgesics** (may heighten pain perception). Residual indications: phenobarbital in epilepsy (and neonatal seizures), refractory status epilepticus, **barbiturate coma** for raised intracranial pressure, thiopental in anesthesia.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 25515681, 39710834.

**Buspirone in three exam points**:
• **5-HT1A partial agonist** (azapirone): full at somatodendritic raphe **autoreceptors**, partial at **postsynaptic** receptors; the anxiolytic effect arises from **autoreceptor desensitization** that restores physiological serotonergic tone (PMID 12559651).
• **Onset latency**: anxiolysis is **delayed by 1-2 weeks** → useless "as needed" and in acute crises (≠ the rapidity of BDZs); indicated in chronic **generalized anxiety disorder**.
• **Key advantage**: **no marked sedation, no respiratory depression, no alcohol potentiation, no dependence/withdrawal** and no abuse → a valuable alternative to BDZs; not useful to wean an already BDZ-dependent patient.
• **PK/limits**: rapid absorption but rapid elimination and extensive first-pass (PMID 12559651); a **CYP3A4** substrate (↓ efficacy with inducers, ↑ levels with inhibitors). ADRs: dizziness, headache, nausea. *(Acute anxiety → GABA/BDZ; chronic anxiety → serotonin.)*

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 12559651.

**Key concept**: neurosteroids (the prototype being **allopregnanolone**, derived from progesterone) are **positive allosteric modulators of GABA-A** binding a **transmembrane** site distinct from the BDZ and barbiturate sites, and they activate both **synaptic** and **extrasynaptic** receptors (tonic inhibition) (PMID 37566239).
• **Modern exam point — brexanolone**: IV allopregnanolone, **FDA-approved in 2019** for **postpartum depression**, whose abrupt neurosteroid drop ("neurosteroid withdrawal") is thought to be causative → **neurosteroid replacement therapy** with a **rapid** (hours) effect (PMID 37566239; PMID 30633900). Main ADRs: headache, dizziness, **somnolence/sedation** (monitored administration). The oral analog is **zuranolone**.
• They explain the action of drugs that increase their synthesis: **etifoxine** (via TSPO) and partly the SSRIs (↑ allopregnanolone).
• Reported profile lacks tolerance on repeated use, unlike BDZs and barbiturates (PMID 37566239).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 37566239, 30633900.

**Malignant hyperthermia**: the most feared pharmacogenetic complication of general anesthesia, triggered by **halogenated agents** and **succinylcholine** in carriers of ryanodine-receptor mutations (**RYR1**, autosomal dominant) → uncontrolled Ca²⁺ release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum.
• **Hypermetabolic** picture: rising end-tidal CO₂ (early sign), muscle rigidity, hyperthermia, tachycardia, acidosis, hyperkalemia, rhabdomyolysis.
• **Specific antidote: dantrolene** (blocks RYR1) → mortality fell from ~80% to <5%; must be available wherever general anesthesia is given.
• **Guedel stages**: stage II (excitement) must be crossed as fast as possible to avoid laryngospasm/arrhythmias; **emergence** depends on redistribution of the lipophilic drug away from the CNS, not on its elimination.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 26238698.

**MAC (minimum alveolar concentration)**: the concentration preventing movement to surgical incision in 50% of patients; an inverse measure of **potency** (lower = more potent). It is additive: nitrous oxide and a halogenated agent add their MAC fractions. It falls with age, hypothermia, opioids, and pregnancy.
• Low **blood/gas coefficient** = fast induction/recovery (sevoflurane, desflurane, nitrous oxide); high = slow (halothane).
• All **halogenated agents** plus succinylcholine can trigger **malignant hyperthermia** → antidote **dantrolene**.
• **Nitrous oxide**: non-competitive **NMDA**-receptor antagonist; prolonged use oxidizes the cobalt of **vitamin B12** → inactivates **methionine synthase** → megaloblastic anemia and myelopathy; it diffuses into closed air spaces (pneumothorax, bowel obstruction).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 1637609, 32240970.

**Propofol**: GABA-A agonist, the most widely used agent for induction/maintenance and ICU sedation; **no analgesia**; **pain on injection** (pre-treat with lidocaine); dose-dependent hypotension and apnea.
• **PRIS (propofol infusion syndrome)**: rare but often fatal, from **high, prolonged** infusions → mitochondrial respiratory-chain damage → metabolic/lactic acidosis, rhabdomyolysis, hyperkalemia, cardiac and renal failure; risk factors: young children, critical illness, catecholamines, steroids.
• **Thiopental** (barbiturate): very rapid onset/recovery by redistribution, ↓ cerebral metabolism (neuroprotection), no analgesia.
• **Ketamine**: **NMDA** antagonist → **dissociative anesthesia**; preserves respiration, **bronchodilator** (useful in asthma), maintains blood pressure; ⚠️ psychotomimetic effects (hallucinations, dysphoria); now a **rapid antidepressant** in treatment-resistant depression. **Etomidate**: cardiovascular stability but suppresses adrenal cortisol synthesis.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 36407194, 32240970.

**Esters vs amides** (count the letter "i" in the name): **esters** (procaine, tetracaine, benzocaine, cocaine) are hydrolyzed by **plasma cholinesterases** → short half-life and a **PABA** metabolite → more allergies; **amides** (lidocaine, mepivacaine, bupivacaine, ropivacaine, prilocaine, articaine) are metabolized by the **liver (CYP)** → longer duration, rare allergies.
• **Use-dependent block**: the drug reaches its intracellular site when the Na⁺ channel is open/inactivated → it blocks high-firing neurons (pain fibers) preferentially.
• The **un-ionized** form crosses the membrane, the **protonated** form binds the channel → in **inflamed/acidic** tissue the charged form predominates → **ineffective anesthesia**.
• Block sequence: unmyelinated **C** and A-δ fibers (pain/temperature) first, **A-α motor** fibers last.

**Bupivacaine**: long-acting, high-potency amide (high protein binding), first choice in labor and prolonged blocks; ⚠️ **the most cardiotoxic** — it binds cardiac Na⁺ channels and dissociates slowly → **refractory** arrhythmias and arrest; greatest risk with accidental intravascular injection.
• **Ropivacaine** (pure S-enantiomer): **lower cardiotoxicity** than bupivacaine and more differentiated sensory-vs-motor block → preferred for epidural/obstetric analgesia.
• **Lidocaine**: rapid-onset, intermediate-duration amide, the clinical reference (also a class Ib antiarrhythmic).
• **Prilocaine** and **benzocaine**: risk of **methemoglobinemia** (o-toluidine metabolite). **Articaine**: extra ester group → mixed metabolism and short duration, widely used in dentistry. **Mepivacaine**: avoid in obstetrics (neonatal accumulation).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 11825334.

**Local anesthetic systemic toxicity (LAST)**: usually from **accidental intravascular injection** or overdose.
• **CNS** (lower threshold, symptoms first): metallic taste, tinnitus, dizziness, agitation → seizures → finally **respiratory depression/coma**.
• **Cardiovascular** (higher doses): conduction block, bradycardia, hypotension up to **arrest**; **bupivacaine** is the most dangerous.
• **Antidote: 20% IV lipid emulsion** ("lipid rescue", lipid sink) → sequesters the lipophilic drug; bolus followed by infusion, after/alongside ACLS.
• **Added vasoconstrictor (epinephrine)**: ↓ systemic absorption → ↑ duration, ↓ toxicity, ↓ bleeding; ⚠️ **never in end-arteries** (fingers, nose, penis → ischemia) and caution with non-selective beta-blockers.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 17895327, 21050161.

**Exam point: the lag dismantles the "simple" monoamine hypothesis.** SSRIs/MAOIs raise synaptic monoamine within hours, yet the clinical effect takes **2-4 weeks** → what matters is not the acute ↑ in 5-HT but the **downstream adaptations** (5-HT1A autoreceptor desensitization, receptor remodeling and neuroplasticity/BDNF) that restore post-synaptic 5-HT1A signaling (PMID 37955823; PMID 10086478).
• **Before the adaptive steady state** there may be transient worsening of anxiety/agitation (reflecting the early up-regulation).
• **Rapid exception**: **esketamine/ketamine** (NMDA antagonist) → improvement within hours, indicated in treatment-resistant depression, intranasal route; typical ADRs: dissociation, ↑ blood pressure, dizziness (PMID 31745646).
• Monoamines remain the targets, but the signature to remember is the mismatch between the (fast) biochemical effect and the (slow) therapeutic effect.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 37955823, 10086478, 31745646.

**Exam point: TCAs are the deadliest in overdose due to sodium-channel-blockade cardiotoxicity.** Blockade of fast myocardial sodium channels slows phase 0 → **QRS widening**, ventricular arrhythmias, hypotension and cardiovascular collapse; the key antidote is **hypertonic sodium bicarbonate** (PMID 22244291; PMID 38597366).
• **Narrow therapeutic index**: one week of therapy can be lethal if taken as a single dose → caution in suicidal patients.
• **Anticholinergic (anti-M1) effects**: dry mouth, constipation, urinary retention, blurred vision, delirium in the elderly; **anti-H1** → sedation and weight gain; **anti-α1** → orthostatic hypotension.
• On QTc, TCAs prolong **more than SSRIs** (PMID 24922496) → contraindicated in conduction disorders (AV block, bundle branch block).
• They retain a role as **adjuvants in neuropathic pain** at doses below antidepressant ones.
• **Amoxapine — the only D2-antagonist TCA**: its dopaminergic antagonism exposes it to **extrapyramidal effects and tardive dyskinesia**, unlike the other TCAs.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 22244291, 38597366, 24922496.

**Exam point: the signature is tyramine-induced hypertensive crisis ("cheese reaction").** By inhibiting intestinal/hepatic MAO, dietary **tyramine** from fermented foods (aged cheeses, wine, cured meats, yeast extracts) is not degraded → reaches the circulation, displaces noradrenaline from nerve endings → potentially fatal **hypertensive crisis** (PMID 34414219; PMID 10086478).
• **Serious serotonergic interactions**: combination with SSRIs/SNRIs/TCAs, triptans, tramadol, meperidine → **serotonin syndrome** → requires a **washout** (≈2 weeks after the MAOI; **≈5 weeks** when switching from fluoxetine due to its long half-life).
• **Irreversible** agents (phenelzine, tranylcypromine): the effect lasts until the enzyme is resynthesized → prolonged precautions; **moclobemide** = reversible, MAO-A-selective (RIMA) → much lower tyramine risk.
• **Orthostatic hypotension** is also possible with chronic therapy.
• Selectivity: **MAO-A** (5-HT/NA → depression); **MAO-B** (dopamine → Parkinson, e.g. selegiline).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 34414219, 10086478.

**Exam point: first line because safe in overdose, but with well-defined class ADRs.**
• **Discontinuation syndrome** (abrupt stop, worst with **paroxetine**, short half-life): dizziness, flu-like symptoms, "electric-shock" paresthesias, anxiety → taper gradually; **fluoxetine** (long half-life) is almost exempt (PMID 37955823).
• **↑ GI bleeding risk**, especially when **combined with NSAIDs** (depletion of platelet serotonin): meta-analysis OR ≈ 2.1 vs NSAIDs alone → consider gastroprotection (PMID 36526813).
• **Hyponatremia/SIADH in the elderly** → monitor sodium in the first weeks (PMID 26042411).
• **Dose-dependent QT prolongation**, greatest with **citalopram** (dose limits in the elderly and in liver disease) (PMID 24922496).
• **Sexual dysfunction** common (≈30-60%: ↓ libido, anorgasmia, delayed ejaculation), a frequent cause of poor adherence (PMID 12243609).
• Early nausea/agitation/insomnia (5-HT2/5-HT3); unlike TCAs they do **not** cause anti-M1/anti-α1 effects.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 37955823, 36526813, 26042411, 24922496, 12243609.

**Exam point: selective NRI with a "noradrenergic" profile.** By inhibiting NET alone it increases noradrenergic transmission → useful for energy-type symptoms (apathy, fatigue, slowing), but overall antidepressant efficacy is modest and debated.
• **Blood pressure**: in class reviews reboxetine is **essentially neutral** on BP in long-term studies (unlike venlafaxine), although both hypotensive and hypertensive effects are reported → still caution in cardiac/hypertensive patients (PMID 34414219).
• Typical noradrenergic ADRs: insomnia, dry mouth, constipation, sweating, tachycardia, urinary retention.
• **Exam link**: **atomoxetine** is an approved selective NRI (non-stimulant) for **ADHD** → no abuse potential, unlike amphetamine psychostimulants (PMID 35697393).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 34414219, 35697393.

**Exam point: two signatures — it lowers the seizure threshold and does NOT cause sexual dysfunction.**
• **Dose-dependent seizure risk** → contraindicated in epilepsy, bulimia/anorexia (electrolyte imbalance) and in alcohol withdrawal; use extended-release formulations and respect the dose ceiling. Incidence at recommended doses is low (~1:1000 or less) but it is the distinctive limit (PMID 28570500; PMID 23728690).
• **No sexual dysfunction** (it may even improve it) and no weight gain → an alternative when these ADRs limit SSRIs (PMID 12243609).
• **Blood pressure**: it may ↑ BP (especially at high doses) and cause orthostatic hypotension in cardiac patients → monitor (PMID 34414219). Toxicology note: in overdose, gap-junction blockade makes it **poorly responsive to bicarbonate** (unlike TCAs) (PMID 38597366).
• Key indication: **smoking cessation** (↓ craving); **link**: **varenicline** (partial nicotinic α4β2 agonist) is now more effective (PMID 23728690).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 28570500, 23728690, 12243609, 34414219, 38597366.

**Exam point: NaSSA — an α2 antagonist that "disinhibits" NA and 5-HT.** By blocking **α2** auto-/heteroreceptors it increases NA and 5-HT release; **5-HT2A/5-HT2C** and **5-HT3** antagonism channels 5-HT toward 5-HT1 receptors (antidepressant effect) and explains its tolerability profile (PMID 29678261).
• **Anti-H1 clinical signature**: strong **sedation** and **increased appetite/weight** → given in the evening and exploited in depressed patients with **insomnia and weight loss**.
• **Few sexual effects** (5-HT2 blockade) and **antiemesis** (5-HT3 blockade) → useful where SSRIs fail for sexual dysfunction or nausea (PMID 12243609).
• Modest effect on blood pressure, but possible **orthostatic hypotension** and falls in the elderly (PMID 34414219).
• At **low doses** sedation is paradoxically greater (histamine blockade predominates before the noradrenergic effect).
• **Rare ADR (exam)**: **agranulocytosis**, to keep in mind alongside sedation and weight gain.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 29678261, 12243609, 34414219.

**Therapeutic framing** (beyond the basics already covered):
• Diagnosis requires **at least one manic episode (type I)** or hypomanic + major depressive (type II); mania defines the disorder, but depression dominates the course and disability burden.
• **Mood stabilizers = cornerstone**: lithium (also **anti-suicidal**), valproate and carbamazepine (stronger on mania), lamotrigine (prevention of the **depressive** pole); many **atypical antipsychotics** (quetiapine, olanzapine, aripiprazole, risperidone) are effective in acute mania and maintenance.
• **Antidepressants**: used cautiously and only **under mood-stabilizer cover**, given the risk of (hypo)manic switch and cycle acceleration (G&G14).
• **GSK3β**: a target shared by lithium and valproate and a unifying hypothesis for the mood-stabilizing action (G&G14).

**Monitoring and toxicity** (completion):
• **Target serum level**: ~0.6-1.2 mEq/L for maintenance; toxicity above ~1.5 mEq/L, severe/potentially lethal beyond ~2.5 mEq/L (coarse tremor, ataxia, confusion, seizures, coma) — G&G14.
• **Kidney**: **nephrogenic diabetes insipidus** from vasopressin-resistant aquaporin-2 down-regulation (polyuria/polydipsia), plus chronic nephropathy long-term; per PubMed (Eur J Pharmacol) [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ejphar.2015.02.040).
• **Interactions that ↑ serum lithium** (renal handling): **NSAIDs, thiazide diuretics, ACE inhibitors/ARBs** → toxicity risk; furosemide is lower-risk, caffeine/theophylline lower levels; per PubMed (Clin Pharmacokinet) [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1007/s40262-016-0370-y).
• **Pregnancy**: dose-dependent association with **cardiac malformations, including Ebstein's anomaly** (NEJM, PubMed) [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1612222).
• Eliminated **unchanged by the kidney**, no protein binding, **no metabolism**.
• **Anti-suicidal effect**: among mood stabilizers, **lithium is the only one** that consistently reduces **suicide risk** (an advantage not shared by valproate/carbamazepine/lamotrigine/antipsychotics).
• **Tremor**: a **fine postural hand tremor** is the **most common ADR within the therapeutic range** (~15-70%, dose-/level-dependent); managed by dose reduction, avoiding caffeine, or with **propranolol** (coarse tremor instead signals toxicity).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 26574046.

**Safety and PK** (completion):
• **Major teratogen**: neural tube defects (spina bifida), malformations and especially dose-dependent **reduced IQ / neurodevelopmental disorders** → contraindicated in pregnancy / in women of childbearing potential without effective contraception; per PubMed (AAN/AES/SMFM guideline) [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1212/WNL.0000000000209279).
• Idiosyncratic **hepatotoxicity** (rare but potentially fatal, highest in <2 yr / polytherapy) and **pancreatitis** → monitor transaminases/amylase (G&G14).
• **Hyperammonemia** (even without liver injury) → encephalopathy/tremor/confusion; dose-dependent **thrombocytopenia** and weight gain (G&G14).
• **PK**: high (saturable) protein binding, **enzyme inhibitor** → raises phenobarbital and **doubles lamotrigine half-life** (halved titration, rash risk); per PubMed (Clin Pharmacokinet, interactions) [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1007/s40262-016-0370-y).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 38748979.

**Toxicity and interactions** (completion):
• **Severe cutaneous reactions (SJS/TEN)**: strong association with the **HLA-B*15:02** allele (common in East/Southeast Asian populations) → pre-treatment screening recommended; per PubMed [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1097/MS9.0000000000002736). DRESS is also linked to **HLA-A*31:01**.
• **Hyponatremia/SIAD**: antidiuretic effect via renal AQP2 up-regulation (nephrogenic mechanism), especially in the elderly; per PubMed (J Clin Med) [DOI](https://doi.org/10.3390/jcm11195810).
• **Myelosuppression**: benign leukopenia is common, rare **agranulocytosis/aplastic anemia** → baseline CBC (G&G14).
• **CYP3A4 autoinduction**: half-life falls over the first weeks → dose must be **re-titrated upward**; potent inducer → lowers levels of oral contraceptives, warfarin, other AEDs, etc. (G&G14).
• Active metabolite **carbamazepine-10,11-epoxide** (contributes to both efficacy and toxicity).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 40109622.

**The 4 pathways and the therapeutic logic** (why an ideal antipsychotic does not exist):
• **Overactive mesolimbic** = useful target: blocking D2 here suppresses **positive symptoms**.
• **Underactive mesocortical**: D2 blockade here **worsens negative/cognitive symptoms** → rationale for atypicals (5-HT2A) that spare it.
• **Nigrostriatal** and **tuberoinfundibular** are "collateral victims" of D2 blockade: extrapyramidal effects and hyperprolactinemia.
**Beyond dopamine**: glutamate (**NMDA** hypofunction — ketamine/PCP also reproduce negative symptoms) and 5-HT contribute; the dopamine-only model is incomplete.
**D2 occupancy threshold** (PET): clinical efficacy above ~**65%**, while extrapyramidal effects emerge around **80%** — the therapeutic window of striatal blockade.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 16513859.

**Potency and profile** (rule of thumb):
• **Low-potency** (chlorpromazine) = more anti-H1/α1/M1 → sedation, orthostatic hypotension, anticholinergic effects.
• **High-potency** (haloperidol, fluphenazine) = more extrapyramidal effects, less sedation.
• **D2 threshold** (PET): occupancy **>65%** = clinical response, **~80%** = extrapyramidal risk (basis of the "60-75% efficacy / >75% EPS" rule).
• **Temporal spectrum of EPS**: acute dystonia (hours-days) → akathisia/parkinsonism (weeks) → **tardive dyskinesia** (months-years, often **irreversible**, from D2 supersensitivity).
• **Neuroleptic malignant syndrome**: rare but potentially lethal, more frequent with high potency/rapid titration; mortality has fallen with early recognition.
• **QT**: thioridazine and pimozide are among the highest-risk for torsades de pointes.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 16513859, 29325237.

**"Atypicality" in practice**: a high **5-HT2A/D2** ratio and "loose" D2 binding (rapid dissociation) → fewer extrapyramidal effects and less hyperprolactinemia than typicals.
• **Metabolic syndrome — risk hierarchy** (exam key): **clozapine ≈ olanzapine** (greatest weight gain, diabetes, dyslipidemia, partly direct and weight-independent) > **risperidone, quetiapine** (intermediate) > **ziprasidone, aripiprazole** (minimal) → monitor weight, glucose, lipids.
• **Risperidone**: at higher doses behaves like a typical → dose-dependent **extrapyramidal effects** and **hyperprolactinemia** (the most prolactin-raising atypical).
• **Clozapine** = the most effective, reserved for **treatment-resistant schizophrenia** (superior even to high-dose olanzapine).
• Beyond agranulocytosis, clozapine causes **myocarditis**, dose-dependent **seizures**, **hypersalivation** and ileus.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 15998156, 17651705.

**The key concept**: **partial D2** agonist (+ partial 5-HT1A, 5-HT2A antagonist) = "dopamine stabilizer".
• Where DA is in excess (mesolimbic) → **functional antagonist** (↓ positive symptoms); where it is deficient (mesocortical) it sustains a **minimal tone** → fewer negative symptoms.
• **Consequences of the mechanism**: it **does not raise prolactin** and tends to **lower it** (adding aripiprazole reduces risperidone-induced hyperprolactinemia), **low EPS**, **favorable metabolic** profile (little weight gain, negligible QT).
• **Typical ADR of partial agonism**: **akathisia/restlessness** and insomnia, sometimes early nausea.
• **Parkinson/psychosis**: an option when typicals are contraindicated, but caution → it may still worsen motor symptoms.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 16508892, 20814333.

**Key warning in the elderly with dementia** (high-yield): all antipsychotics (atypicals **and** typicals) increase **mortality** and **cerebrovascular events** in dementia-related psychosis/agitation → atypicals meta-analysis: **mortality OR ~1.5** vs placebo, the basis of the FDA **boxed warning**. Use only if essential, lowest dose, shortest time.
**Quick ADR map by molecule**:
• **Clozapine** → agranulocytosis (blood count), myocarditis, seizures, hypersalivation.
• **Olanzapine/clozapine** → metabolic syndrome.
• **Risperidone** → dose-dependent hyperprolactinemia and EPS.
• **Typicals** (thioridazine, pimozide), **ziprasidone** → QT prolongation.
**LAI**: ensure adherence but **not at the first episode** and never before confirming tolerability/efficacy with the oral form; the drawback is long persistence if an ADR occurs.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 16234500, 32815018.

**Rationale: dopaminergic deficit + DA/ACh imbalance**
• Degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in the **substantia nigra pars compacta** denervates the **putamen/caudate**, leaving striatal ACh relatively unopposed — hence **antimuscarinics** help tremor.
• **Staged strategy**: in younger patients/mild disease, **levodopa is often delayed** (to postpone dyskinesia) in favor of dopamine agonists or MAO-B inhibitors; in the elderly and advanced disease **levodopa** remains the most effective, best-tolerated drug. No agent halts neurodegeneration — therapy is **symptomatic**.
• ⚠️ **Exam trap**: Parkinsonian rest tremor and **essential tremor** (postural/action, treated with β-blockers) are distinct; levodopa improves the former and **worsens** the latter.
• **On-off motor fluctuations** partly reflect levodopa pharmacokinetics (variable gut absorption, competition with dietary amino acids for transport across the BBB), not just disease progression (Nutt, NEJM 1984, PMID 6694694).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 6694694.

**Why L-DOPA, not dopamine, and why always carbidopa/benserazide**
• Dopamine cannot cross the BBB; **L-DOPA** can (neutral amino-acid transporter) and is then decarboxylated to dopamine. **Carbidopa** and **benserazide** inhibit **peripheral DOPA decarboxylase** and **do not cross the BBB**, cutting peripheral conversion → **less nausea, vomiting and hypotension** and allowing the L-DOPA dose to drop by ~75%.
• **Protein meals**: large neutral amino acids compete with L-DOPA for both gut absorption and BBB transport → take on an empty stomach, shift protein intake to the evening (classic demonstration: Nutt, NEJM 1984, PMID 6694694).
• **Long-term complications**: after a few years, dose-related **motor fluctuations (wearing-off, on-off)** and **dyskinesias** appear, driven by **pulsatile** dopaminergic stimulation — hence interest in more continuous delivery (e.g. subcutaneous levodopa-carbidopa infusion, PMID 38499015).
• **Early nausea** is mediated by the chemoreceptor trigger zone (area postrema, outside the BBB) → prefer **domperidone** (does not cross the BBB); **never** metoclopramide/antipsychotics (block central D2 and worsen parkinsonism).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 6694694.

**Advantages, and the two ADRs you must remember**
• They act **directly** on postsynaptic receptors, **independently** of surviving nigral neurons → longer action than L-DOPA and **less tendency to dyskinesia** → often preferred as **initial monotherapy in younger patients** (to delay L-DOPA).
• ⚠️ **Impulse-control disorders (ICDs)**: pathological gambling, hypersexuality, compulsive shopping/eating — a **class effect**, particularly tied to **D3** stimulation (pramipexole/ropinirole); per PubMed, pramipexole use may itself confer high ICD risk (PMID 37955562). Ask about them actively.
• ⚠️ **Sudden-onset sleep attacks** and daytime somnolence: documented as a **class effect** (~6.6% in specialist centers, men more affected), even while driving and without warning (PMID 12077032) → warn about driving.
• Other ADRs: nausea, orthostatic hypotension, peripheral edema, hallucinations (more than L-DOPA in the elderly). **Ergot derivatives** (bromocriptine, pergolide, cabergoline) can cause **fibrosis** (valvular, retroperitoneal, pleuropulmonary) → now second-line.
• **Apomorphine** SC = rapid rescue for **off** episodes (strongly emetogenic → premedicate).
• **Receptor selectivity (Goodman corrects the lecture notes)**: **bromocriptine** is a **potent D2 agonist** (weak D1 antagonist), not D1-selective; **pergolide** is a partial D1 + strong D2/D3 agonist; **ropinirole** and **pramipexole** are D2-like with highest affinity for **D3**; **rotigotine** acts on D2 with some D1 activity. Impulse-control disorders are linked mainly to **D3**.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 37955562.

**MAO-B selectivity, "unproven" neuroprotection, interactions**
• At therapeutic doses they **selectively inhibit MAO-B** (the predominant brain isoform, which degrades dopamine), sparing intestinal **MAO-A** → far lower **tyramine (cheese-reaction) hypertensive-crisis risk** than non-selective MAOIs. Selectivity is **lost at high doses** → caution with tyramine-rich foods and serotonergics.
• **Neuroprotection**: the **ADAGIO** delayed-start trial showed benefit for **rasagiline 1 mg/day but not 2 mg/day**, a result inconsistent with a clear **disease-modifying** effect; clinically rasagiline **delays the need for L-DOPA** but disease modification is **unproven** (PMID 21482191).
• Use: symptomatic monotherapy in early disease or **add-on** to L-DOPA to reduce wearing-off. **Selegiline** is metabolized to **amphetamine/methamphetamine** (insomnia) — **rasagiline** is not.
• ⚠️ **Interactions**: with SSRIs/SNRIs/triptans/tramadol/meperidine → **serotonin syndrome** (see file B3); avoid the combination and respect the washout.
• **Safinamide**: dual mechanism (MAO-B + Na⁺-channel block/glutamate-release modulation), add-on to L-DOPA for fluctuations.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 21482191.

**Only as add-on to L-DOPA, and tolcapone's liver problem**
• COMT methylates peripheral L-DOPA to inert **3-O-methyldopa**; inhibiting it **prolongs L-DOPA's plasma half-life** and steadies its brain delivery → useful for **wearing-off**. Alone they have **no** antiparkinsonian effect: always **combined with L-DOPA**.
• **Entacapone**: peripheral-only, short half-life (given with each L-DOPA dose — hence the fixed levodopa/carbidopa/entacapone pill, *Stalevo*). In RCTs it adds **~0.7–1.6 h of ON time**; **STRIDE-PD did not show** that early use delays dyskinesia, and it **needs no liver monitoring** (PMID 37493495).
• ⚠️ **Tolcapone = serious hepatotoxicity**: more potent (central + peripheral) but linked to **fatal fulminant hepatic failure** (3 deaths in ~40,000 patient-years → EU withdrawal; US black-box with mandatory transaminase monitoring) (PMID 12908845) → second-line, after entacapone failure.
• **Opicapone**: once-daily evening dosing, high affinity, favorable liver profile.
• Common ADRs: enhanced dopaminergic effects (**dyskinesia**, nausea, hypotension → lower the L-DOPA), **diarrhea**, **brownish-orange urine** discoloration (entacapone).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 37493495.

**Amantadine = anti-dyskinesia, antimuscarinics = tremor, accessory drugs**
• **Amantadine**: **NMDA** antagonist plus facilitation of dopamine release/reuptake block. It is the **only** drug with proven efficacy against **L-DOPA-induced dyskinesia** (meta-analysis of NMDA antagonists: benefit only for amantadine, PMID 22728853); modest symptomatic effect in early disease. ADRs: livedo reticularis, ankle edema, hallucinations/confusion (elderly); originally an anti-influenza-A antiviral.
• **Antimuscarinics** (benztropine, trihexyphenidyl): correct striatal cholinergic excess → mainly help **tremor and rigidity**, little effect on bradykinesia. ⚠️ Atropine-like ADRs and **cognitive decline/confusion in the elderly** → avoid beyond ~65–70 years; atropine/scopolamine are not used (peripheral or sedative).
• **Accessory drugs for non-motor complications**: **pimavanserin**, a **5-HT2A** inverse agonist without D2 blockade, approved for **Parkinson's disease psychosis** on the basis of a phase 3 RCT (PMID 24183563) — it does not worsen motor function, unlike classic antipsychotics; **droxidopa** (norepinephrine precursor) for **neurogenic orthostatic hypotension**; **rivastigmine** (cholinesterase inhibitor) for Parkinson's disease **dementia**.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 22728853.

**Exam key point:** all "classic" therapy is **symptomatic**, not disease-modifying; it slows decline without halting it.
• **Cholinergic rationale**: cholinergic neurons of the **nucleus basalis of Meynert** degenerate first → cortical ACh deficit → basis of the cholinergic hypothesis and AChE inhibitors.
• **Glutamatergic rationale**: tonic glutamate excess at **NMDA** receptors drives chronic Ca²⁺ influx and **excitotoxicity** → memantine's target.
• The recent advance are anti-amyloid **disease-modifying** agents (lecanemab): first drugs to reduce plaque and slow decline, but with modest efficacy and ARIA (see dedicated card).
• **NSAIDs/antioxidants/chelators**: theoretical rationale (neuroinflammation, oxidative stress) but **no proven benefit** in trials.

**Symptomatic only**: modest effect on cognition/function, no halt of neurodegeneration; stop if ineffective or poorly tolerated.
• **Dose-dependent cholinergic ADRs** (class signature): **GI** (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, anorexia, weight loss — limit titration), muscle cramps, hypersalivation, vivid dreams/insomnia.
• ⚠️ **Cardiovascular**: increased vagal tone → **bradycardia, AV block, syncope** → caution/ECG in sick sinus syndrome or conduction disease; avoid with other bradycardic agents.
• ⚠️ Relative: **bronchospasm** (asthma/COPD), peptic ulcer/GI bleeding, seizures, urinary retention/obstruction.
• **Galantamine**: also a **positive allosteric modulator of nicotinic receptors**; CYP2D6/3A4 metabolism. **Rivastigmine**: also inhibits **butyrylcholinesterase**, transdermal patch → fewer GI effects. **Donepezil**: once daily, long half-life.
• **Tacrine** abandoned for **hepatotoxicity**.
• **AChE binding (exam)**: **donepezil** and **rivastigmine** = **non-competitive** inhibitors; **galantamine** = **competitive** inhibitor (plus nicotinic allosteric modulator).

**Non-competitive, voltage-dependent, low-to-moderate affinity NMDA antagonist**: blocks tonic pathological Ca²⁺ influx yet rapidly unblocks during physiological depolarization → preserves normal synaptic transmission and memory.
• **Indication**: **moderate-to-severe** Alzheimer (G&G14), often **combined with an AChE inhibitor** (complementary mechanisms).
• According to PubMed, in moderate-to-severe patients already on donepezil, adding memantine gave significant benefit on cognition, ADL and behavior (Tariot 2004, JAMA, [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.291.3.317)).
• **PK favorable in the elderly**: bioavailability ~100%, **not CYP-metabolized** (few interactions), **renal** elimination → reduce dose in severe renal impairment.
• **ADRs** generally mild: dizziness, headache, confusion, hallucinations, constipation. Better tolerated than AChE inhibitors (no cholinergic GI/cardiac effects).
• ⚠️ Avoid with other NMDA antagonists (amantadine, ketamine, dextromethorphan).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 14734594.

**Anti-amyloid antibodies = first true disease-modifiers** (slow decline by clearing plaque; not symptomatic).
• **Lecanemab**: humanized IgG1 against **soluble Aβ protofibrils**. In Clarity AD (18 months) it slowed CDR-SB decline vs placebo (difference −0.45) (van Dyck 2023, NEJM, [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2212948)).
• ⚠️ **ARIA** (amyloid-related imaging abnormalities), a class effect: **ARIA-E** (edema/effusion) and **ARIA-H** (microhemorrhage/siderosis) → serial **MRI monitoring**; risk increased with **APOE ε4 homozygosity** and anticoagulants. ARIA-E ~6% vs ~1% placebo (Clarity AD, [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tjpad.2025.100160)); infusion reactions ~26% (van Dyck).
• **Aducanumab**: anti-fibril; controversial accelerated FDA approval, later withdrawn.
• **Failed/abandoned**: β/γ-secretase inhibitors (semagacestat, verubecestat), early mAbs (bapineuzumab, solanezumab), anti-Tau.
• **NSAIDs, vitamin E, statins, chelators**: theoretical rationale, **no proven clinical benefit**.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 36449413.

**Key point**: glucocorticoids treat **only the acute relapse** (shorten attack duration) but do **not modify long-term course** or cumulative disability.
• **Typical regimen**: **high-dose IV methylprednisolone** (e.g. 1 g/day for 3-5 days), with or without an oral taper; IV pulse is preferred over low-dose oral.
• **Mechanism**: bind the cytoplasmic receptor → inhibition of **NF-κB** → reduced pro-inflammatory cytokines, reduced lymphocyte migration across the BBB, T-cell apoptosis/redistribution, barrier stabilization (reduces lesion edema).
• **Acute ADRs** (even with short courses): hyperglycemia, insomnia/agitation or **steroid psychosis**, fluid retention/hypertension, hypokalemia, gastritis; gastric prophylaxis as needed.
• **Chronic ADRs** (avoid prolonged use): osteoporosis/osteonecrosis, myopathy, cataract, iatrogenic Cushing syndrome, HPA-axis suppression.

**Concept**: **non-specific** cytotoxic immunosuppressants, now second-line in MS (replaced by targeted DMTs); they hit any rapidly proliferating cell, not only lymphocytes.
• **Target = DNA synthesis**: azathioprine and cladribine (purine analogues), methotrexate (antifolate, inhibits dihydrofolate reductase), cyclophosphamide (alkylating agent), teriflunomide (inhibits **dihydroorotate dehydrogenase** → reduced de novo pyrimidines).
• **Class ADRs**: **myelosuppression** (monitor blood count), mucositis, alopecia, immunosuppression with opportunistic infections, hepato/nephrotoxicity, long-term **malignancy risk**.
• ⚠️ **Cyclophosphamide**: hemorrhagic cystitis (from acrolein → **mesna** + hydration), infertility. **Methotrexate**: hepatotoxicity, pneumonitis, give **folic acid** supplement; teratogenic. **Teriflunomide**: hepatotoxicity, alopecia, **teratogenic** (long half-life → accelerated elimination with cholestyramine).
• Retain a role in rheumatoid arthritis, SLE, IBD.

**Concept**: DMTs reduce relapse rate and MRI activity; higher efficacy = higher risk (line-based choice).
• **Classic injectables** (1st line, safe): **interferon-β** (increases anti-inflammatory cytokines, reduces lymphocyte trafficking; ADR: flu-like syndrome, skin reactions, raised transaminases); **glatiramer acetate** (peptide that diverts the autoimmune response; well tolerated).
• **Anti-CD20** (B-cell depletion): **ocrelizumab**, the only one also approved for **primary progressive MS** (ORATORIO: 12-week confirmed disability progression 32.9% vs 39.3% placebo, Montalban 2017, NEJM, [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1606468)); ADR: infusion reactions, infections.
• ⚠️ **Natalizumab** (anti-α4-integrin, blocks lymphocyte entry into CNS): risk of **JC-virus PML** → **risk stratification with anti-JCV serology** + treatment duration + prior immunosuppressant (Sørensen 2012, [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1177/1352458511435105)); anti-JCV screening reduced PML incidence (Vukusic 2020, [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaneurol.2019.2670)).
• ⚠️ **Fingolimod** (**S1P** modulator, traps lymphocytes in lymph nodes): **first-dose bradycardia/AV block** → **6-hour ECG monitoring** (Espinosa 2011, [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1177/1352458511410344); reviewed in Brown 2007, [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1345/aph.1G424)); also lymphopenia, macular edema, rare PML.
• Other orals: **dimethyl fumarate** (Nrf2 pathway, antioxidant; flushing, GI), teriflunomide. **Alemtuzumab** (anti-CD52): secondary autoimmunity (thyroid, ITP, nephropathy).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 28002688.

**Concept**: **symptomatic** therapy for MS-related complaints, parallel to DMTs; does not affect course but quality of life.
• **Spasticity**: **baclofen** (spinal **GABA-B** agonist → reduced tone; sedation, hypotonia, abrupt withdrawal → rebound spasticity/seizures; **intrathecal** route in severe cases); alternatives: **tizanidine** (α2-agonist), benzodiazepines, dantrolene; **botulinum toxin** for focal forms.
• **Walking impairment**: **dalfampridine/fampridine** (blocks voltage-gated **K⁺** channels → prolongs the action potential in demyelinated axons → improves walking speed); ⚠️ dose-dependent **seizures**, contraindicated in epilepsy or renal impairment.
• **Neuropathic pain**: **tricyclic antidepressants** and **gabapentinoids** (gabapentin, pregabalin); **cannabinoids** (THC/cannabidiol oromucosal spray) for refractory spasticity and pain.
• **Other symptoms**: bladder dysfunction (anticholinergics for overactive bladder; cholinergics/catheterization for retention), **fatigue**, depression, sexual dysfunction.

**Logic of the WHO ladder: you step up, you don't "skip".** Designed for cancer pain but it guides all nociceptive pain: start at the step matching intensity and move up if control is inadequate, keeping the baseline non-opioid.
• **Sparing effect (exam point)**: adding a non-opioid (NSAID/paracetamol) or an adjuvant to an opioid lets you **lower the opioid dose** → less respiratory depression, constipation, nausea and less tolerance, for the same analgesia.
• **Adjuvants = pure non-analgesics** but useful on specific components: **antidepressants (TCAs, SNRI/duloxetine) and gabapentinoids (gabapentin, pregabalin)** are central in **neuropathic pain**; corticosteroids in inflammatory/compressive pain; **ketamine** (anti-NMDA) in refractory/central pain.
• **Mind the NSAID**: effective in mild/bone pain but with an analgesic ceiling and GI/renal/CV toxicity → ketorolac must be limited to a few days.
• Chronic pain is **subjective** (0-10 scale): a realistic goal is to cut intensity and disability and improve function, not to abolish it.

**Exam key: all opioid receptors are GPCRs coupled to Gi/Go.** Activation **inhibits adenylyl cyclase (↓ cAMP)**, **closes voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channels** (presynaptic → ↓ release of pain neurotransmitters, e.g. substance P/glutamate) and **opens K⁺ channels** (postsynaptic → hyperpolarization). Net effect: reduced neuronal excitability.
• **μ (MOP)** mediates most clinical analgesia but also euphoria, respiratory depression, constipation and dependence; **κ (KOP)** gives spinal analgesia and tends to cause **dysphoria**; **δ (DOP)** modulates affective tone and analgesia.
• **Two sites of action**: at the **spinal** level (dorsal horn) it blocks transmission of the pain signal; at the **supraspinal** level it activates **descending inhibitory pathways** (periaqueductal gray → raphe magnus).
• **Basis of euphoria/addiction**: in the ventral tegmental area μ inhibits GABAergic interneurons → disinhibits dopaminergic neurons → ↑ dopamine in the nucleus accumbens (reward circuit).

**Respiratory depression is the cause of death in overdose.** μ-opioids depress the brainstem centres by blunting the **sensitivity to CO₂** → hypoventilation, hypercapnia and respiratory arrest; risk rises with lipophilicity and with other depressants (benzodiazepines, alcohol).
• **Overdose triad**: coma, **pinpoint miosis** and respiratory depression; mydriasis appears late (hypoxia/anoxia, an ominous sign).
• **Differential tolerance (exam)**: it develops quickly to analgesia, euphoria, sedation and respiratory depression, but **NOT to miosis and constipation**, which persist throughout treatment (PMID 18443635).
• **Constipation**: μ on the enteric plexuses → ↓ peristalsis and ↑ sphincter tone; it must be prevented routinely (osmotic/stimulant laxatives).
• **Dependence vs addiction**: **physical dependence** (withdrawal syndrome on stopping/antagonist: mydriasis, sweating, cramps, diarrhea, piloerection) is expected and is not the same as **addiction** (compulsive use, craving). Tolerance is reversible → a detoxified patient who resumes the old dose risks overdose.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 18443635.

**Codeine = prodrug: it works only after bioactivation.** **CYP2D6** O-demethylates it to **morphine** (≈10%), the true analgesic/antitussive; codeine itself has low μ affinity (PMID 15907643).
• **CYP2D6 polymorphism (high-yield)**: **ultrarapid metabolizers** make too much morphine → toxicity/respiratory depression; **poor metabolizers** get no analgesia. Hence codeine is **contraindicated in children** (especially after tonsillectomy/adenoidectomy and with OSAS) following documented cases of respiratory depression, anoxic injury and death (PMID 28323671); caution in breastfeeding.
• **Tramadol = dual mechanism**: weak μ-agonist **+ inhibition of noradrenaline and serotonin reuptake** (boosts descending pathways); it too is activated by CYP2D6 to O-desmethyltramadol (PMID 21074743).
• ⚠️ **Tramadol class risks**: it **lowers the seizure threshold** (seizures in overdose or at high doses) and, through its serotonergic action, can cause **serotonin syndrome**, especially combined with SSRIs/SNRIs/MAOIs (PMID 31721720).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 15907643, 28323671, 21074743, 31721720.

**Morphine = reference, but the metabolites matter.** It is glucuronidated to **M6G** (active, contributes to analgesia) and **M3G** (non-analgesic, neuroexcitatory); both are renally cleared → in **renal failure M6G accumulates**, causing prolonged sedation and respiratory depression (PMID 15907643). Here opioids with inactive metabolites are preferred.
• **Methadone**: μ-agonist with a **long, highly variable half-life** → risk of accumulation; above all it **prolongs the QT (hERG block) → torsades de pointes**, dose-dependent and favoured by hypokalemia/female sex/arrhythmogenic drugs (PMID 19386945; PMID 15918160). Useful in substitution therapy.
• **Buprenorphine**: **partial μ-agonist** → it shows a **ceiling effect on respiratory depression** (wider safety margin), also exploited for addiction treatment (Suboxone = + naloxone) (PMID 29671504).
• **Fentanils** (fentanyl, sufentanil, remifentanil): extremely potent and highly lipophilic; illicit fentanyl is now a leading cause of overdose death. **Tapentadol**: μ-agonist + NA-reuptake inhibition (no serotonin) → lower risk of serotonin syndrome (PMID 21074743). **Meperidine/pethidine**: its metabolite **normeperidine is neurotoxic** (seizures) and accumulates with repeated doses even with normal renal function → strongly discouraged (PMID 12659611). **Hydromorphone**: an option in renal failure.
• **Equianalgesia (exam)**: **morphine** is the reference; **fentanyl is ~100 times** more potent than morphine, **sufentanil ~1000 times**, whereas **tramadol is less potent**.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 15907643, 19386945, 15918160, 29671504, 21074743, 12659611.

**Naloxone = the life-saving antidote for opioid overdose.** A high-affinity competitive μ-antagonist, **parenteral/intranasal** (orally it has a strong first-pass effect → ineffective, which is what blocks IV abuse of Suboxone); it rapidly reverses respiratory depression (PMID 26289651).
• ⚠️ **Exam point: short half-life (~30-60 min), shorter than many opioids (methadone, extended-release opioids)** → risk of **re-narcotization/re-sedation** once the antidote wears off → titrate the lowest effective dose and **keep the patient under observation**, with repeat boluses or an infusion if needed.
• In dependent patients it precipitates an **acute withdrawal syndrome** → titrate cautiously.
• **Naltrexone**: oral, long-acting → maintenance of abstinence (alcohol/opioids), not for emergencies. **Loperamide**: a μ-agonist that does not cross the blood-brain barrier → a peripheral antidiarrheal.
• **Paracetamol**: analgesic/antipyretic with a **mainly central action**, weak anti-inflammatory; not gastrotoxic. ⚠️ Dose-dependent **hepatotoxicity** (NAPQI) in overdose → antidote **N-acetylcysteine**.
• **Cannabinoids**: analgesia via **CB1** receptors on pain circuits (modulation of descending pathways).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 26289651.

**General principle:** AEDs raise the **seizure threshold** or limit spread without curing the disease; the goal is **monotherapy at the lowest effective dose**, slowly titrated.
• **Targets by class:** **Na⁺** channels (stabilise the inactivated state → use-dependence, like local anaesthetics); thalamic **T-type Ca²⁺** channels (absences); **GABA** enhancement; **glutamate** suppression (few drugs).
• **First-generation drugs are strong CYP inducers** (CYP3A4/2C9/1A2) → lower oral contraceptives, anticoagulants and other AEDs; **valproate** is the exception (an inhibitor).
• ⚠️ "Do not switch an effective AED" reflects relapse risk; any switch is done by gradual **overlap**.
• **Never stop abruptly** → rebound seizures/status epilepticus.

**Phenytoin — kinetics is the exam point:** elimination is **saturable (Michaelis-Menten)**, i.e. **zero-order** at therapeutic doses → small dose increments cause **disproportionate concentration jumps** and toxicity (PubMed PMID 4082240, https://doi.org/10.1097/00007691-198512000-00007).
• **Dose-related toxicity (narrow window):** nystagmus → cerebellar ataxia → lethargy/coma as levels rise.
• **Chronic:** gingival hyperplasia, hirsutism, peripheral neuropathy, osteomalacia.
• **~90% protein-bound** → free fraction rises in hypoalbuminaemia/uraemia; teratogenic (fetal hydantoin syndrome).
• **Carbamazepine:** use-dependent Na⁺ block; first-line for trigeminal neuralgia; autoinduces its metabolism; causes hyponatraemia (SIADH) and ⚠️ **SJS/TEN in HLA-B*15:02 carriers** (PMID 40109622, https://doi.org/10.1097/MS9.0000000000002736).
• **Oxcarbazepine:** less induction but more hyponatraemia.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 4082240, 40109622.

**Barbiturates vs benzodiazepines (key distinction):** both act on **GABA-A**, but barbiturates increase the **duration** of Cl⁻-channel opening (and at high dose open it **without GABA** → respiratory depression/coma, no safety ceiling), whereas BDZs increase its **frequency** (safer ceiling).
• **Phenobarbital:** enzyme inducer, long half-life; **primidone** is its prodrug.
• **Ethosuximide:** selective thalamic **T-type Ca²⁺** block → first-line for pure absences; not effective on tonic-clonic seizures.
• **Valproate — multiple mechanism** (Na⁺, T-Ca²⁺, ↑ GABA, ↓ glutamate) → broad-spectrum AED and mood stabiliser.
• ⚠️ **Valproate is the most teratogenic AED:** highest risk of **major malformations** (neural-tube defects) and neurodevelopmental harm → avoided in pregnancy (PubMed PMID 32768086, https://doi.org/10.1016/B978-0-444-64240-0.00009-X). Other ADRs: hepatotoxicity (severe in children <2 y), hyperammonaemia, thrombocytopenia, weight gain.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 32768086.

**Why second-generation prevailed:** similar efficacy, **less enzyme induction**, fewer interactions, better pregnancy profile.
• **Levetiracetam/brivaracetam:** bind synaptic-vesicle glycoprotein **SV2A**, modulating neurotransmitter release; SV2A is a validated antiepileptic target (PubMed PMID 28393712, https://doi.org/10.2174/1871527316666170404115027). No CYP metabolism (levetiracetam renally cleared), fast titration; ⚠️ signature **behavioural** ADRs (irritability, aggression, depression).
• **Lamotrigine:** Na⁺ block; clean profile, good in pregnancy/bipolar, but ⚠️ **rash → SJS** with fast titration (slow it with valproate).
• **Topiramate:** multiple mechanism + **carbonic-anhydrase** inhibition → weight loss, paraesthesias, renal stones, cognitive slowing; teratogenic (cleft lip/palate).
• **Gabapentin/pregabalin:** bind the Ca²⁺ **α2δ** subunit (not direct GABA-mimetics) → useful in neuropathic pain.
• **Vigabatrin** (visual-field constriction) and **felbamate** (hepatotoxicity + aplastic anaemia) limited by toxicity.
• **PK advantage of 2nd generation: largely CYP-independent** → few interactions and minimal induction (vs the potent 1st-gen inducers: phenobarbital/phenytoin/carbamazepine).
• **Levetiracetam**: ~65% excreted unchanged in urine + acetamide hydrolysis; **neither induces nor is a CYP/UGT substrate**. **Topiramate**: mostly **excreted unchanged renally**. **Lamotrigine**: **glucuronidation (UGT)**.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 28393712.

**Quick exam map (drug ↔ seizure type):**
• **Absences (petit mal):** ethosuximide first-line; valproate if tonic-clonic coexist; ⚠️ carbamazepine and phenytoin can **worsen** absences.
• **Focal (partial):** carbamazepine/lamotrigine/levetiracetam; oxcarbazepine, lacosamide.
• **Generalised tonic-clonic / idiopathic generalised epilepsy:** valproate (most effective, but avoided in women of childbearing potential) → otherwise lamotrigine/levetiracetam.
• **Infantile spasms (West):** ACTH/steroids; vigabatrin (especially in tuberous sclerosis).
• **Status epilepticus:** IV benzodiazepines (lorazepam/diazepam) first, then IV phenytoin/valproate/levetiracetam.
• **Pregnancy:** prefer second-generation (lamotrigine, levetiracetam) + folic acid; avoid valproate.

**Mechanism logic:** inhibiting **HMG-CoA reductase** (rate-limiting step) lowers intra-hepatocyte cholesterol → the cell up-regulates **LDL receptors** via **SREBP-2** → more LDL clearance (competitive, reversible).
• **Evening dosing** maximises effect because endogenous synthesis is nocturnal (mainly for **short-half-life** statins: simvastatin, lovastatin, fluvastatin); long-half-life atorvastatin/rosuvastatin can be taken anytime.
• **Metabolism = interaction divide:** simvastatin, lovastatin and atorvastatin depend on **CYP3A4**; **pravastatin and rosuvastatin** (hydrophilic) are **not** major CYP3A4 substrates → safer with 3A4 inhibitors.
• **Pravastatin:** hydrophilic, little CYP hepatic entry, an option when interactions are feared.
• **Pleiotropic effects** (plaque stabilisation, anti-inflammatory) are real but secondary to LDL lowering.

**Muscle ADR (the critical point):** **statin-associated muscle symptoms (SAMS)** range from myalgia/weakness to **rhabdomyolysis** with markedly raised **CK** and myoglobinuric renal failure; they affect **10-25%** of users and are the leading cause of discontinuation (PubMed PMID 34769118, https://doi.org/10.3390/ijms222111687). Cerivastatin was withdrawn for rhabdomyolysis.
• **Risk-raising factors:** high dose, **fibrate co-therapy** (especially gemfibrozil), **CYP3A4 inhibitors** (macrolides, azoles, cyclosporine), hypothyroidism, age, CKD.
• **Hydrophilic statins (pravastatin, rosuvastatin)** carry less interaction risk than CYP3A4-dependent simvastatin/lovastatin.
• **Hepatotoxicity:** transaminase rise usually mild/reversible (baseline check; routine ongoing monitoring not needed).
• ⚠️ **New-onset diabetes:** statins raise incident diabetes (~9-13% in RCTs; RR ~1.44 observational), a small class effect outweighed by cardiovascular benefit (PMID 28416099, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.numecd.2017.03.001).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 34769118, 28416099.

**Placing them vs statins (by lipid target):**
• **Fibrates** (gemfibrozil, fenofibrate): **PPAR-α** agonists → ↑ lipoprotein-lipase and β-oxidation → **first-line for marked hypertriglyceridaemia** (pancreatitis prevention). ⚠️ They **raise myopathy/rhabdomyolysis risk with statins**, worst with **gemfibrozil** (inhibits statin glucuronidation) → prefer **fenofibrate** if combining; gallstone risk.
• **Ezetimibe:** inhibits intestinal **NPC1L1** → blocks **cholesterol absorption** (dietary and biliary) without affecting fat-soluble vitamins; synergistic with statins.
• **Bile-acid resins** (cholestyramine, colestipol): unabsorbed → ⚠️ reduce absorption of **fat-soluble vitamins A/D/E/K** and other drugs (space dosing 1-2 h before or 4-6 h after); may raise triglycerides.
• **Niacin/acipimox:** reduce lipolysis → **↑ HDL**; limited by prostaglandin-mediated **flushing** (eased by aspirin); little used today.
• **PCSK9 inhibitors** (alirocumab, evolocumab): subcutaneous anti-PCSK9 **monoclonal antibodies** that **prevent LDL-receptor degradation** (boosting its hepatic recycling) → **marked LDL reduction**; used in familial hypercholesterolemia and as add-on to statins.

**Why combine rather than up-titrate.** Each class hits a different system (RAAS, volume/kidney, sympathetic outflow, vessel wall): adding a second drug with a distinct target gives better BP control and fewer dose-dependent effects than doubling monotherapy.
• **Rationale of fixed combinations:** ACE-I/ARB + thiazide (RAAS blockade blunts diuretic-induced hyperreninemia and hypokalemia); ACE-I/ARB + dihydropyridine CCB (synergy, less ankle edema).
• **⚠️ Never combine ACE-I + ARB** (nor + aliskiren in diabetics): no added benefit, more hyperkalemia/renal injury.
• **Practical sequence:** often start with two low-dose drugs when BP is very high or organ damage is present.
• **Central sympatholytics and α1-blockers stay late-line** (adverse effects, no proven mortality reduction vs major classes).

**Abrupt clonidine withdrawal is an emergency.** After chronic use, sudden stop triggers **rebound hypertension** with tachycardia, sweating, anxiety and headache, driven by unmasked central noradrenergic overactivity (shown experimentally, PMID 6177942). In a controlled trial **64% of clonidine patients** had a diastolic rise ≥10 mmHg by **day 2**, earlier and greater than with guanfacine (PMID 3882307) → **taper slowly**; concurrent β-blockade worsens the rebound (unopposed peripheral α).
• **Managing rebound:** restart clonidine or give an α-blocker ± β-blocker (never a β-blocker alone).
• **Moxonidine/rilmenidine** (I1 imidazoline agonists): better metabolic profile, less sedation/depression.
• **Clonidine** also helps opioid/alcohol withdrawal, flushing, diarrhea, and comes as a weekly transdermal patch.

**First-choice antihypertensive in pregnancy.** Methyldopa has the best obstetric safety record, with **long-term follow-up of children exposed in utero** showing no neurodevelopmental harm (PMID 1825149) → hence preferred over more potent but less-tested agents; conversely **β-blockers from the 2nd trimester** are linked to fetal growth restriction (PMID 1825149).
• **Positive direct Coombs test** in ~10-20% after 6-12 months, but overt **autoimmune hemolytic anemia** in only a minority: methyldopa induces anti-Rh autoantibodies (PMID 1090160) → monitor CBC and Coombs; the test may stay positive for months after stopping.
• **Hepatotoxicity** (from asymptomatic transaminase rise to hepatitis) → monitor.
• **Sedation** and slow onset (central active metabolite) limit its use outside pregnancy.

**"First-dose phenomenon."** Marked orthostatic hypotension, even syncope, after the initial dose (described for prazosin, PMID 793676) → **start low, at bedtime, lying down**, then titrate slowly. It is **transient**: chronic α1 blockade up-regulates vascular α2 receptors that resume part of the tone, explaining the developing orthostatic tolerance (PMID 2417044).
• **Favorable metabolic effect:** mild ↓ LDL/triglycerides and ↑ glucose tolerance (useful in dyslipidemia/diabetes).
• **⚠️ ALLHAT:** the doxazosin arm was stopped for excess heart failure vs chlorthalidone → α1-blockers are **not first-line monotherapy** for hypertension.
• **α1A-selective agents** (tamsulosin, silodosin): relax prostate/bladder neck in BPH with less hypotension, but cause **retrograde ejaculation** and **IFIS** (intraoperative floppy iris syndrome) during cataract surgery.

**Cardioselectivity is dose-dependent and relative.** At high doses even atenolol/metoprolol block β2 → bronchospasm possible: selective agents are the **safest but not harmless** in asthma/COPD, whereas non-selective ones (propranolol, nadolol, timolol) are the riskiest (PMID 14724718).
• **Carvedilol and labetalol = β + α1 blockade:** added α1 block gives vasodilation, prevents reflex α-mediated vasoconstriction and the pressor surge; **carvedilol** also has antioxidant/anti-remodeling effects and in the **COMET** trial reduced all-cause mortality vs metoprolol tartrate (PMID 15516861; PMID 9558423).
• **ISA** (pindolol, acebutolol): less resting bradycardia/lipid effect, but not used post-MI or in heart failure.
• **Nebivolol** (3rd-gen β1-selective): endothelial **NO-mediated** vasodilation.
• **Lipophilic** agents (propranolol) → CNS effects (nightmares, depression) and hepatic CYP metabolism/interactions; **hydrophilic** ones (atenolol, nadolol) → renal elimination, fewer central effects.

**Never stop abruptly.** Chronic therapy up-regulates β-receptors; sudden withdrawal triggers a **withdrawal syndrome** with tachycardia, hypertension and, in coronary patients, **angina, MI or sudden death**: in the NEJM trial ischemic events occurred in 10/20 patients after abrupt propranolol cessation (PMID 1097925), with severe anginal flare vs verapamil (PMID 6814227) → **taper over 1-2 weeks**.
• **⚠️ Insulin-treated diabetic:** β-blockers **mask the adrenergic warning symptoms of hypoglycemia** (tachycardia, tremor), leaving only sweating, and **non-selective agents delay glycemic recovery** by blocking β2-mediated glycogenolysis (PMID 6106148) → prefer β1-selective agents.
• **⚠️ Pheochromocytoma:** never a β-blocker alone → paradoxical hypertension from unopposed α; α-block first.
• **Mortality-benefit indications:** post-MI and heart failure (bisoprolol, carvedilol, metoprolol succinate, nebivolol), with very gradual up-titration.
• Other uses: arrhythmias (class II), angina, migraine prophylaxis, essential tremor, hyperthyroidism, esophageal varices (non-selective).
• **Benefit in the young hypertensive (<60 yr)**: because of catecholaminergic over-activation they cut the risk of **myocardial infarction by 30-40%** and **stroke by 50-55%**; in the unselected hypertensive the effect fades over time (**"pseudo-antihypertensive"**) → they stay 3rd-line apart from the known exceptions.

**Logic of the targets** (upstream → downstream): renin (→ aliskiren), ACE (→ ACE inhibitors), the AT1 receptor (→ sartans), aldosterone (→ antagonists, file B11b). Blocking any single point lowers Ang II/aldosterone tone.
• **Negative feedback**: Ang II restrains renin release; blocking the system removes this brake → ↑ renin and Ang I upstream (the basis of efficacy loss over time and of activation of alternative pathways such as **chymase**, which is ACE-independent).
• **ACE/AT1 vs ACE2/AT2-Ang(1-7) axis**: the first is vasoconstrictive, pro-fibrotic, sodium-retaining; the second is vasodilatory/cardioprotective. The drugs shift the balance toward the protective arm.
• **ACE2** is the cellular entry gate of **SARS-CoV-2**: the spike glycoprotein binds ACE2 to enter human cells (PMID 32155444); consumption of the receptor lowers protective Ang(1-7).
• **Bradykinin**: ACE (= kininase II) degrades it → its inhibition makes it accumulate (NO-mediated vasodilation, but also cough/angioedema): this is the true clinical watershed between ACE inhibitors and sartans.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 32155444.

**The two "bradykinin" ADRs** (class signature, NOT dose-dependent): the irritating **dry cough** affects roughly **5-20%** of treated patients, **angioedema** in ~**0.1-0.5%** (rare but potentially fatal if it involves the airway); being class effects, switching one ACE inhibitor for another does not resolve them (PMID 25058867).
• **Hyperkalemia + functional AKI**: ↓ aldosterone retains K⁺; in **bilateral renal artery stenosis** (or a stenotic solitary kidney) Ang II maintains filtration pressure by constricting the **efferent** arteriole → blocking it precipitates **acute renal failure** (absolute contraindication).
• ⚠️ **PREGNANCY = contraindication**: exposure (even in the **1st trimester**) increases major congenital malformations — cardiovascular and CNS (RR ~2.7 in the first trimester); in the 2nd-3rd trimester, fetopathy with oligohydramnios, skull hypoplasia, neonatal AKI, fetal death (PMID 16760444).
• **Dual-blockade rule**: do NOT combine an ACE inhibitor + a sartan → no cardiovascular benefit but more hypotension, hyperkalemia and **renal damage** (ONTARGET) (PMID 18707986).
• **Nephroprotection**: ↓ proteinuria by dilating the efferent arteriole → indicated in the diabetic with albuminuria.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 25058867, 16760444, 18707986.

**Why they cause neither cough nor (almost) angioedema**: they block the AT1 receptor downstream of ACE and **do not interfere with bradykinin metabolism** → the kinin effects are absent → they are the choice in a patient who has developed **ACE-inhibitor cough** (PMID 25058867).
• They do share with ACE inhibitors: **hyperkalemia**, AKI in **bilateral renal artery stenosis**, and the **absolute contraindication in pregnancy** (the same RAAS-blockade fetopathy).
• **Dual-blockade rule** (applies here too): sartan + ACE inhibitor → more hyperkalemia, hypotension and worse **renal outcomes** without cardiovascular benefit (ONTARGET) (PMID 18707986). Same ban with aliskiren in the diabetic.
• **Nephroprotection in type 2 diabetes (high-yield)**: **losartan** reduces creatinine doubling (-25%) and ESRD (-28%) beyond the blood-pressure effect (RENAAL, PMID 11565518); **irbesartan** slows nephropathy independently of blood pressure and better than amlodipine (IDNT, PMID 11565517).
• **Losartan**: mild **uricosuric** effect (useful with hyperuricemia/gout).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 25058867, 18707986, 11565518, 11565517.

**Two families, two profiles not to be confused** (although not RAAS drugs, they sit in this block as co-antihypertensives):
• **Dihydropyridines** (amlodipine, nifedipine): vessel-selective → arteriolar vasodilation, **no negative chronotropic/dromotropic effect**; immediate-release nifedipine causes **reflex tachycardia** and is dangerous in unstable angina, whereas **amlodipine** (slow absorption, long t½) is free of it.
• **Verapamil/diltiazem** (non-dihydropyridines): act on the **heart** → negative chronotropic/dromotropic and inotropic (class **IV** antiarrhythmics) → ⚠️ **bradycardia and AV block**; the **combination with beta-blockers is contraindicated** (AV block/asystole, decompensation) and so is use in reduced-ejection-fraction heart failure.
• **CYP3A4 interaction**: verapamil/diltiazem are inhibitors → ↑ levels of **digoxin**, statins, cyclosporine.
• **Favorable RAAS combination**: **dihydropyridine + ACE inhibitor** (e.g. amlodipine + perindopril) — the ACE inhibitor blunts reflex tachycardia and RAAS activation and **reduces the dependent edema** of the dihydropyridine. *(In IDNT amlodipine was less renoprotective than the sartan at equal blood pressure — PMID 11565517.)*

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 11565517.

**Why β-blockers in heart failure (the apparent paradox).** Chronic sympathetic overactivation is initially compensatory but becomes **maladaptive**: down-regulation/desensitization of **β1** receptors (via β-arrestins and GRK), catecholamine toxicity on cardiomyocytes, arrhythmias and remodeling. Blocking β1 **reverses remodeling** and improves survival in heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF).
• **Three β-blockers with proven mortality benefit**: **carvedilol, bisoprolol, metoprolol succinate** (slow titration, never in acute decompensated failure).
• **Modern cornerstone**: the "unloading" strategy (↓ pre/afterload, Na⁺ and water) is achieved through neurohormonal blockade — **ACE inhibitors/ARBs, aldosterone antagonists, β-blockers** — which improves prognosis; **digitalis** remains symptomatic/functional, with no survival benefit.
• **Excitation–contraction coupling** depends on Ca²⁺: the **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase** maintains the Na⁺ gradient that drives the **Na⁺/Ca²⁺ antiporter** — the target exploited by digitalis for its inotropic effect.

**Very narrow therapeutic window** (target ~0.5–0.9 ng/mL in heart failure): digoxin has a **low therapeutic index** and acts by inhibiting the **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase** (PMID 25089630). High-yield exam material:
• **Hypokalemia = key precipitating factor**: K⁺ competes with digoxin at the pump, so low potassium **unmasks/worsens toxicity** even at "therapeutic" levels → beware of combination with loop/thiazide diuretics.
• **Toxic triad**: **GI** (nausea, vomiting, anorexia), **visual** (blurred vision, halos, **yellow-green dyschromatopsia/xanthopsia**), **cardiac** (any arrhythmia: ectopic beats, AV block, triggered-activity tachyarrhythmias).
• **Antidote**: **digoxin-specific Fab fragments** in severe poisoning — life-threatening arrhythmias, hemodynamic instability, **hyperkalemia >5–6 mmol/L** (marker of severe acute toxicity) (PMID 25089630).
• **Kinetics**: low hepatic extraction, **predominantly renal elimination** → reduce dose in renal impairment and the elderly; bioavailability 60–80%, low protein binding (~20%). Many interactions (e.g. **quinidine, verapamil, amiodarone ↑ digoxin levels**).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 25089630.

**Xanthine with a narrow therapeutic window** (classic range ~10–20 µg/mL): theophylline often requires **plasma concentration monitoring**, because toxicity (tachyarrhythmias, seizures) appears at levels only slightly above effective ones.
• **Metabolized via CYP1A2** (with a CYP3A4 component): it is a textbook drug for interactions and for the effect of **smoking**, which **induces CYP1A2** → smokers clear more theophylline; **smoking cessation** or adding **CYP1A2 inhibitors** raises levels toward toxicity (PMID 19490292).
• **Exam interactions**: **fluoroquinolones** (ciprofloxacin/enoxacin) and **macrolides** (clarithromycin/erythromycin) inhibit CYP1A2/CYP3A4 → reduce theophylline clearance and cause toxicity (PMID 11408986).
• **Mechanisms**: at therapeutic doses **adenosine-receptor antagonism** predominates; phosphodiesterase inhibition (↑ cAMP) and Ca²⁺ effects require higher concentrations. Net effects: **bronchodilation**, CNS/respiratory stimulation, cardiac stimulation (positive chrono/inotropy) → caution in cardiac patients.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 19490292, 11408986.

**Reasoned map of site → K⁺ effect and potency.** The more proximally Na⁺ reabsorption is blocked, the more Na⁺ reaches downstream segments where it is exchanged for K⁺ → this explains the **potassium rule**.
• **Carbonic-anhydrase inhibitors → loop → thiazides = K⁺ loss** (hypokalemia); **Na⁺-channel blockers (amiloride/triamterene) + aldosterone antagonists (spironolactone) = K⁺ sparing** (hyperkalemia risk).
• **Potency by fraction of Na⁺ blocked**: **loop diuretics** are most potent (block up to ~25% of filtered Na⁺, thick ascending limb); **thiazides** are intermediate (distal tubule); **K⁺-sparing** agents are weak (collecting duct).
• **Cross-cutting ADRs** predictable from site: volume depletion/prerenal azotemia (all except collecting-duct agents), **hypokalemia + hypochloremic alkalosis** (loop and thiazides), **hyperkalemia + acidosis** (K⁺-sparing), hyponatremia (loop/thiazides) (PMID 10653441).
• Clinical logic: classes acting at **different sites are often combined** (e.g. loop + thiazide) to overcome resistance, with electrolyte monitoring.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 10653441.

**Weak, self-limiting diuretic.** By inhibiting carbonic anhydrase in the proximal tubule, acetazolamide reduces **NaHCO₃** reabsorption → bicarbonaturia, alkaline urine and **metabolic acidosis**; but the natriuretic effect wanes within days (systemic acidosis reduces substrate), so it is little used as a pure diuretic.
• **Acute mountain sickness**: the renally induced metabolic acidosis **stimulates ventilation** and counteracts the respiratory alkalosis of high-altitude hyperventilation, improving oxygenation and sleep quality; with additional effects on the carotid body and diuresis (PMID 17023566).
• **Other indications**: **glaucoma** (↓ production of bicarbonate-rich aqueous humor), metabolic alkalosis (including loop-diuretic–induced), some forms of epilepsy.
• **ADRs**: **hypokalemic metabolic acidosis**, paresthesias, drowsiness; alkaline urine with hypocitraturia favors **calcium-phosphate stones**. It is a **sulfonamide** → cross allergic reactions; rarely myelosuppression. **Contraindicated in cirrhosis/severe liver disease** (reduced NH₄⁺ excretion → risk of encephalopathy).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 17023566.

**Osmotically active, non-reabsorbed solute.** Mannitol is filtered and **not reabsorbed**: it holds water in the tubular lumen (mainly in the **descending limb of the loop**, which is water-permeable) → water diuresis. It must be given **intravenously** (not absorbed orally, where it acts as an osmotic laxative).
• **Key indication = raised intracranial pressure/cerebral edema**: by raising plasma osmolarity it **draws water out of the brain parenchyma** (and the eye) across intact barriers → ↓ intracranial pressure; also used in acute glaucoma.
• **Kinetics/use**: it acts early by expanding intravascular volume before diuresis; also used to maintain urine flow in some forms of oliguric acute kidney injury.
• **⚠️ Safety contraindication**: the transient volume expansion can precipitate **pulmonary edema in congestive heart failure**; also contraindicated in **anuria** (without filtration mannitol accumulates in the circulation) and active intracranial hemorrhage. It may cause **dilutional hyponatremia** followed by dehydration/hypernatremia. *(Slide topic.)*

**The most potent diuretics** ("high-ceiling"): they block the **Na⁺/K⁺/2Cl⁻ (NKCC2)** cotransporter of the thick ascending limb, the segment that reabsorbs 25–30% of filtered Na⁺. They also abolish the corticomedullary gradient and paracellular **Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺** absorption.
• **Electrolyte–metabolic profile (exam)**: **hypokalemia**, **hypochloremic alkalosis**, **hypomagnesemia**, **hypovolemia/hyponatremia**; unlike thiazides they **increase Ca²⁺ excretion** → useful in **hypercalcemia**. Metabolic effects: **hyperuricemia** (competition for tubular secretion → gout risk), **hyperglycemia**, dyslipidemia (PMID 10653441).
• **Dose-dependent ototoxicity**: typically **reversible**, linked to high infusion rate/doses and renal impairment; **greatest with ethacrynic acid**. It is **potentiated by aminoglycosides** (and cisplatin) → avoid the combination (PMID 9643307; PMID 10653441).
• **Clinical**: drug of choice in **acute pulmonary edema** and in heart failure with overload; they act from the luminal side, so adequate filtration/secretion is needed (probenecid ↓ efficacy). **NSAIDs** reduce their effect (↓ prostaglandins/renal flow).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 10653441, 9643307.

**Thiazides (HCTZ, chlorthalidone, indapamide): cornerstone for hypertension.** They block the distal-tubule **Na⁺/Cl⁻ (NCC)** cotransporter ("low-ceiling" diuretics, ineffective when GFR is very low).
• **Typical electrolyte–metabolic picture**: **hypokalemia**, **hyponatremia** (the most feared adverse effect, potentially severe/neurological), **hyperuricemia/gout**, **hyperglycemia**, dyslipidemia; chlorthalidone and indapamide are **more potent** than HCTZ for a similar metabolic risk (PMID 28631393; PMID 26049382; PMID 10653441).
• **Calcium peculiarity (≠ loop)**: thiazides **reduce urinary Ca²⁺ excretion** → prevent **calcium stones** and protect bone (↓ bone-mass loss and hip fractures after menopause) → useful in the osteoporotic hypertensive woman (PMID 8983370).
• **K⁺-sparing agents – two mechanisms**: **amiloride/triamterene** block the epithelial Na⁺ channel (ENaC); **spironolactone/eplerenone** are **mineralocorticoid (aldosterone) receptor antagonists**, cardioprotective in heart failure. Common ADR: **hyperkalemia**.
• **Spironolactone vs eplerenone (exam)**: spironolactone, poorly selective, also binds androgen and progesterone receptors → **gynecomastia, breast pain, menstrual disturbances/impotence**; **eplerenone is selective** for the mineralocorticoid receptor → far fewer sexual effects, though hyperkalemia risk remains (PMID 26404746; PMID 15590870).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 26404746, 15590870, 8983370, 28631393, 26049382, 10653441.

**Logic of antithrombotic targets.** The coagulation cascade amplifies thrombin generation exponentially; drugs hit the highest-gain nodes.
• **Factor Xa** = convergence point of intrinsic and extrinsic pathways → one Xa yields many thrombin molecules (target of heparin/LMWH via antithrombin and of the -xaban DOACs).
• **Thrombin (IIa)** = terminal enzyme: converts fibrinogen to fibrin and activates platelets and factors V, VIII, XI, XIII (direct target of dabigatran).
• **Antithrombin** = physiologic inhibitor boosted by heparin; **vitamin K** = cofactor for γ-carboxylation of factors II, VII, IX, X (target of VKAs).
• **Clinical rule**: platelet-rich **arterial** thrombus → antiplatelets; fibrin-rich **venous**/stasis thrombus → anticoagulants.
• **Each target has its antidote**: **protamine** neutralizes heparin (~1 mg per 100 U remaining); **vitamin K** reverses VKAs; **idarucizumab** binds dabigatran; **andexanet alfa** traps factor Xa inhibitors.
• Protamine also interacts with platelets and fibrinogen and in excess has its own **anticoagulant effect** → give the smallest effective dose.

**Why one low daily dose suffices.** Aspirin **irreversibly** acetylates Ser529 of platelet COX-1; the anucleate platelet cannot resynthesize the enzyme, so inhibition lasts the platelet lifespan (~7-10 days) and recovers only with turnover (~10%/day).
• **Dose selectivity**: low doses (75-100 mg) inhibit platelet COX-1 (→ ↓TXA2) while relatively sparing endothelial **PGI2** (nucleated cells regenerate COX); high doses lose this selectivity.
• **NSAID interaction**: ibuprofen occupies the COX-1 channel and blocks aspirin's access to Ser529 → give aspirin **before** the NSAID or avoid the combination.
• Dose-dependent **GI toxicity** (even at low doses) → consider gastroprotection in at-risk patients.

**Clinical hierarchy of P2Y12 blockers (trial evidence).**
• **Clopidogrel**: prodrug bioactivated by **CYP2C19**; loss-of-function allele carriers form less active metabolite → weaker platelet inhibition and more ischemic events after PCI — rationale for CYP2C19 genotyping (PubMed PMID 33953382).
