• **Prasugrel** (TRITON-TIMI 38): vs clopidogrel reduces ischemic events but increases major bleeding → contraindicated with prior stroke/TIA, caution if >75 y or <60 kg (PubMed PMID 17982182).
• **Ticagrelor** (PLATO): reversible, non-prodrug, lowers CV death/MI vs clopidogrel; signature ADR **dyspnea**; **twice-daily** dosing due to short half-life (PubMed PMID 19717846).
• **Cangrelor** IV: reversible inhibition with onset/offset within minutes, useful in the cath lab.

**GP IIb/IIIa inhibitors = blockade of the final common pathway.** Activated GP IIb/IIIa (integrin αIIbβ3) binds **fibrinogen**, bridging platelets; blocking it abolishes aggregation regardless of the upstream stimulus (TXA2, ADP, thrombin). These are **intravenous**, hospital-only agents (high-risk PCI/ACS), now niche due to bleeding risk.
• **Abciximab**: monoclonal-antibody Fab fragment, high-affinity nearly irreversible binding; large molecule → immunogenicity and thrombocytopenia (~1%); effect attenuated by **platelet transfusion**.
• **Eptifibatide/tirofiban**: small reversible synthetic molecules with short half-life; thrombocytopenia less frequent, but platelet transfusion does not correct the effect (free drug in circulation).
• **Others**: dipyridamole (PDE/adenosine-uptake inhibitor, vasodilator), cilostazol (PDE3 inhibitor, claudication); oral thrombin PAR-1 antagonists (vorapaxar).

**Heparin vs LMWH: the difference comes from chain length.** Heparin binds antithrombin; inactivating **thrombin (IIa)** needs a long chain that bridges enzyme and antithrombin, whereas inhibiting **factor Xa** requires only the pentasaccharide. LMWHs (short chains) therefore inhibit mainly **Xa**, with a high anti-Xa/anti-IIa ratio.
• **Monitoring**: unfractionated heparin → **aPTT** (variable PK, IV dosing); LMWH → predictable response, **no routine monitoring** (anti-Xa optional in pregnancy/obesity/renal failure).
• **HIT (type II)**: IgG antibodies against the **PF4-heparin** complex activate platelets via **FcγRIIA** → a paradoxical **prothrombotic** state with thrombosis, not bleeding (PubMed PMID 28416511). More frequent with unfractionated heparin than LMWH.
• **Antidote**: **protamine** fully neutralizes heparin, only partially LMWH; **fondaparinux** is not neutralized by protamine.

**VKA vs DOAC: what matters for the exam.**
• **Warfarin** inhibits **VKOR** → lowers factors II, VII, IX, X **and** proteins C/S. Because protein C has a short half-life, a transient **procoagulant** state arises early (risk of **skin necrosis** in protein C deficiency) → bridge with heparin. Dose guided by **INR**; wide variability from **VKORC1/CYP2C9** genotype and food/drug interactions (PubMed PMID 33953382). **Teratogenic** → contraindicated in pregnancy. Antidote: **vitamin K** (slow) or **prothrombin complex concentrate** (rapid).
• **DOACs**: predictable effect, no routine monitoring, few interactions. **Dabigatran** (anti-IIa, ~80% renal elimination) → antidote **idarucizumab** (PubMed PMID 26095746); **rivaroxaban/apixaban/edoxaban** (anti-Xa) → antidote **andexanet alfa**, which binds and sequesters the inhibitor (PubMed PMID 39379749).

**Fibrinolytics: dissolving the already-formed clot.** They convert **plasminogen** to **plasmin**, which degrades fibrin. The key distinction is **fibrin specificity**: **alteplase/reteplase/tenecteplase** (recombinant t-PA) activate plasminogen preferentially **on the clot**; **streptokinase** is non-specific (systemic fibrinolysis) and antigenic.
• **Time window (ischemic stroke)**: IV alteplase within **4.5 hours** of onset improves functional outcome → "time is brain" (ESO guidelines, PubMed PMID 33817340).
• **Core risk = hemorrhage**, especially **intracranial** → absolute contraindications: active bleeding, hemorrhagic/recent stroke, recent major surgery or trauma, severe uncontrolled hypertension.
• **"Ideal" antithrombotic** (concept): 100% effective, no bleeding, oral, fixed dose without monitoring, low cost — DOACs approach but do not fully achieve it.
• **Fondaparinux and renal function (European guidelines)**: contraindicated in severe CKD (**GFR <20 mL/min**), but the **drug of choice in moderate impairment** (GFR **30-60 mL/min**).

**Exam point: all "direct" vasodilators relax smooth muscle, but the molecular target shapes the clinical signature.** The lecture's thread: Ca-channel blockers, endothelin antagonists, NO donors, K⁺-ATP channel openers and hydralazine all converge on ↓ vascular tone but with different ADRs and indications.
• **Endothelin axis (the body's most potent vasoconstrictor)**: preproET-1 → big-ET → **ET-1** (cleaved by the converting enzyme) → **ETa** receptors (smooth muscle → vasoconstriction and proliferation) and **ETb** (endothelium → ET-1 clearance and NO/prostacyclin release) (PMID 18533738).
• **Bosentan**: **non-selective ETa/ETb** antagonist, oral first-line in **pulmonary arterial hypertension**; class signature = **dose-dependent hepatotoxicity** (↑ transaminases) → monthly transaminase monitoring, and **teratogenicity** (PMID 18533738).
• **Selective ETa** agents (ambrisentan; sitaxentan, later withdrawn): fewer interactions and a smaller hepatic signal (PMID 18533738).
• ⚠️ All ERAs cause fluid retention/edema and are contraindicated in pregnancy.
• **ET-1 synthesis (exam)**: **endothelin-converting enzyme (ECE-1)** cleaves big-ET-1 to **ET-1** (rate-limiting step); **NO and prostacyclin (PGI₂) reduce ET-1 gene expression** (negative feedback).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 18533738.

**Exam point: nitrates are NO donors → ↑ cGMP → myosin dephosphorylation → predominantly VENOUS vasodilation → ↓ preload.** The fall in venous return lowers wall tension and myocardial O₂ demand; in the coronaries it redistributes flow toward the ischemic subendocardium.
• **Tolerance (tachyphylaxis)**: with continuous dosing the effect is lost; the central mechanism = inhibition of **mitochondrial aldehyde dehydrogenase (ALDH-2)**, which bioactivates nitroglycerin, plus oxidative stress → a daily **nitrate-free interval** of 8-12 h is mandatory (PMID 21081219).
• ⚠️ **ABSOLUTE CONTRAINDICATION with PDE5 inhibitors** (sildenafil, tadalafil, vardenafil): PDE5 degrades cGMP, so the combination lets it accumulate → **potentially fatal hypotension** (PMID 25392015). The safe interval depends on half-life: ≈24 h for sildenafil/vardenafil, ≈48 h for tadalafil (PMID 16387566).
• **ADRs**: throbbing headache (meningeal vasodilation), hypotension, **reflex tachycardia**, flushing.
• **Sodium nitroprusside**: an IV **arterial + venous** dilator for hypertensive emergencies; it releases **cyanide** → risk of cyanide/thiocyanate toxicity (metabolic acidosis), especially at high doses or in renal failure; **photosensitive → protect from light** (PMID 19645589).
• **Rapid PK (exam)**: sublingual nitroglycerin = plasma peak in ~**4 min**, t½ **1-3 min** (acute use); **isosorbide-5-mononitrate** = no hepatic first-pass → high bioavailability but onset too slow for an acute attack (chronic use).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 21081219, 25392015, 16387566, 19645589.

**Exam point: K⁺-ATP channel openers hyperpolarize the cell → closure of voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channels → ARTERIOLAR vasodilation → marked reflex tachycardia and fluid retention (combine with a β-blocker + diuretic).**
• **Minoxidil**: a potent arteriolar vasodilator for resistant hypertension; signature = **hypertrichosis** (the same K⁺-ATP mechanism used topically for androgenetic alopecia) (PMID 1545141).
• **Diazoxide**: same vascular mechanism, but on the **β-cell K⁺-ATP channel** (Kir6.2/SUR1) it **inhibits insulin release** → **hyperglycemia** → used in **hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia** (insulinoma, nesidioblastosis) (PMID 15180566).
• **Hydralazine**: a **direct arteriolar** vasodilator (lowers peripheral resistance); causes **reflex tachycardia** and Na⁺/water retention.
• ⚠️ Hydralazine signature = **lupus-like syndrome** (arthralgia, rash, ANA/anti-histone) → more frequent in **slow acetylators** of N-acetyltransferase, in whom the drug persists longer; reversible on withdrawal (PMID 2524348).
• Key hydralazine indication: combined with a **nitrate** (isosorbide dinitrate) in heart failure, especially useful in African-American patients.
• **Hydralazine in pregnancy (exam)**: direct arteriolar vasodilator with **rapid action**, useful in **hypertensive emergencies in pregnancy / pre-eclampsia**.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 1545141, 15180566, 2524348.

**All 4 antiarrhythmic strategies aim to suppress abnormal automaticity and re-entry circuits.** To grasp the drugs, anchor the action potential: in **pacemaker** cells (SA/AV node) phase 0 and diastolic phase 4 depend on **Ca²⁺** (slow depolarization, automaticity), whereas in **fast** cells (atria, His-Purkinje, ventricle) phase 0 depends on **Na⁺**, the plateau on Ca²⁺ and repolarization on **K⁺**.
• **Effective refractory period (ERP)**: window in which a new stimulus cannot depolarize; lengthening it (class III, β-blockers on the node) breaks **re-entry**.
• **Arrhythmogenic mechanisms**: enhanced/ectopic automaticity, re-entry (the commonest cause of tachyarrhythmias), early afterdepolarizations (EAD, from long QT → torsades) or delayed ones (DAD, from Ca²⁺/digitalis overload).
• The **Vaughan-Williams** classification (I Na⁺ block, II β-blockers, III K⁺ block, IV Ca²⁺ antagonists) remains the reference clinical/teaching scheme.
• **Key exam point**: every antiarrhythmic is also potentially **proarrhythmic** (it can generate the very arrhythmia it should treat).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 1901320.

**Golden rule of the subclasses: all block Na⁺, but they differ in kinetics and in what they do to action-potential duration (and thus the QT).** Ia (intermediate) also blocks K⁺ → **lengthens AP/QT**; Ib (fast kinetics) **shortens AP/QT** and is active on ischemic/ventricular tissue; Ic (slow kinetics, potent Na⁺ block) **does not change the QT** but markedly slows conduction (widens the QRS).
• **CAST (critical exam point)**: in post-MI patients flecainide/encainide suppressed ectopic beats but **increased mortality** (total mortality about 7.7% vs 3.0% with placebo, RR ~2.5) → **Ic drugs are contraindicated in ischemic/structural heart disease**.
• **Ia**: quinidine (vagolytic → premedicate to avoid ↑ ventricular rate; ↑ serum digoxin), procainamide (risk of **drug-induced lupus**), disopyramide (strong **anticholinergic** and negative-inotropic effects).
• **Ib lidocaine**: drug of choice for acute **ventricular** arrhythmias, IV (high hepatic first-pass), short t½; CNS toxicity (paresthesias, seizures).
• **Ic flecainide/propafenone**: useful in AF on a **structurally normal heart** ("pill in the pocket"); propafenone also has weak β-blocking activity.
• **Procainamide (exam)**: drug-induced lupus is more frequent in **slow acetylators** (rash + arthralgia, reversible on withdrawal); the metabolite **N-acetylprocainamide (NAPA)** loses Na⁺ blockade and acts as a **class III** agent (can cause torsades).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 2473403, 1900101, 1901320.

**Central concept: β-blockers act mainly on nodal tissue, where phase 4 and conduction are Ca²⁺/cAMP-dependent and therefore under sympathetic control.** Blocking β1 removes the catecholaminergic drive → ↓ SA-node automaticity (↓ rate), slows AV conduction and lengthens its ERP → controls the ventricular response in AF/flutter and interrupts nodal re-entry.
• **Core indications**: rate control in AF/flutter, AV-nodal re-entrant tachycardias, arrhythmias from adrenergic overactivity (thyrotoxicosis, pheochromocytoma, exertion, perioperative), prevention of arrhythmic death **post-MI** and in heart failure.
• Unlike class I drugs, they **reduce mortality** in structural heart disease → often first line, excellent safety profile.
• **ADR/cautions**: bradycardia, AV block (avoid combining with verapamil/diltiazem → risk of complete block), bronchospasm (asthma), masking of hypoglycemia, fatigue; do not stop abruptly (rebound).
• **Sotalol** is formally class II **and** III (see class III card). Esmolol: ultrashort t½, useful IV in acute/perioperative settings.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 26513538.

**Core idea: by blocking K⁺ they prolong repolarization → ↑ AP duration and ERP (anti-re-entry), but for the same reason they prolong the QT and can cause torsades de pointes.** Characteristic is **reverse use-dependence** (greater effect at low rates), which reduces efficacy during tachycardia and worsens arrhythmic risk during bradycardia.
• **Sotalol**: non-selective β-blocker (II) + K⁺ block (III); effective but with dose-dependent **torsades de pointes** → monitor QT/electrolytes; over 20% of patients must discontinue it for adverse effects.
• **Dofetilide/ibutilide**: **atrial** arrhythmias only (cardioversion of AF/flutter); start in a monitored setting because of torsades risk.
• **Amiodarone (exam cornerstone)**: class III but with properties of **all four** classes; an **iodinated** benzofuran, highly **lipophilic**, with tissue accumulation and a **very long t½** (weeks). It prolongs the QT yet, paradoxically, causes **torsades rarely**; safe in ventricular dysfunction; it is the most effective drug for maintaining sinus rhythm in AF.
• **Extracardiac toxicity of amiodarone (dose/time-dependent)**: **pulmonary fibrosis/pneumonitis**, **thyroid dysfunction** (hypo- or hyperthyroidism, from its iodine), **corneal microdeposits**, photosensitivity/**slate-gray** skin discoloration, hepato- and neurotoxicity; many **interactions** (↑ warfarin, ↑ digoxin).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 31756386, 39087074, 26513538.

**Unifying thread: these agents target the AV node, the electrophysiologic target of re-entrant supraventricular tachycardias.** The **non-dihydropyridine** Ca²⁺ antagonists (verapamil, diltiazem) block **L-type** Ca²⁺ channels in nodal tissue → ↓ SA-node automaticity, slow AV conduction and lengthen its ERP (↑ PR) → rate control in AF/flutter and termination of nodal re-entry.
• **Cautions**: negative inotropes → avoid in systolic heart failure and **do not combine with β-blockers** (AV block/asystole); verapamil ↑ serum digoxin. Do **not** use in undifferentiated wide-complex tachycardia or in WPW with AF (risk of accelerating conduction down the accessory pathway).
• **Adenosine (first choice in narrow-complex PSVT)**: activates purinergic **A1** receptors (hyperpolarizing K⁺ current + cAMP antagonism) → **transient AV block** that breaks nodal re-entry. **Half-life of a few seconds** → rapid IV bolus followed by a flush; it terminates most re-entrant PSVTs that involve the AV node.
• **Adenosine ADR**: very brief and self-limiting — flushing, dyspnea/chest tightness, headache, short bradycardia/block; antagonized by caffeine/theophylline, potentiated by dipyridamole.
• **Potassium**: **hypokalemia** promotes ectopy/EADs and torsades; **hyperkalemia** slows conduction up to arrest (emergency management, dialysis in renal failure).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 27484659, 2184971.

**"Signature" organ toxicities of chemotherapy** (completing the high-turnover tissue-damage concept):
• **Cyclophosphamide** (alkylator): its metabolite **acrolein** causes **hemorrhagic cystitis** → prevented with hydration and **mesna** (binds acrolein in the urinary tract); late risk of bladder cancer (G&G14).
• **Methotrexate** (antifolate): **mucositis, myelosuppression, nephrotoxicity** at high doses → "rescue" with **folinic acid** (leucovorin), which bypasses dihydrofolate-reductase blockade (G&G14).
• **Anthracyclines** (doxorubicin): **cumulative dose-dependent cardiotoxicity** (cardiomyopathy/heart failure) → cardioprotection with **dexrazoxone** (iron chelator) (G&G14).
• **Cisplatin**: **nephro-, oto- and neurotoxicity** plus severe emesis (hydration + antiemetics); **bleomycin**: **pulmonary fibrosis**; **vinca alkaloids/taxanes**: **peripheral neuropathy** (G&G14).
• ⚠️ Early risk of **tumor lysis syndrome** (hyperuricemia, hyperK⁺, hyperphosphatemia, hypoCa²⁺) in high-burden/high-turnover tumors → hydration + **allopurinol/rasburicase** (G&G14).

**Completion on imatinib and the oncogenic-driver concept:**
• Imatinib is an **ATP-competitive inhibitor** of the **BCR-ABL** kinase domain (also KIT and PDGFR): in chronic-phase CML the IRIS trial showed a **10-year overall survival of 83.3%**, turning the disease into a chronic one; per PubMed (NEJM, Hochhaus) [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1609324).
• **Predictive-biomarker logic**: anti-EGFR (e.g. EGFR-TKIs in EGFR-mutant lung cancer), **ALK** inhibitors in translocations, anti-**HER2** in amplifications → the drug works only when the molecular target is present (G&G14).
• **Tumor-agnostic (histology-independent) therapy**: in **NTRK** fusions, **larotrectinib/entrectinib** give rapid, durable responses across tumors of different organs, with a favorable tolerability profile; per PubMed (Pollack, Curr Med Res Opin) [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1080/03007995.2020.1847057).
• ⚠️ Common TKI limitation: **acquired resistance** (target mutations, e.g. T315I for BCR-ABL; bypass pathways) → next-generation drugs are needed (G&G14).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 28273028.

**Mechanistic and clinical completion:**
• PARP-1 repairs **single-strand breaks** (base-excision repair); when inhibited, lesions become **double-strand breaks** at replication, normally repaired by **homologous recombination (HR)**. In **BRCA-mutated/HRD** cells HR is absent → irreparable damage → **synthetic lethality**; per PubMed (olaparib review, Frampton) [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1007/s40259-015-0125-6).
• Added contribution from **PARP trapping** (the inhibited enzyme stays "trapped" on DNA, a lesion more cytotoxic than catalytic loss alone) (G&G14).
• **Prototypes**: olaparib, niraparib, rucaparib, talazoparib; used as **maintenance** in **platinum-sensitive** ovarian cancer responding after chemo (Study 19: significant PFS gain, greatest in BRCA-mutated); per PubMed [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1007/s40259-015-0125-6).
• **Class ADRs**: **myelotoxicity** (anemia, neutropenia, thrombocytopenia → serial CBCs), fatigue, nausea; rare late risk of **MDS/acute myeloid leukemia** (G&G14).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 25899311.

**Completion on targets and immune-related adverse events (irAEs):**
• Two checkpoint axes: **CTLA-4** (lymphocyte priming in the node → **ipilimumab**) and **PD-1/PD-L1** (effector phase in the tumor → **nivolumab, pembrolizumab** anti-PD1; **atezolizumab** anti-PD-L1) (G&G14).
• **irAEs** = autoimmune toxicity from releasing the immune "brake"; **any organ**, often delayed onset and prolonged duration: **colitis/diarrhea, thyroiditis, hepatitis, pneumonitis, hypophysitis/adrenal insufficiency, dermatitis**; per PubMed (Nat Rev Dis Primers, Ramos-Casals) [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41572-020-0160-6).
• **Management**: graded by severity → drug hold and systemic **corticosteroids**, with other immunosuppressants in refractory cases (e.g. infliximab for colitis, but avoided in hepatitis); irAEs are more frequent/earlier with the **anti-CTLA-4 + anti-PD-1** combination; per PubMed [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41572-020-0160-6).
• **CAR-T** (engineered T cells, hematologic malignancies): characteristic toxicities are **CRS** (cytokine release syndrome → tocilizumab) and **neurotoxicity (ICANS)** (G&G14).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 32382051.

**Fosfomycin — the single-dose cystitis drug.**
• **Key use**: acute uncomplicated cystitis in women → **single 3 g oral dose** (tromethamine salt); high, days-long urinary concentration.
• **Spectrum/resistance**: broad Gram+/Gram−; low resistance, active against many **ESBL E. coli** and Enterococcus → salvage option.
• **PK**: highly hydrophilic, no protein binding, unchanged renal excretion; food slows absorption (take on empty stomach, at night after voiding).
• **Comparison**: in an RCT the single dose was slightly **less effective** than 5-day nitrofurantoin, yet remains valid and well tolerated.
• **Safety**: well tolerated (mild diarrhea); usable in pregnancy for asymptomatic bacteriuria.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 29710295.

**Cycloserine — second-line antitubercular, neurotoxic.**
• **Mechanism**: **D-alanine** analogue → inhibits **alanine racemase** and **D-Ala–D-Ala ligase**, blocking peptidoglycan synthesis (upstream of the glycopeptide target).
• **Signature toxicity = CNS**: high penetration (including CSF) causes headache, insomnia, irritability → dose-dependent **psychosis, depression with suicidal ideation, seizures**.
• **Prophylaxis**: co-administer **pyridoxine (vit. B6)** to reduce neurological effects; contraindicated in epilepsy, severe depression, alcohol abuse.
• **Indication**: only multidrug-resistant TB (MDR-TB), always in combination; monitor plasma levels.
• Crosses the placenta → avoid in pregnancy.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 29866015, 15182146.

**Bacitracin — topical use only.**
• **Mechanism**: blocks dephosphorylation of **bactoprenol** (undecaprenyl pyrophosphate), the lipid carrier that recycles to ferry peptidoglycan subunits across the membrane → cell-wall synthesis halts.
• **Why topical only**: marked **nephrotoxicity** systemically → limited to skin, eye, mucosae.
• **Spectrum**: mainly Gram+; inactive on Gram− (cannot cross the outer membrane).
• **Combinations**: often in triple ointments with **neomycin + polymyxin B** (e.g. Neosporin) to also cover Gram−.
• Topical ADRs: allergic contact dermatitis, rare anaphylactic reactions.
• **Precise spectrum** (G&G14): highly active on **Gram+ cocci/bacilli, Neisseria, H. influenzae and T. pallidum** (≤0.1 U/mL); **resistant**: Enterobacteriaceae, Pseudomonas, Candida and Nocardia. **Nephrotoxicity on parenteral use** is the reason it is topical-only; hypersensitivity reactions are rare.

**Glycopeptides (vancomycin, teicoplanin) — bactericidal anti-Gram+.**
• **Mechanism**: bind the **D-Ala–D-Ala** terminus of the peptidoglycan precursor, sterically blocking transglycosylases and transpeptidases → halt cell-wall elongation (different site from β-lactams, hence no cross-resistance).
• **Spectrum**: Gram+ only (molecule too large for the Gram− outer membrane): MRSA, enterococci, C. difficile (oral).
• **VanA resistance**: the cluster replaces the target with **D-Ala–D-lactate** → affinity collapses; inducible and cross-resistant vancomycin/teicoplanin (VRE).
• ⚠️ **Class note**: **daptomycin is NOT a glycopeptide** but a **cyclic lipopeptide** (Ca²⁺-dependent membrane depolarization); inactivated by pulmonary surfactant → do not use in pneumonia.
• Time-dependent bactericidals, renally excreted.

**Vancomycin — clinical pillars and toxicity.**
• **Red-man syndrome**: erythema/pruritus of face-neck-trunk from **too-rapid infusion** → direct **histamine** release (NOT IgE-mediated allergy) → slow the infusion (≥60 min) and give an antihistamine; in a controlled trial the reaction was **infusion-rate dependent**.
• **Nephrotoxicity** (especially with aminoglycosides/diuretics) and dose-dependent **ototoxicity**.
• **Monitoring (TDM)**: recent guidelines recommend an **AUC/MIC 400–600** target rather than trough alone, because trough monitoring is associated with **greater nephrotoxicity**.
• **Routes**: **oral** (not absorbed) → only intestinal C. difficile; **IV** for systemic MRSA infections.
• Resistance: progressive MIC creep, VanA phenotype in enterococci.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 2449506, 32227354.

**Antitubercular therapy — why long and combined.**
• Mycobacteria grow slowly, are **intracellular**, and have a waxy wall (mycolic acids) → therapy lasts **months** and uses **multiple drugs** to prevent resistant mutants.
• **Standard "HRZE" regimen** (drug-susceptible TB): initial **2 months** of **isoniazid + rifampin + pyrazinamide + ethambutol**, then **4 months** continuation with **isoniazid + rifampin** (6 months total).
• **Roles**: rifampin and isoniazid = bactericidal backbone; pyrazinamide = **sterilizes** semi-dormant bacilli in acidic milieu (allows shortening to 6 months); ethambutol = bacteriostatic, covers initial resistance.
• **Adherence** is critical → DOT (directly observed therapy) to prevent MDR/XDR-TB.
• TB remains a leading infectious cause of death and of mortality in HIV/AIDS.

**Isoniazid — cornerstone of TB therapy.**
• **Mechanism**: prodrug activated by mycobacterial catalase-peroxidase **KatG**; inhibits **InhA** → blocks **mycolic acid** synthesis in the wall. Bactericidal on replicating bacilli.
• **Peripheral neuropathy**: INH competes with **pyridoxine (vit. B6)** → functional deficiency → co-administer B6, especially in slow acetylators, diabetics, pregnant women, alcoholics, malnourished.
• **Acetylation (NAT2)**: the slow/fast acetylator polymorphism modulates exposure; **slow** acetylators have more toxicity (neuropathy, hepatitis).
• **Hepatotoxicity**: age-dependent risk, potentiated by rifampin and alcohol → monitor transaminases.
• Inhibits some CYPs (e.g. CYP2C19) → interactions (phenytoin).
• **Acetylation (NAT2) — quantitative**: **slow acetylators** have isoniazid concentrations ~**3 times** higher than fast acetylators → greater toxicity (neuropathy, hepatitis); **fast acetylators** instead risk reduced efficacy/relapse.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 6269259.

**Ethambutol — the drug that "watches the eyes".**
• **Mechanism**: inhibits **arabinosyl-transferase (embB)** → blocks **arabinogalactan** synthesis, preventing mycolic-acid anchoring to the wall. Bacteriostatic.
• **Signature toxicity = optic neuritis** (often **retrobulbar**): reduced visual acuity, **red-green color blindness**, central scotoma → **dose- and duration-dependent**, usually reversible if stopped early (reversibility not guaranteed).
• **Monitoring**: visual acuity and color vision at baseline and periodically; instruct the patient to report visual changes immediately.
• Reduce dose in renal impairment (renal excretion).
• Use: initial phase of the HRZE regimen, to cover primary resistance.
• **Hyperuricemia**: like pyrazinamide, ethambutol **reduces renal urate excretion** → ↑ uricemia, with possible arthralgia/gout.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 16495590.

**Rifampin — potent enzyme inducer.**
• **Mechanism**: inhibits bacterial **DNA-dependent RNA polymerase** (β subunit, rpoB gene) → blocks transcription initiation; bactericidal even on slow-growing and intracellular bacilli.
• ⚠️ **Inducer of CYP3A4, 2C9, 2C19 and P-glycoprotein**: induction peaks in ~1 week and wanes ~2 weeks after stopping → ↓ efficacy of **oral contraceptives, warfarin, cyclosporine/tacrolimus, antiretrovirals (PIs), methadone, statins**.
• **Harmless tell-tale effect**: turns urine, tears, sweat **red-orange** (warn the patient; stains contact lenses).
• ADRs: hepatotoxicity, flu-like syndrome, rare immune-mediated cytopenias (intermittent regimens).
• Also used in leprosy and prophylaxis of meningococcal/H. influenzae b contacts.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 12882588.

**Pyrazinamide — the "sterilizer" of the initial phase.**
• **Mechanism**: prodrug converted by **pyrazinamidase (pncA)** into **pyrazinoic acid**, active at **acidic pH** inside phagolysosomes → hits the **semi-dormant** bacilli the other drugs miss. This sterilizing activity is what allows **shortening therapy to 6 months**.
• **Hepatotoxicity** dose-dependent (most hepatotoxic of the regimen) → monitor transaminases.
• **Hyperuricemia**: pyrazinoic acid **inhibits renal urate excretion** → arthralgias, possible gout flares.
• Used only in the **first 2 months** (initial phase), never as monotherapy.
• Resistance linked to pncA mutations.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 30090474.

**Second-line antituberculars — more toxic, for MDR-TB.**
• **When**: resistance/intolerance to first-line drugs or relapse; always combined, under monitoring.
• **Fluoroquinolones (levo-/moxifloxacin)**: now a **backbone of oral MDR-TB regimens** (act on DNA gyrase; target distinct from mycolic acids); risk of QT prolongation and tendinopathy.
• **Injectable aminoglycosides (amikacin, kanamycin, streptomycin) and the polypeptide capreomycin**: bactericidal but **oto- and nephrotoxic**; **ototoxicity** can be **irreversible** and dramatically amplified by the mitochondrial **12S rRNA m.1555A>G** mutation (deafness even at non-toxic doses).
• **Cycloserine**: effective but with dose-dependent **psychosis/seizures** (see dedicated card).
• Caution when combining multiple neurotoxic agents (e.g. with isoniazid).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 18830133, 29866015.

**Severe cutaneous reactions (the exam point)**
• Antibacterial sulfonamides are the **antibiotic most often associated with SJS/TEN**: in a meta-analysis they account for ~**32%** of antibiotic-induced cases, ahead of penicillins and cephalosporins; SJS/TEN mortality up to ~50% (PMID 36790777).
• Mechanism: **delayed T-cell-mediated hypersensitivity** (not IgE); risk is higher in HIV/AIDS and linked to **HLA** alleles (PMID 29188475).
**Other toxicities to remember**
• **G6PD deficiency** → acute hemolytic anemia; in the **neonate** they displace bilirubin from albumin → risk of **kernicterus** → contraindicated in late pregnancy and in the newborn.
• **Crystalluria**: hydrate and alkalinize urine; newer, more soluble agents make it rare.
**Cotrimoxazole** (sulfamethoxazole + trimethoprim): besides myelosuppression, **hyperkalemia** (trimethoprim blocks the distal-nephron ENaC Na⁺ channels, an amiloride-like effect), especially at high doses / renal impairment / elderly (PMID 7633023).
• **Resistance mechanism (exam)**: mutation/overproduction of **dihydropteroate synthase** (low-affinity form), increased **PABA** synthesis, reduced penetration; often **plasmid-mediated** and **cross-resistant** across sulfonamides.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 36790777, 29188475, 7633023.

**Collagen toxicity (high-yield)**
• **Tendinopathy and Achilles tendon rupture**: meta-analysis → rupture risk **OR ~2.5** and tendinitis **OR ~4**; greater risk at **age ≥60** and with concomitant **corticosteroids** (PMID 31270563).
• **Aortic aneurysm and dissection**: an association that led to the **2018 FDA black-box warning**; caution in the elderly, hypertensives, Marfan/Ehlers-Danlos, known aneurysm (PMID 34583637).
**Other signature effects**
• **Dysglycemia**: hypo- and hyperglycemia from blockade of pancreatic **β-cell K⁺-ATP channels** (most with gatifloxacin, withdrawn; monitor diabetics) (PMID 17057046).
• **QT prolongation** → torsade de pointes (highest with moxifloxacin); **photosensitivity**; rare **peripheral neuropathy** (may be irreversible).
• **Chelation by cations** (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, Al³⁺, Fe²⁺): dairy/antacids/iron salts ↓ absorption → space dosing. **Contraindicated in pregnancy** and, with few exceptions, in **children**.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 31270563, 34583637, 17057046.

**Enzyme induction (the real exam reason)**
• Rifampicin is one of the **most potent enzyme inducers**: it activates the nuclear receptor **PXR**, which upregulates **CYP3A4** (plus other CYPs, UGTs, P-glycoprotein) (PMID 19460945; PMID 18473749).
• Result: ↓ efficacy of **oral contraceptives** (use a back-up method), warfarin, corticosteroids, cyclosporine/tacrolimus, antiretrovirals, methadone, digoxin, verapamil, azoles.
**Other key points**
• Turns **urine, sweat, tears, saliva red-orange** (harmless, but can stain soft contact lenses) → useful as an adherence marker.
• **Hepatotoxicity** (↑ transaminases/cholestasis), especially with isoniazid or pre-existing liver disease; immune-mediated reactions (flu-like syndrome, thrombocytopenia, AKI) can occur with intermittent dosing.
• As monotherapy → **rapid resistance** (rpoB mutation) → always used in **combination** (e.g. TB with isoniazid).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 19460945, 18473749.

**Cross-class theme: QT and CYP3A4**
• Macrolides prolong the **QT** interval (blockade of K⁺/hERG currents) → risk of **torsade de pointes**, additive with other QT-prolonging drugs and with hypokalemia/hypomagnesemia.
• Gradient of **CYP3A4** inhibition: **erythromycin and clarithromycin = strong inhibitors**, **azithromycin essentially none** (useful when few interaction risks are needed) (PMID 18473749).
**Other useful concepts**
• **Bacteriostatic**, time-dependent effect; good tissue and intracellular penetration → useful against **atypicals** (Mycoplasma, Chlamydia, Legionella).
• Resistance by **methylation of the 23S target (erm genes)** → cross **MLS_B** resistance with lincosamides and streptogramins; also efflux pumps (mef).
• **Prokinetic** effect (motilin-receptor agonism), greatest with erythromycin → sometimes exploited in gastroparesis.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 18473749.

**The key fact: sudden cardiac death**
• NEJM cohort study (Ray): oral erythromycin **doubles** the risk of sudden cardiac death, rising to **~5-fold** when combined with a **strong CYP3A4 inhibitor** (e.g. verapamil, diltiazem, azoles); no increase with amoxicillin (PMID 15356306).
• Substrate and **strong CYP3A4 inhibitor** → ↑ levels of statins (rhabdomyolysis), benzodiazepines, carbamazepine, cyclosporine, warfarin.
**Other points**
• Narrow, **Gram-positive** spectrum; alternative in penicillin-allergic patients (pharyngitis, diphtheria, pertussis, Campylobacter, Legionella).
• **Marked GI effects** (the worst macrolide) from **motilin** stimulation → cramps, nausea, diarrhea.
• **Reversible ototoxicity** at high doses / renal-hepatic impairment; the estolate can cause **cholestatic hepatitis**; in the **neonate** it is associated with **hypertrophic pyloric stenosis**.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 15356306.

**Important caveat about the QT**
• Azithromycin does NOT inhibit CYP3A4 (a real advantage), **but it can still prolong the QT**: observational studies show a **small increase in cardiovascular deaths**, mainly in already at-risk patients (cardiac disease, elderly, hypokalemia, other QT drugs) → do not regard it as entirely "cardiac-neutral" (PMID 24285766).
**Strengths to remember**
• **Very long half-life** (~68 h) with tissue/intracellular accumulation → short regimens (e.g. 500 mg ×3 days or a single 1 g dose for chlamydia) with a prolonged post-treatment effect.
• Macrolide of choice for **chlamydia/STIs** and for Haemophilus; used in **COPD/cystic fibrosis** exacerbations partly for its low-dose **immunomodulatory** effect.
• **Mainly biliary elimination** (little renal) → little affected by renal function.
• **Class-wide cross-resistance** (MLS_B): ineffective against an erythromycin-resistant strain.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 24285766.

**The exam fact: C. difficile colitis**
• Clindamycin is among the antibiotics with the **highest risk of Clostridioides difficile infection** (pseudomembranous colitis), even though it is itself inactive against the organism (PMID 32003951).
• For that same CDI, metronidazole is no longer first-line: **oral vancomycin or fidaxomicin** are used (PMID 32003951).
**Indications and useful niches**
• Excellent against **anaerobes** (Bacteroides) and Gram-positives (including many community MRSA); good **bone** penetration → osteomyelitis, odontogenic infections, lung abscess, diabetic foot.
• **Suppression of toxin/exotoxin synthesis** → added in **necrotizing fasciitis** and streptococcal/staphylococcal **toxic shock** (synergy with penicillin).
• **D-zone test**: detects inducible MLS_B resistance (erythromycin-R, apparently clindamycin-S).
• **Topical** formulations for acne; neuromuscular blockade → caution in **myasthenia gravis**.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 32003951.

**The two hematologic toxicities to distinguish**
• **Dose-dependent, reversible bone-marrow suppression** (anemia, ↓ reticulocytes) from inhibition of mitochondrial protein synthesis.
• **Idiosyncratic aplastic anemia**, rare (~1:20,000–40,000), **dose-independent, often irreversible and potentially fatal**: the reason the drug is now third-line.
**Other key points**
• **Gray baby syndrome** (newborn/premature): immature **glucuronyl transferase** + reduced renal excretion → accumulation → grayish cyanosis, hypotension, collapse → avoid in neonates or monitor levels.
• **CYP inhibitor** (CYP2C9/3A4) → ↑ warfarin, phenytoin, sulfonylureas (hypoglycemia).
• Excellent **CNS** penetration; broad spectrum (including rickettsiae, typhoid); reserved for severe infections when safer alternatives are lacking.

**Organ toxicity (high-yield)**
• **Ototoxicity** (cochlear and vestibular) and **nephrotoxicity** (proximal tubular, usually reversible) are the signature effects; narrow therapeutic index → **level monitoring**.
• A **mitochondrial predisposition** exists: the **m.1555A>G variant of MT-RNR1 (12S rRNA)** causes hearing loss even at normal doses → avoid aminoglycosides in carriers (CPIC guideline) (PMID 34032273).
**Key pharmacologic concepts**
• **Concentration-dependent bactericidals** with a long **post-antibiotic effect** → **once-daily dosing** maximizes peak/MIC and reduces accumulation (hence toxicity).
• **O₂-dependent uptake** → inactive against **anaerobes** and in acidic/hypoxic environments (abscesses).
• **Synergy with β-lactams** (which ease entry) → endocarditis, severe sepsis.
• ⚠️ Avoid with other ototoxic drugs and **loop diuretics**; they potentiate **neuromuscular blockade** (myasthenia, post-anesthesia).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 34032273.

**Overview of individual aminoglycosides** (Goodman & Gilman, 14th ed.)
• **Gentamicin**: the standard; serious Gram-negatives, synergistic with β-lactams in endocarditis (enterococcus, S. viridans).
• **Tobramycin**: more active against **Pseudomonas**; **inhaled formulation** in cystic fibrosis.
• **Amikacin**: the **most resistant to inactivating enzymes** → reserved for multidrug-resistant Gram-negatives and anti-mycobacterial regimens (TB, MAC).
• **Streptomycin** (the first): second-line TB, plague, tularemia, brucellosis (with doxycycline); notable **vestibular toxicity**.
• **Neomycin**: too toxic systemically → **topical** and **oral** use (gut decontamination, hepatic encephalopathy).
• **Netilmicin**: semisynthetic, sometimes less ototoxic.
• Rule of thumb: all share **oto- and nephrotoxicity** and **parenteral** administration for systemic infection (oral absorption negligible).

**Age contraindications (the exam point)**
• They chelate **Ca²⁺** in forming bone/teeth → **permanent tooth discoloration** and enamel hypoplasia → contraindicated in **pregnancy, breastfeeding and <8 years**.
• **Doxycycline** binds calcium less avidly and stains less: it is considered acceptable for short courses in children (e.g. rickettsial disease), though caution is still advised (PMID 30442509).
**Other useful points**
• **Oral chelation** with dairy, antacids, iron, Ca/Mg → reduced absorption: take away from meals (doxycycline is less affected).
• **Photosensitivity**; **doxycycline** → esophagitis if taken without water/lying down; **minocycline** → dizziness/vestibular disturbance, hyperpigmentation, lupus-like syndrome.
• Doxycycline is **eliminated via the bile/gut** → usable in **renal impairment** (unlike other tetracyclines).
• **Expired** tetracyclines → tubular toxicity (Fanconi syndrome).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 30442509.

**Class overview**
• Shared **β-lactam ring** is the pharmacophore; β-lactamases hydrolyze it.
• Ring acylates the catalytic serine of **PBPs (transpeptidases)** → blocks peptidoglycan cross-linking → bactericidal, **time-dependent** (T>MIC).
• Highest-risk class for **drug-induced anaphylaxis** (β-lactams ≈42.6% of severe cases, PMID 29017833).
• Resistance: β-lactamases, efflux/porin loss, PBP alteration (e.g. MRSA).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 29017833.

**Natural penicillins**
• Narrow Gram+ spectrum; **drug of choice for syphilis** and streptococcal infection.
• Pen G parenteral only (acid-labile); Pen V oral. Short t½, renal tubular secretion (slowed by **probenecid**).
• **Benzathine/procaine** depots give prolonged release (syphilis, rheumatic-fever prophylaxis).
• ADR: hypersensitivity up to **IgE anaphylaxis**, serum sickness, **Jarisch-Herxheimer** reaction.
• Cephalosporin cross-allergy is low and **side-chain driven**, not ring-driven (PMID 29017833).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 29017833.

**Antistaphylococcal penicillins**
• For **MSSA** (penicillinase producers); no Gram-/enterococcal activity.
• **Oxacillin, nafcillin, dicloxacillin** used; **methicillin dropped** (interstitial nephritis). Nafcillin/oxacillin are hepatobiliary-cleared.
• **MRSA**: **mecA** gene → altered **PBP2a** with very low affinity for all β-lactams → class fails (PMID 26034890).
• Modern exception: **ceftaroline** binds PBP2a and covers MRSA.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 26034890.

**Aminopenicillins**
• Amino group adds hydrophilicity → cross Gram- porins → extended spectrum (E. coli, Proteus, H. influenzae, Listeria, enterococci).
• **Amoxicillin** oral (better bioavailability); **ampicillin** is drug of choice for **Listeria/Enterococcus**.
• Amoxicillin: backbone of **H. pylori** triple therapy; **safe in pregnancy**.
• **Non-allergic rash** in EBV mononucleosis — not true IgE allergy.
• β-lactamase-sensitive → combined with **clavulanate/sulbactam**. Higher cross-allergy with **aminocephalosporins** (~16-20%) due to shared side chain (PMID 31170539).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 31170539.

**Ureidopenicillins**
• Anti-**Pseudomonas**, hospital use for severe/nosocomial infection.
• Only **piperacillin** remains, always as **piperacillin-tazobactam** (covers many anaerobes too).
• β-lactamase-sensitive alone; cannot overcome high-burden ESBL.
• ⚠️ In **ESBL bloodstream infections**, the **MERINO** trial showed pip-tazo **inferior to meropenem** → prefer a **carbapenem** (PMID 31407238).
• Class ADR: hypersensitivity, high **Na⁺ load**, platelet dysfunction.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 31407238.

**Cephalosporins overview**
• Same PBP-acylation mechanism on a **7-aminocephalosporanic** core; generally **more β-lactamase-stable**, spectrum widens by generation.
• **No cephalosporin covers enterococci/Listeria** ('enterococcal gap'); only ceftaroline covers MRSA.
• Penicillin cross-allergy is **low** and **R1 side-chain dependent**, not ring-dependent (PMID 31170539).
• ⚠️ **N-methylthiotetrazole (MTT)** side-chain agents (cefoperazone, cefamandole, cefotetan) cause **hypoprothrombinemia/bleeding** and **disulfiram-like reaction** with alcohol (PMID 2183609).
• **MTT side-chain — include cefazolin too**: the disulfiram-like reaction and hypoprothrombinemia are not a class effect but depend on the N-methylthiotetrazole side-chain; Goodman lists among the MTT agents **cefazolin, cefamandole, cefotetan, cefoperazone**.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 31170539, 2183609.

**2nd-generation cephalosporins**
• More **Gram-** activity (H. influenzae, some Enterobacterales) with retained Gram+ cover.
• **Cephamycins** (cefoxitin, cefotetan) add **anti-anaerobe** activity → abdominal/gynecologic surgery.
• No Pseudomonas, MRSA or enterococcal activity.
• ⚠️ **Cefotetan** has the **MTT** side chain → **bleeding/hypoprothrombinemia** and **disulfiram-like reaction** (PMID 2183609).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 2183609.

**3rd-generation cephalosporins**
• Broad **Gram-** activity, good CNS penetration → **empiric bacterial meningitis** (cefotaxime, ceftriaxone).
• Anti-**Pseudomonas** only **ceftazidime** (and cefoperazone).
• **Ceftriaxone**: once-daily, biliary-renal clearance.
• ⚠️ **Contraindicated in neonates**: displaces **bilirubin** (kernicterus) and **precipitates with calcium** → neonatal deaths; do not co-administer IV calcium (PMID 18759704). Causes reversible **biliary sludge** (PMID 2183609).
• **Cefoperazone** has **MTT** side chain → bleeding/disulfiram effect (PMID 2183609).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 18759704, 2183609.

**4th-generation (cefepime)**
• **Broadest** spectrum: anti-**Pseudomonas** plus solid **Gram+** cover.
• Zwitterion → fast porin entry and **stability to chromosomal AmpC** (class C).
• Good CNS penetration; severe/nosocomial infections, **febrile neutropenia** monotherapy.
• No MRSA/enterococcal cover; **ESBL-sensitive**.
• ⚠️ **Cefepime neurotoxicity** (encephalopathy, myoclonus) in **renal impairment** if undosed → adjust to clearance.

**New cephalosporins**
• **Ceftaroline**: binds **PBP2a** → **active against MRSA** (plus streptococci, MSSA); CAP and skin/soft-tissue infections. No Pseudomonas/ESBL cover.
• **Ceftolozane** (as **ceftolozane-tazobactam**): very **stable to Pseudomonas resistance** (efflux, porin loss, AmpC) → complicated intra-abdominal/urinary infections and MDR-Pseudomonas nosocomial pneumonia. No MRSA cover.
• They are **complementary**; neither overcomes **carbapenemases**.

**Carbapenems**
• **Broadest β-lactam spectrum**, stable to **ESBL/AmpC** → mainstay for severe ESBL infections (PMID 31407238).
• **Ertapenem** lacks Pseudomonas/Acinetobacter cover; once-daily.
• **Imipenem + cilastatin** (inhibits renal **dehydropeptidase-I**; kinetic adjuvant, not a β-lactamase inhibitor).
• ⚠️ **Lower seizure threshold** (greatest with imipenem, esp. renal impairment; meropenem safer) (PMID 25051950); carbapenems **drop valproate levels** → avoid co-use.
• Resistance: **carbapenemases** (class A KPC, class B metallo-β-lactamases).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 25051950, 31407238.

**Monobactam (aztreonam)**
• Monocyclic β-lactam: **aerobic Gram- only**, incl. **Pseudomonas**; no Gram+/anaerobe activity.
• **Key point**: **no cross-allergy** with other β-lactams → **safe in penicillin allergy** (IgE- and T-mediated) (PMID 33500632, PMID 27016799).
• ⚠️ Shares its side chain with **ceftazidime** → caution if ceftazidime-allergic.
• **Stable to class B metallo-β-lactamases** → role in MBL infections.
• **Inhaled** form in **cystic fibrosis** (Pseudomonas).

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 33500632, 27016799.

**Ambler classification**
• **Class A** (serine): penicillinases, **ESBLs** (CTX-M, PMID 31407238), **KPC** carbapenemases; inhibited by clavulanate/avibactam.
• **Class B** (**metallo-β-lactamases**, NDM/VIM/IMP): hydrolyze **all β-lactams except aztreonam**; not inhibited by clavulanate/avibactam.
• **Class C** (**AmpC**, serine): cephalosporinases, clavulanate-resistant.
• **Class D** (**OXA**, serine): oxacillinases, some carbapenemases.
Mnemonic: **A, C, D serine; B metallo**.

**Sources**: Goodman & Gilman 14th ed.; PubMed PMID 31407238.

**β-lactamase inhibitors, 1st generation**
• **Clavulanate, sulbactam, tazobactam**: β-lactam analogues, **no own antibacterial activity**, **irreversible suicide inhibitors** restoring partner activity.
• Mainly **class A** (penicillinases, many ESBLs); **ineffective** on **AmpC (C)** and **metallo-β-lactamases (B)**.
• Fixed combos: **amoxicillin-clavulanate** (oral), **ampicillin-sulbactam**, **piperacillin-tazobactam**.
• **Sulbactam** has intrinsic activity vs **Acinetobacter**.
• ⚠️ **Amoxicillin-clavulanate** is a leading cause of **cholestatic drug-induced liver injury**.

**β-lactamase inhibitors, 2nd generation & new**
• Non-β-lactam, **not hydrolyzed** (reversible, recyclable), broader inhibition.
• **Avibactam**: class A (incl. **ESBL, KPC**), **C (AmpC)**, part of **D (OXA-48)** → **ceftazidime-avibactam**; **not** class B metallo.
• **Vaborbactam** (boronate): class A/C incl. **KPC** → **meropenem-vaborbactam**.
• **Relebactam**: class A/C → **imipenem-relebactam**.
• None inhibits **class B metallo-β-lactamases**; for MBL use **ceftazidime-avibactam + aztreonam**.
